Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: I am a 52 year old woman and I have a terrible stomach ache. I had something like this 2 years ago! I also feel sick, sweaty and have a fever at 38.2 degrees. The pain is more in the left side of the stomach, more down - kind of a pressure pain. I have been suffering from hypertension and widespread athrosis for a long time. The only medications I take are Ramipril and HCT. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Diverticulitis
 
  Model Output:1. Diverticulitis (Most Likely)
 Common in people over 50, especially with a prior episode
 Lower left abdominal pain (sigmoid colon), often pressure-like
 Fever, nausea, and sweating
 Complications: Abscess, perforation, peritonitis
 Diagnosis: CT scan of the abdomen
 2. Nephrolithiasis (Kidney Stone)
 Pain can be severe, crampy, and radiate to the groin
 Hematuria (blood in urine) may be present
 Sweating and nausea are common
 Diagnosis: Urinalysis, kidney ultrasound or CT scan
 3. Left-Sided Pyelonephritis (Kidney Infection)
 Fever and flank or lower abdominal pain
 Possible nausea, vomiting, and burning urination
 Complication: Can lead to sepsis if untreated
 Diagnosis: Urinalysis, urine culture, and ultrasound
 4. Sigmoid Volvulus (Twisting of the Colon)
 Lower left-sided abdominal pain and bloating
 Severe constipation, possible nausea, vomiting
 More common in elderly or people with chronic constipation
 Diagnosis: Abdominal X-ray (shows "coffee bean" sign), CT scan
 5. Ischemic Colitis (Reduced Blood Flow to Colon)
 Left-sided pain with bloody diarrhea (possible)
 Common in people with hypertension or vascular disease
 Fever and nausea possible
 Diagnosis: CT scan, colonoscopy

Diagnosis Task: I am a 67-year-old woman, a farmer by profession. I have had muscle pain all over for 4 months. I am exhausted and can hardly sleep. At first it was more the neck. Then it moved to the hands. But most of all in the upper arm. But it also goes from my back to the back of my knees. I could hardly move my legs. For example, I have to lift my leg out when I get out of the car. When I get up from a sitting position, I first bend down until I can walk. That's how much it hurt my back. Also in the knees. It was especially bad in my arms at night. I couldn't stretch because there was always a pulling sensation at the bottom. I thought that it came from the stomach. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Polymyalgia rheumatica
 
  Model Output:1. Polymyalgia Rheumatica (PMR) – Most Likely
 Typical age > 50, more common in women
 Severe stiffness and pain in the shoulders, neck, upper arms, and hips
 Morning stiffness and difficulty getting up from sitting or a car
 Often associated with fatigue and weight loss
 Responds dramatically to low-dose corticosteroids
 Check ESR (erythrocyte sedimentation rate) and CRP (C-reactive protein)
 2. Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA)
 Morning stiffness and symmetrical joint pain
 Hand and knee involvement (can affect shoulders and hips too)
 Possible swelling in the joints
 Check rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-CCP antibodies
 3. Fibromyalgia
 Widespread muscle pain with tender points
 Fatigue and sleep disturbances
 No significant inflammation (normal ESR/CRP)
 Diagnosis is clinical (no specific test)
 4. Hypothyroidism (Myopathy)
 Muscle weakness and pain, especially in the shoulders and thighs
 Fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, and dry skin
 Check TSH and free T4 levels
 5. Vitamin D Deficiency / Osteomalacia
 Muscle pain and weakness, difficulty standing or walking
 Can cause a "bone pain" sensation, especially in the back and legs
 Common in people with little sun exposure
 Check vitamin D levels

Diagnosis Task: My one-year-old daughter had a persistent high fever up to 40 degrees. I have already been to the doctor's office and there it was classified as "uncharacteristic fever". Other than a reddened throat, I didn't notice anything else. We were prescribed an antibiotic two days ago (clarithromycin) because the fever was still persistent. She responded poorly to paracetamol. Now the fever is finally gone, but a rash has appeared on the abdomen. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Roseola/Sixth disease
 
  Model Output:1. Roseola Infantum (Exanthem Subitum, HHV-6) – Most Likely
 Classic course: High fever for 3–5 days → sudden fever resolution → appearance of a blanching pink maculopapular rash (starts on the trunk and spreads)
 Child usually appears well despite rash
 Cause: Human herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6) or HHV-7
 No specific treatment needed
 2. Scarlet Fever (Group A Streptococcus)
 Fever + sore throat + rash (fine, sandpaper-like, starts on the trunk, then spreads)
 Red tongue ("strawberry tongue")
 Common after strep throat or tonsillitis
 Treatment: Antibiotics (penicillin or clarithromycin if allergic)
 3. Drug-Induced Rash (Antibiotic Reaction)
 Non-itchy, red, widespread rash after starting clarithromycin
 Often seen with amoxicillin if the child has undiagnosed EBV (infectious mononucleosis)
 Usually harmless but should be monitored
 4. Viral Exanthem (Non-Specific Virus)
 Many viruses cause fever + rash (adenovirus, enterovirus, parvovirus B19)
 Rash may be mild, itchy, or blotchy and resolve in a few days
 Supportive care only
 5. Kawasaki Disease (Less Likely but Important to Consider)
 Fever for ≥5 days + rash + red throat
 Red eyes, swollen hands/feet, cracked lips, swollen lymph nodes
 Risk of coronary artery complications
 Needs urgent pediatric evaluation if suspected

Diagnosis Task: I am a 63 year old male and have had a cough for over 3 months. At first I thought it was a cold, but it can't last that long! I have been suffering from high blood pressure and coronary heart disease for a long time. I have also had 2 stents in this context. I take Ramipril, Atorvastatin and ASS as medication. Otherwise I am symptom-free. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: bronchial asthma 
 
  Model Output:ACE inhibitor-induced cough (Ramipril-related)
 Reason: Ramipril, an ACE inhibitor, is a common cause of chronic dry cough (occurs in up to 20% of users).
 Typical cough: Dry, persistent, and non-productive.
 Clue: Resolves within weeks after stopping the drug.
 Next step: Consider switching to an angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB) like Losartan.
 
 Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) with chronic cough
 Reason: GERD can cause silent reflux, irritating the airways.
 Typical cough: Often worse at night or after meals, may be associated with throat clearing or hoarseness.
 Next step: Try proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) like Pantoprazole, lifestyle modifications.
 
 Chronic bronchitis (COPD spectrum, possibly smoking-related)
 Reason: If you have a smoking history, chronic bronchitis is a possibility.
 Typical cough: Productive (mucus) and worsens in the morning.
 Next step: Pulmonary function tests (PFTs), consider bronchodilators.
 
 Heart failure with pulmonary congestion ("cardiac cough")
 Reason: Heart failure can cause pulmonary congestion, leading to a chronic cough.
 Typical cough: Worse at night, may be associated with shortness of breath or leg swelling.
 Next step: Echocardiography to assess heart function.
 
 Interstitial lung disease or pulmonary fibrosis
 Reason: Chronic cough in an older individual can be due to lung fibrosis, sometimes associated with medications or underlying conditions.
 Typical cough: Dry, persistent, and exertional.
 Next step: High-resolution CT (HRCT) scan of the lungs.

Diagnosis Task: My husband is 78 years old and since last night he can no longer lift his arm properly and he can no longer walk. My son and I take care of him (care level 2). 3 months ago he was in the hospital because of bleeding with prostate cancer. A certain therapy was discontinued at that time. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Stroke
 
  Model Output:1. Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident, CVA) – Most Urgent Concern
 Reason: Sudden weakness in one arm and inability to walk strongly suggest a stroke (ischemic or hemorrhagic).
 Additional Signs: Face drooping, slurred speech, confusion, or vision problems.
 Next Steps: Immediate emergency evaluation (call emergency services, CT or MRI brain scan).
 2. Brain Metastases (Secondary Brain Tumors from Prostate Cancer)
 Reason: Prostate cancer can metastasize to the brain, causing neurological symptoms like weakness or difficulty walking.
 Additional Signs: Progressive neurological decline, headaches, seizures.
 Next Steps: Brain MRI or CT scan to check for metastases.
 3. Spinal Cord Compression (Tumor or Fracture from Bone Metastases)
 Reason: Prostate cancer often spreads to bones, including the spine, potentially causing spinal cord compression.
 Typical Symptoms: Gradual or sudden weakness in the arms and legs, difficulty walking, loss of bowel/bladder control.
 Next Steps: Urgent MRI of the spine to check for compression.
 4. Subdural Hematoma (Delayed Bleeding, Especially After Recent Hospitalization)
 Reason: Given his recent bleeding episode, he may have developed a slow brain bleed (subdural hematoma), especially if he was on blood thinners.
 Typical Symptoms: Gradual worsening of confusion, weakness, and difficulty walking over days or weeks.
 Next Steps: CT brain scan to check for bleeding.
 5. Severe Electrolyte Imbalance or Metabolic Disorder (e.g., Hypercalcemia from Cancer)
 Reason: Prostate cancer can cause high calcium levels (hypercalcemia), leading to muscle weakness, confusion, and impaired mobility.
 Additional Signs: Excessive thirst, nausea, confusion.
 Next Steps: Blood tests (calcium, kidney function, electrolytes).

Diagnosis Task: I am a 68 year old man and I always have such a twinge in my chest. It is a feeling of pressure in the chest that has been there for 6 weeks. Especially when I exert myself, it stings in there under the breastbone. I am slightly overweight, but otherwise healthy. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Coronary artery disease
 
  Model Output:1. Stable Angina (Coronary Artery Disease - CAD) – Most Likely
 Chest pressure or tightness during exertion (e.g., walking, climbing stairs)
 Relieved by rest within minutes
 Caused by narrowed coronary arteries (atherosclerosis)
 Urgent evaluation needed (ECG, stress test, coronary angiography)
 2. Atypical Angina (Microvascular Angina / Variant Angina)
 Similar to stable angina but may occur at rest or unpredictably
 Spasms in smaller coronary arteries (Prinzmetal’s angina if vasospastic)
 Can be triggered by stress or cold exposure
 3. Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) / Esophageal Spasm
 Burning or pressure-like chest pain, often after meals
 Worse when lying down or bending over
 May mimic heart-related pain but not exertion-dependent
 4. Costochondritis / Musculoskeletal Chest Pain
 Localized pain at the sternum (rib joints), worsened by movement or pressure
 No direct relation to exertion
 Can last for weeks but usually improves with anti-inflammatory medications
 5. Aortic Stenosis (Heart Valve Disease)
 Exertional chest pain (angina), shortness of breath, dizziness
 More common in older adults
 Needs echocardiogram to assess heart valves

Diagnosis Task: Our neighbor is an avid motorcyclist and was stung by a wasp under his leather suit on his way home today. He has quaddle-shaped skin rashes all over his body. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Anaphylaxis/Anaphylactic shock
 
  Model Output:1. Anaphylaxis (Severe Allergic Reaction) – Most Concerning
 Generalized urticaria (hives, quaddle-shaped rash)
 Swelling, difficulty breathing, dizziness, low blood pressure (shock)
 Potentially life-threatening
 Needs immediate epinephrine (EpiPen) and emergency care!
 2. Systemic Urticaria (Allergic Reaction)
 Widespread hives (quaddles) without anaphylactic symptoms
 Mediated by histamine release from the sting
 Can occur without full anaphylaxis
 Treatment: Antihistamines (e.g., cetirizine, loratadine), corticosteroids if severe
 3. Toxic Reaction (Multiple Stings or High Venom Load)
 If multiple wasp stings, venom may trigger a toxic systemic reaction
 Fever, nausea, muscle pain, headache, and widespread rash
 Can mimic anaphylaxis but is not immune-mediated
 Supportive treatment: IV fluids, antihistamines, pain relief
 4. Serum Sickness-Like Reaction (Delayed Hypersensitivity)
 Can occur days after the sting
 Hives, joint pain, fever, and swelling
 Immune complex-mediated rather than an immediate allergic reaction
 Treatment: Antihistamines, corticosteroids if severe
 5. Contact Dermatitis (Irritant Reaction from Wasp or Suit)
 Localized skin irritation due to venom, sweat, or friction from leather suit
 May cause redness, burning, or hives, but usually not full-body
 Mild cases resolve on their own

Diagnosis Task: I am a 28 years old woman and I came back from Thailand 5 days ago. There I celebrated the graduation with some girlfriends. Shortly before leaving, I noticed a greenish discharge, but I ignored it for now. However, in the last few days I have been feeling increasingly worse. I have an elevated temperature of 38 degrees, my entire lower abdomen hurts and I have pain when urinating. My preliminary examinations have so far always been unremarkable. I use contraception with the pill. I met a man in Thailand and had unprotected sex with him after a party. I don't know anything about him except that his name is Pablo and he is from Mexico. I read on the Internet that a fresh HIV infection could also be accompanied by fever and exhaustion. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Gonorrhea
 
  Model Output:1. Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) – Most Likely
 Fever, lower abdominal pain, painful urination, abnormal discharge
 Often caused by untreated gonorrhea or chlamydia
 Can lead to infertility or chronic pelvic pain if untreated
 Needs urgent antibiotic treatment (ceftriaxone + doxycycline + metronidazole)
 2. Gonorrhea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae)
 Greenish or yellow vaginal discharge, painful urination, pelvic pain
 Can cause PID if untreated
 Diagnosis: PCR test (vaginal/cervical swab or urine test)
 Treatment: Ceftriaxone + azithromycin/doxycycline
 3. Chlamydia (Chlamydia trachomatis)
 Often asymptomatic but can cause discharge, painful urination, and pelvic pain
 Can lead to PID and infertility if untreated
 Diagnosis: PCR test (swab or urine sample)
 Treatment: Azithromycin or doxycycline
 4. Trichomoniasis (Trichomonas vaginalis)
 Greenish, frothy discharge with a foul smell
 Itching, burning, pain during sex and urination
 Diagnosis: Microscopy or rapid antigen test
 Treatment: Metronidazole (both partners should be treated)
 5. Acute HIV Infection (Less Likely)
 Fever, fatigue, muscle pain, sore throat, swollen lymph nodes
 Usually occurs 2–4 weeks after exposure (yours may be too soon)
 Unlikely to cause localized pelvic symptoms
 Diagnosis: HIV PCR test (viral load) and antigen/antibody test

Diagnosis Task: I am a 34 year old woman expecting my first child. I am in the 36+2 SSW. Since yesterday I have been experiencing increasing upper abdominal pain, mainly localized in the right upper abdomen. I also have mild nausea. My husband must have also eaten something bad 2 days ago and is also feeling bad. I am still worried about the child. It is an absolutely desired child. The pregnancy has gone without complications so far. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: HELLP syndrome
 
  Model Output:1. HELLP Syndrome (Severe Pre-Eclampsia Variant) – Most Concerning
 Right upper abdominal pain (due to liver involvement)
 Nausea, malaise, elevated blood pressure, proteinuria (not always present early)
 Hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets (HELLP)
 Urgent evaluation needed (blood pressure check, liver/kidney function tests)
 2. Pre-Eclampsia
 Severe right upper quadrant (RUQ) pain, headache, vision changes
 High blood pressure, protein in urine
 Can progress to HELLP or eclampsia (seizures)
 Requires immediate medical assessment
 3. Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy (AFLP) – Rare but Dangerous
 RUQ pain, nausea, vomiting, jaundice, fatigue
 Signs of liver dysfunction (hypoglycemia, coagulopathy)
 High maternal and fetal risk – needs emergency care
 4. Gallbladder Disease (Gallstones or Cholecystitis)
 RUQ pain, nausea, worse after fatty meals
 More common in pregnancy due to hormonal changes
 Ultrasound to check for gallstones
 5. Gastroenteritis (Food Poisoning or Viral Infection)
 Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramps
 Your husband also feels unwell → suggests an infectious cause
 Supportive treatment unless dehydration or worsening symptoms

Diagnosis Task: I am a 32 year old woman and have had cycle related lower abdominal pain for over 15 years. In the beginning, the pain was not that severe and I believed it was normal during my period. However, for about 7 years the pain has been almost unbearable. In addition, for about 6 months I have also noticed rectal bleeding during my period. Pain during urination or bowel movements is something I have had almost all the time. I have been treated several times, changed my gynecologist, but no one has been able to help me. They always tell me that I have too much stress. In the meantime, the complaints are also putting a strain on my relationship. Sexual intercourse is extremely unpleasant and our desire to have a child has been unfulfilled for over three years. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Endometriosis
 
  Model Output:1. Deep Infiltrating Endometriosis (DIE) – Most Likely
 Severe cycle-related pelvic pain, progressively worsening
 Rectal bleeding during menstruation → suggests bowel involvement
 Painful urination and bowel movements → suggests bladder or bowel endometriosis
 Painful intercourse (dyspareunia) → common in deep endometriosis
 Infertility (a key symptom in severe endometriosis)
 Diagnosis: Pelvic ultrasound, MRI, or diagnostic laparoscopy
 2. Adenomyosis
 Severe cramping and heavy menstrual bleeding
 Enlarged, tender uterus (on examination)
 Can co-exist with endometriosis
 MRI or transvaginal ultrasound can help diagnose
 3. Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS) – Often Misdiagnosed in Endometriosis Patients
 Chronic pelvic pain with bowel symptoms
 Worsening pain with stress, but not cycle-dependent
 Can coexist with endometriosis, complicating diagnosis
 Diagnosis by exclusion, no structural abnormalities found
 4. Interstitial Cystitis / Bladder Pain Syndrome
 Chronic bladder pain and painful urination
 Pain worsens when bladder is full, relieved by voiding
 Can co-exist with endometriosis
 Cystoscopy and urodynamic testing may help
 5. Pelvic Congestion Syndrome (PCS)
 Chronic pelvic pain, worsens with standing or prolonged sitting
 Relief when lying down
 Associated with dilated pelvic veins (varicosities)
 Diagnosed via Doppler ultrasound, MRI, or venography

Diagnosis Task: I am a 42 year old woman and for the past few months I have noticed light vaginal bleeding after sexual intercourse and at times pain. This has never happened before. I also have a watery, brownish discharge that kind of irritates me. I had an abortion when I was 18. At that time I was not ready for a steady partnership, let alone family. I partied a lot back then, sometimes took drugs and smoked. In the meantime, I lead a very bourgeois life. I am married, have a daughter and a good job. I work a lot and therefore have not been to the gynecologist for 5 years. I have never had such complaints. Otherwise I have always been healthy and my cycle is regular. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cervical carcinoma
 
  Model Output:1. Cervical Cancer (Most Concerning)
 Postcoital bleeding (classic symptom)
 Watery, brownish, or foul-smelling discharge
 Pelvic pain, pain during intercourse
 Risk factors: HPV infection (which is sexually transmitted), smoking history, lack of recent Pap smears
 Diagnosis: Pap smear, HPV test, colposcopy, and biopsy
 2. Cervical Ectropion (Erosion)
 Common cause of postcoital bleeding
 Soft, friable cervical tissue that bleeds easily
 Often associated with hormonal changes (e.g., birth control pills)
 Diagnosis: Pelvic exam, colposcopy
 3. Cervicitis (Chronic or Infectious Inflammation)
 Can cause postcoital bleeding, discharge, and irritation
 Caused by bacterial vaginosis (BV), chlamydia, gonorrhea, or HPV
 Diagnosis: Cervical swabs for STI testing
 4. Endometrial Hyperplasia or Early Endometrial Cancer
 Irregular bleeding, spotting, or brownish discharge
 More common in women with irregular cycles or hormone imbalances
 Diagnosis: Transvaginal ultrasound, endometrial biopsy
 5. Atrophic Vaginitis (If Estrogen Declining)
 Thinning of vaginal walls due to low estrogen
 Can cause postcoital bleeding, irritation, and dryness
 More common after menopause but can start in perimenopause
 Diagnosis: Pelvic exam, hormone tests

Diagnosis Task: I am a yoga instructor and a participant just felt an extreme lower abdominal pain after an exercise and could not get up. She says she is actually healthy, just that her last period missed. She has a lot of stress, her boyfriend broke up with her 4 months ago and I guess that still bothers her a lot. She could not be pregnant because she has not had sexual intercourse for several months. She has never experienced this kind of pain before. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Adnexal torsion / ovarian torsion
 
  Model Output:Ovarian Cyst Rupture – Functional ovarian cysts can develop, especially in cases of menstrual irregularities. A sudden rupture can cause acute lower abdominal pain.
 
 Mittelschmerz (Ovulation Pain) – If her menstrual cycle is irregular due to stress, she might have ovulated unexpectedly. Some women experience severe, sudden pain when the follicle releases an egg.
 
 Endometriosis or Adenomyosis – If she has underlying endometriosis, stress and exercise could trigger acute pain due to inflammation and adhesions.
 
 Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) – Even though she hasn't had recent sexual activity, a past untreated or low-grade infection could flare up, causing lower abdominal pain.
 
 Muscle Strain or Abdominal Wall Injury – Strenuous yoga poses can strain abdominal or pelvic muscles, especially if she was performing deep stretches or core-focused movements.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 52 year old woman, actually athletic, slim and fit. But I haven't been able to sleep properly for months. I wake up all the time. In addition, my cycle is completely unpredictable. Sometimes the period stays away for several months and then it suddenly comes three times in quick succession and so strongly, as I don't know it at all. In addition, I am often depressed, quickly irritable and sweat very often. I have always been healthy, do not smoke and drink very little alcohol. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Menopause
 
  Model Output:Perimenopause – The most probable explanation given your age (52) and symptoms. Fluctuating estrogen and progesterone levels cause irregular periods, mood swings, night sweats, and sleep disturbances. Muscle function can also be affected due to hormonal shifts.
 
 Menopausal Transition-Related Sleep Disorder – Hormonal changes during perimenopause often lead to sleep disturbances, frequent awakenings, and night sweats, which can contribute to fatigue and reduced physical performance.
 
 Iron Deficiency Anemia – Heavy and frequent periods can lead to iron deficiency, causing fatigue, reduced strength, and even depressive moods. Anemia can also impact muscle performance, potentially explaining the punching difficulty.
 
 Hypothyroidism – Thyroid dysfunction can mimic perimenopausal symptoms, including fatigue, mood swings, sweating abnormalities, and irregular cycles. It’s important to check thyroid hormone levels to rule this out.
 
 Depressive Disorder (Hormonal-Linked) – Perimenopausal hormonal fluctuations can trigger mood disorders, including increased irritability, low energy, and depressive symptoms.

Diagnosis Task: I am a nurse and for the last 4 days I have been having severe pain at the vaginal entrance and on the left side I also feel a hardening that has become much bigger in the last few days. I went to my gynecologist yesterday but her ointment hardly helps. Today the pain is so bad that I can barely sit. I have never had gynecological problems before. My cycle is regular and I haven't had a change of partners either. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Bartholin's abscess / bartholinitis
 
  Model Output:1. Bartholin’s Gland Abscess (Most Likely)
 Painful swelling at the vaginal entrance, typically one-sided
 Can grow rapidly, causing severe pain when sitting or walking
 May start as a cyst but become infected, forming an abscess
 Treatment: Incision & drainage, antibiotics, sitz baths
 2. Bartholin’s Cyst (Non-Infected)
 Painless or mildly uncomfortable lump at the vaginal entrance
 Can become infected and turn into a painful abscess
 Treatment: Warm compresses, sitz baths, possible drainage if large
 3. Skene’s Gland Abscess
 Painful swelling near the urethra or vaginal entrance
 Can cause urinary discomfort or difficulty urinating
 Requires drainage and antibiotics if infected
 4. Infected Sebaceous Cyst or Folliculitis
 Small, hard lump near the vaginal entrance
 Can become red, painful, and swollen if infected
 Treatment: Warm compresses, topical antibiotics, drainage if needed
 5. Vulvar Cellulitis or Abscess
 Diffuse pain, redness, and swelling of the vulvar area
 Can be caused by bacterial infection of small skin breaks
 Treatment: Antibiotics, pain management, possible drainage

Diagnosis Task: I am a 20 year old female and have been having pain throughout my lower abdomen since yesterday, which initially got better but worsened significantly tonight. I have a fever up to 39.5 degrees. I do not have nausea or vomiting. Bowel movements are regular and firm. I am otherwise healthy and my last period was a week ago. My last gynecological checkup 6 months ago was unremarkable. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID)
 
  Model Output:Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) – An infection of the female reproductive organs (often due to sexually transmitted bacteria like chlamydia or gonorrhea). Symptoms include progressive lower abdominal pain, fever, and possible cervical motion tenderness. Even without recent gynecological symptoms, an asymptomatic infection could have progressed.
 
 Appendicitis – Early appendicitis can cause lower abdominal pain that initially improves but worsens again, sometimes without nausea or vomiting. The fever suggests an advanced stage, possibly early perforation or abscess formation.
 
 Ovarian Cyst Rupture with Secondary Infection – If an ovarian cyst ruptured, it could initially cause mild pain that worsens over time due to secondary infection or peritoneal irritation, leading to fever and severe lower abdominal pain.
 
 Endometritis – A uterine infection, sometimes occurring after menstruation, which can cause lower abdominal pain, fever, and uterine tenderness. It is more common after childbirth or gynecological procedures but can happen sporadically.
 
 Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) with Pyelonephritis – Although you do not mention urinary symptoms, bladder or kidney infections can sometimes present with lower abdominal pain and high fever, particularly if the infection has spread to the kidneys.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 28 year old female who has had severe right sided lower abdominal pain since this afternoon. No nausea, no vomiting, no fever. I have no previous illness and no appendix anymore. I am doubled over in pain though. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Ectopic pregnancy
 
  Model Output:1. Ovarian Torsion (Emergency)
 Sudden, severe unilateral pain (often right-sided)
 No nausea or vomiting required, but possible
 Risk factors: ovarian cyst, enlarged ovary, or spontaneous twisting
 Requires urgent ultrasound with Doppler to check blood flow
 2. Ruptured Ovarian Cyst
 Acute, sharp pain that may start suddenly
 Can be severe, especially if bleeding occurs
 Pain may radiate to back or thigh
 Ultrasound needed to confirm
 3. Kidney Stone (Ureteral Colic)
 Severe, colicky pain radiating from flank to lower abdomen or groin
 No fever, nausea, or vomiting required
 May have blood in urine (hematuria)
 CT scan without contrast or ultrasound needed
 4. Mittelschmerz (Ovulation Pain)
 Occurs mid-cycle (around ovulation)
 Unilateral, short-lived pain (can be severe)
 No other symptoms, self-limiting
 5. Endometriosis (Ruptured Endometriotic Cyst or Acute Flare)
 Severe lower abdominal pain, often cyclic but can be sudden
 May worsen during menstruation
 Often associated with infertility, painful periods, or pain during intercourse

Diagnosis Task: My newborn, Chris (10 days old), is increasingly tired, doesn't want to drink, and keeps vomiting. We had a normal pregnancy. The birth weight was 3490g. Everything went smoothly and so we were discharged after 3 days. We did not do a newborn screening because the examination for congenital hormonal and metabolic disorders in the children from the first marriage in each case had been unremarkable. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia
 
  Model Output:Neonatal Sepsis – A life-threatening bacterial infection (often Group B Streptococcus or E. coli) that can cause lethargy, poor feeding, and vomiting. If untreated, it can progress to shock and organ failure.
 
 Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) – A disorder of cortisol and aldosterone production, leading to electrolyte imbalances (low sodium, high potassium), dehydration, and shock-like symptoms in the second week of life. Vomiting, poor feeding, and lethargy are early warning signs.
 
 Inborn Errors of Metabolism (e.g., Urea Cycle Disorder, Galactosemia, Maple Syrup Urine Disease) – If metabolic disorders are not detected in newborn screening, they can lead to toxic metabolite accumulation, causing vomiting, poor feeding, lethargy, seizures, or coma. Galactosemia worsens after lactose (milk) intake.
 
 Pyloric Stenosis (Less Likely at 10 Days) – A condition where the stomach outlet is narrowed, causing projectile vomiting, dehydration, and weight loss. However, it typically develops between 2-6 weeks of age.
 
 Hypothyroidism (Congenital or Acquired) – Untreated hypothyroidism can cause lethargy, feeding difficulties, prolonged jaundice, and poor growth. Newborn screening typically detects it, but it was missed in Chris.

Diagnosis Task: My 2 year old son suffers from infections very frequently. I first noticed poor wound healing when he was 8 months old. An abrasion quickly became suppurative, which was treated with antibiotics as an inpatient. Since then, our son has received antibiotic therapy four times for ear infections. A week ago, he was discharged after a 10-day inpatient stay with pneumonia and already has another cold. He also has a fever of 38.3. This is not normal. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Agammaglobulinemia
 
  Model Output:1. Chronic Granulomatous Disease (CGD)
 Poor wound healing with suppurative infections
 Frequent bacterial and fungal infections (especially pneumonia, abscesses)
 Defective phagocytic function (impaired neutrophil oxidative burst)
 2. X-linked Agammaglobulinemia (XLA, Bruton's Disease)
 Recurrent bacterial infections (otitis media, pneumonia, skin infections)
 Low or absent B cells and immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM)
 Onset around 6 months of age (maternal antibodies wane)
 3. Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID)
 Recurrent respiratory infections (pneumonia, sinusitis, otitis)
 Low immunoglobulin levels (IgG, IgA, sometimes IgM)
 Can present in early childhood or later
 4. Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency (LAD)
 Delayed wound healing with skin infections
 Recurrent bacterial infections (without pus formation)
 Elevated white blood cell (WBC) count but defective migration
 5. Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID)
 Life-threatening bacterial, viral, and fungal infections
 Failure to thrive and persistent infections
 Requires urgent workup (flow cytometry for T, B, NK cells)

Diagnosis Task: I am a mother of a 7 year old son. I have been annoyed for three days that toothpaste always drips out of his mouth onto his shirt when he brushes his teeth. Today I also noticed that his facial expressions on the right side of his face are almost absent and his right eye is watery. He is otherwise calm but quite tired. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Lyme Neuroborreliosis
 
  Model Output:1. Bell’s Palsy (Idiopathic Facial Nerve Paralysis)
 Unilateral facial weakness (often sudden onset)
 Drooling, difficulty closing one eye, and watery eye (due to reduced blinking)
 Often preceded by viral infections (e.g., herpes simplex virus)
 Usually self-limiting but may need corticosteroids
 2. Otitis Media with Facial Nerve Involvement
 Middle ear infection spreading to the facial nerve
 Unilateral facial weakness with ear pain or recent ear infection
 Possible hearing loss or fever
 Needs urgent ENT evaluation and antibiotics
 3. Lyme Disease (Neuroborreliosis)
 Facial nerve palsy (often bilateral but can be unilateral)
 Fatigue, mild fever, headache
 Possible history of tick exposure
 Serologic testing for Borrelia burgdorferi needed
 4. Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS, Miller Fisher Variant)
 Progressive weakness (often starting in the face)
 Possible difficulty swallowing, eye problems (watery eye, double vision)
 History of recent viral illness
 Needs urgent hospital evaluation (risk of respiratory failure)
 5. Brainstem Tumor (Rare but Important to Consider)
 Slowly progressive facial weakness
 Fatigue and possibly headaches
 Needs MRI of the brain to rule out a central lesion

Diagnosis Task: My son is 15 months old. For 2 days he has had a cold, sore throat and fever up to a maximum of 40 degrees. Due to a positive throat swab for group A streptococcus the day before at the pediatrician, we are now giving him an antibiotic treatment with penicillin. Today there was a situation in which Marcel was very quiet, he no longer reacted properly to speech, his eyes were often turned upwards and he twitched at all extremities. This condition lasted for about 60 seconds. After that, he fell asleep. There are no allergies, pre-existing conditions or permanent medication. He was also vaccinated according to STIKO recommendations. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Febrile seizure
 
  Model Output:Febrile Seizure – The most probable diagnosis. These seizures occur in young children due to a rapid rise in fever, typically lasting less than 5 minutes, followed by sleepiness. Simple febrile seizures do not cause long-term harm but should be monitored.
 
 Meningitis (Bacterial or Viral) – Although less likely since he was vaccinated, bacterial meningitis (e.g., from Streptococcus pneumoniae or Neisseria meningitidis) or viral meningitis can cause fever, seizures, lethargy, and altered consciousness. Neck stiffness and irritability would be concerning additional symptoms.
 
 Scarlet Fever with CNS Involvement – Since Group A Streptococcus causes scarlet fever, rare cases may involve neurological symptoms, including encephalopathy or post-infectious complications.
 
 Antibiotic Reaction (Penicillin-Induced Seizure, Though Rare) – Some children experience neurotoxic reactions to penicillin, especially in cases of renal impairment or high doses, leading to seizures.
 
 Epileptic Seizure (First-Time Epilepsy Onset) – Although less likely, first-time epilepsy (e.g., absence or generalized epilepsy) can appear around this age. However, the fever makes febrile seizure more likely.

Diagnosis Task: My 14 year old daughter Mira has been complaining of abdominal pain for the past 6 months, mainly localized in the right lower abdomen. They occur especially after eating and before she goes to the toilet. This is also the reason why Mira is eating less and less and has lost 4kg of weight in the last months. Partly she also has mushy and mucous diarrhea. She also seems very weak. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Crohn's disease
 
  Model Output:Crohn’s Disease – A chronic inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) that often affects the terminal ileum (right lower abdomen). Symptoms include abdominal pain, diarrhea with mucus, weight loss, and fatigue. The worsening after eating and chronicity strongly suggest this.
 
 Ulcerative Colitis (UC, Atypical Presentation) – Another form of IBD, but it mainly affects the colon. It causes bloody or mucous diarrhea, abdominal cramps, and weight loss, though right-sided pain is less typical than in Crohn’s.
 
 Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS, Possibly IBS-D) – Though less likely due to weight loss and weakness, postprandial pain, mucous stools, and bowel irregularities could suggest IBS. However, IBS does not typically cause inflammation or severe malnutrition.
 
 Celiac Disease – A reaction to gluten that causes intestinal damage, leading to chronic diarrhea, weight loss, and malabsorption. It can present with fatigue and growth problems in teenagers.
 
 Chronic Appendicitis (Less Common but Possible) – A rare form of appendiceal inflammation causing chronic, intermittent right lower quadrant pain, sometimes

Diagnosis Task: My 10 month old infant may have a failure to thrive. He has barely gained weight since 6 months of age and weight has slipped from the 25th to the 3rd percentile. Stools are bulky, greasy, and smelly. At 8 months of age, Tim has gone through a persistent upper respiratory infection with whistling breath sounds. He also tastes strangely salty when kissed. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cystic fibrosis
 
  Model Output:1. Cystic Fibrosis (CF) – Most Likely
 Failure to thrive despite adequate nutrition
 Bulky, greasy, foul-smelling stools (steatorrhea) → pancreatic insufficiency
 Frequent respiratory infections, wheezing
 Salty-tasting skin (high chloride in sweat → classic CF sign)
 Diagnosis: Sweat chloride test, genetic testing for CFTR mutations
 2. Celiac Disease (Gluten-Sensitive Enteropathy)
 Failure to thrive, poor weight gain
 Bulky, foul-smelling stools (malabsorption)
 Can present after gluten introduction (~6 months)
 Diagnosis: Anti-tTG (tissue transglutaminase) antibodies, biopsy
 3. Exocrine Pancreatic Insufficiency (EPI) from Another Cause
 Pancreatic dysfunction (other than CF)
 Malabsorption with greasy stools
 May be linked to Shwachman-Diamond syndrome (rare genetic disorder)
 Diagnosis: Fecal elastase test, genetic workup
 4. Primary Immunodeficiency (e.g., Severe Combined Immunodeficiency - SCID)
 Failure to thrive, chronic diarrhea
 Frequent respiratory infections
 Diagnosis: Lymphocyte subset analysis, immunoglobulin levels
 5. Congenital Chloride Diarrhea (Rare)
 Salty sweat and diarrhea from birth
 Failure to thrive, electrolyte imbalances
 Diagnosis: Stool chloride test, genetic testing

disease_GT: Insulin-dependent Diabetes mellitus / Diabetes mellitus type 1
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: My daughter, Zoe, is 5 years old and has been floppy and tired for four weeks. Since last week she has been complaining of abdominal pain, has to urinate frequently and threw up twice yesterday. She had a febrile upper respiratory infection 8 weeks ago. Zoe's appetite remains good. However, she has lost 2 kg in weight. She drinks very well, up to 4 liters a day and always asks for iced tea and lemonade. So far, Zoe has always been healthy. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Insulin-dependent Diabetes mellitus / Diabetes mellitus type 1
 
  Model Output:1. Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) – Most Likely & Life-Threatening
 Excessive thirst (polydipsia), frequent urination (polyuria), weight loss, fatigue, vomiting
 Craving for sweet drinks (due to high blood sugar levels)
 Risk of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) → a medical emergency
 Diagnosis: Urgent blood glucose test, urine ketones, HbA1c
 ⚠ Immediate action: Take Zoe to a doctor immediately for blood sugar testing!
 2. Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)
 Frequent urination, abdominal pain, fatigue
 Possible fever (if progressing to pyelonephritis)
 Diagnosis: Urinalysis, urine culture
 Treatment: Antibiotics if UTI is confirmed
 3. Diabetes Insipidus (DI) – Rare but Considered
 Excessive drinking (polydipsia) and urination (polyuria)
 Usually, urine is very dilute (low specific gravity)
 No weight loss or vomiting unless dehydrated
 Diagnosis: Water deprivation test, serum osmolality, urine osmolality
 4. Addison’s Disease (Adrenal Insufficiency)
 Fatigue, weight loss, vomiting, salt cravings
 Can cause abdominal pain and low blood pressure
 Diagnosis: ACTH stimulation test, cortisol levels
 5. Chronic Kidney Disease (Less Likely)
 Fatigue, weight loss, excessive thirst, frequent urination
 Could cause electrolyte imbalance and poor growth
 Diagnosis: Blood tests (creatinine, urea), urine analysis

Diagnosis Task: My 4 year old boy has had an increasingly waddling and unsteady gait since age 2. He also has difficulty standing up. The symptoms have continuously worsened. Otherwise Peter is a healthy, agile boy. He has been walking freely since the age of 17 months. But now I always have to think of my great-uncle, who died of muscle weakness at the age of 20. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD)
 
  Model Output:1. Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD) – Most Likely
 Progressive muscle weakness starting in early childhood (age 2–5)
 Waddling gait, difficulty standing up (Gower’s sign: using hands to push on thighs to stand)
 Pseudohypertrophy of calves (large but weak calf muscles)
 X-linked recessive → affects boys, inherited from the mother
 Diagnosis: Creatine kinase (CK) levels (very high), genetic testing for DMD gene mutation
 2. Becker Muscular Dystrophy (BMD) – Milder Variant of DMD
 Similar to DMD but slower progression (symptoms start later, around age 5–15)
 Can still walk into late teens or early adulthood
 Diagnosis: Creatine kinase (CK) levels, genetic testing
 3. Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA) – Type 3 (Kugelberg-Welander Disease)
 Progressive muscle weakness but with normal intelligence
 Proximal muscles (hip and shoulder) affected more
 No calf hypertrophy, unlike DMD
 Diagnosis: Genetic testing (SMN1 gene deletion)
 4. Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy (LGMD)
 Affects pelvic and shoulder muscles
 Progresses more slowly than DMD
 Can be inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern
 Diagnosis: Muscle biopsy, genetic testing
 5. Congenital Myopathy (e.g., Central Core Disease)
 Floppy baby at birth or delayed milestones, but progressive weakness later
 Usually milder than DMD/BMD
 Diagnosis: Muscle biopsy, genetic testing

Diagnosis Task: My 13 year old daughter is gaining an inordinate amount of weight and this is with a growth arrest. She has also been in a depressive phase for about 6 weeks. Our otherwise cheerful daughter is withdrawing more and more, reads a lot and has few social contacts. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cushing's disease
 
  Model Output:1. Hypothyroidism (Most Likely)
 Weight gain despite normal eating habits
 Fatigue, depression, social withdrawal
 Delayed growth (growth arrest)
 Dry skin, cold intolerance, constipation possible
 Diagnosis: Check TSH, free T4, and anti-TPO antibodies
 2. Cushing’s Syndrome (Excess Cortisol)
 Rapid weight gain, especially in face, neck, and trunk ("moon face" and truncal obesity)
 Growth delay (cortisol suppresses growth hormone)
 Depression, irritability, or anxiety
 Purple stretch marks (striae), high blood pressure, easy bruising
 Diagnosis: 24-hour urine cortisol, dexamethasone suppression test, ACTH levels
 3. Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)
 Weight gain, irregular periods (if she has started menstruating)
 Depression and social withdrawal (common in adolescents with PCOS)
 Acne, excessive hair growth (hirsutism)
 Insulin resistance (may have dark skin patches – acanthosis nigricans)
 Diagnosis: LH/FSH ratio, testosterone levels, fasting insulin, pelvic ultrasound
 4. Atypical Depression (Psychiatric Cause)
 Weight gain (increased appetite, craving for sweets/carbs)
 Fatigue, lack of motivation, social withdrawal
 Sensitive to rejection, excessive sleeping (hypersomnia)
 Diagnosis: Psychological evaluation (rule out organic causes first)
 5. Growth Hormone Deficiency (GHD)
 Growth arrest (short stature compared to peers)
 Mild weight gain (due to low metabolism)
 Fatigue, depressive mood
 Delayed puberty (if pubertal development is also affected)
 Diagnosis: IGF-1, growth hormone stimulation test, bone age X-ray

Diagnosis Task: I am a 23 year old woman and have severe abdominal pain. At times they are also crampy. They started the night before after a pizza with mild nausea in the upper abdomen and shifted to the lower right abdominal region during the day. I vomited once in the morning. I feel listless and have no appetite at all. My bowel movements and urine are unremarkable. I can't think of any important previous illnesses. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute appendicitis
 
  Model Output:1. Acute Appendicitis (Most Likely – Medical Emergency)
 Classic presentation:
 Starts as vague upper abdominal pain → shifts to the right lower abdomen (McBurney’s point)
 Nausea, vomiting, and loss of appetite
 Fever may develop later
 Pain worsens with movement (e.g., hopping on one leg may be painful)
 Diagnosis:
 Clinical exam (rebound tenderness, Rovsing’s sign, psoas sign)
 Abdominal ultrasound or CT scan
 ⚠ Urgent surgical evaluation needed (appendectomy if confirmed).
 2. Gastroenteritis (Food Poisoning)
 Starts with nausea, vomiting, and cramping abdominal pain
 Recent pizza meal suggests foodborne illness (bacteria like Salmonella, E. coli)
 Symptoms usually include diarrhea (you don’t have this, making appendicitis more likely)
 Diagnosis: Stool tests (if diarrhea develops), symptom monitoring
 3. Ovarian Cyst Rupture (Gynecological Cause)
 Sudden severe lower right abdominal pain
 Can mimic appendicitis
 May have mild nausea but no fever
 Diagnosis: Pelvic ultrasound
 4. Mesenteric Lymphadenitis (Inflamed Abdominal Lymph Nodes)
 Common in young adults, often follows a viral infection
 Pain in the lower right abdomen (can mimic appendicitis)
 Mild fever, nausea, but usually no severe rebound tenderness
 Diagnosis: Ultrasound (shows swollen lymph nodes), rule out appendicitis
 5. Right-Sided Ureteral Stone (Kidney Stone)
 Sharp, crampy pain radiating from the flank to the lower abdomen
 May cause nausea and vomiting
 Pain is usually wave-like and intense, but urine is unremarkable in your case
 Diagnosis: CT scan or ultrasound

Diagnosis Task: I am a middle-aged man (42) and have a swelling in the left groin area. It appeared after a violent coughing attack and is painful. I noticed a small bump on the groin some time ago. But it did not hurt yet. I am a smoker (30 cigarettes/day) and therefore have a chronic smoker's cough. Otherwise I have nothing. Two years ago I had a meniscus operation on my left knee. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Inguinal hernia (indirect/direct?)
 
  Model Output:1. Inguinal Hernia (Most Likely)
 Groin swelling that appeared after coughing
 Small lump was already present before → now painful
 Can enlarge with coughing or standing, may reduce when lying down
 Complication: Strangulation (severe pain, no reducibility, possible bowel obstruction → emergency!)
 Diagnosis: Physical exam, ultrasound if unclear
 2. Femoral Hernia
 Less common than inguinal hernia, more frequent in women but possible in men
 Appears in the groin, just below the inguinal ligament
 Higher risk of strangulation than inguinal hernias
 Diagnosis: Clinical exam, ultrasound
 3. Enlarged Lymph Node (Reactive Lymphadenopathy)
 Can appear as a swollen lump in the groin
 Caused by infections, inflammation, or malignancies
 Usually firm but not reducible like a hernia
 Diagnosis: Ultrasound, blood tests if infection suspected
 4. Lipoma (Benign Fat Tumor)
 Soft, painless lump in the groin
 Does not change with coughing or straining
 No associated pain unless it presses on nerves
 Diagnosis: Physical exam, ultrasound
 5. Saphena Varix (Enlarged Vein at the Saphenous-Femoral Junction)
 Soft, compressible swelling in the groin
 Becomes more visible when standing, disappears when lying down
 Mimics a hernia but is venous in origin
 Diagnosis: Doppler ultrasound

Diagnosis Task: I am a 59-year-old woman and have had increasing pain in my left lower abdomen for 2 days. Since then I feel weak, have slight diarrhea and no appetite. Today I also have a fever. About six months ago I had similar complaints, but not in this intensity. My urine is normal. 12 years ago I had a hysterectomy (because of uterine fibroids) and three years ago my gallbladder was removed because of gallstones. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Diverticulitis
 
  Model Output:1. Acute Diverticulitis (Most Likely)
 Left lower abdominal pain (classic location for sigmoid colon involvement)
 Fever, weakness, loss of appetite
 Mild diarrhea (possible)
 Previous similar episode 6 months ago → suggests recurrent diverticulitis
 Diagnosis:
 CT abdomen with contrast (gold standard)
 CRP, WBC (elevated in infection/inflammation)
 2. Infectious Colitis (e.g., Bacterial or Viral)
 Lower abdominal pain with diarrhea and fever
 Weakness and loss of appetite due to infection
 Common causes: Campylobacter, Salmonella, Clostridium difficile
 Diagnosis: Stool culture, PCR for bacterial pathogens
 3. Ischemic Colitis
 Left-sided abdominal pain, fever, diarrhea (sometimes bloody)
 Risk factors: Older age, vascular disease
 Diagnosis: Colonoscopy (after ruling out perforation), CT angiography
 4. Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) – Ulcerative Colitis or Crohn’s Disease
 Recurrent abdominal pain and diarrhea (sometimes bloody)
 Can flare up periodically
 Diagnosis: Colonoscopy with biopsy
 5. Pelvic Abscess or Post-Surgical Adhesions (Less Likely but Considered)
 Post-hysterectomy adhesions may cause intermittent pain
 Abscess possible with fever and worsening pain
 Diagnosis: Pelvic ultrasound or CT scan

Diagnosis Task: I am a 24 year old woman and have been having lower abdominal pain (especially on the right) and increasing diarrhea for a few days. At times there is also slight nausea and twice I had to vomit. I have already had my appendix removed as a child. I have no complaints/pre-existing conditions apart from lactose intolerance. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Crohn's Disease
 
  Model Output:1. Ileocolitis (Possible Crohn’s Disease) – Most Likely
 Chronic inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) affecting the terminal ileum (right lower abdomen)
 Diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, weight loss
 Pain mimicking appendicitis but with persistent diarrhea
 Diagnosis: Stool tests, blood tests (CRP, fecal calprotectin), colonoscopy
 2. Infectious Gastroenteritis (Bacterial or Viral)
 Acute onset of diarrhea, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting
 Common causes: Norovirus, Salmonella, Campylobacter, E. coli
 Short-lived, self-limiting unless bacterial
 Diagnosis: Stool culture, PCR if persistent symptoms
 3. Yersinia Enterocolitica Infection ("Pseudoappendicitis")
 Right lower abdominal pain with diarrhea and nausea
 Can mimic appendicitis but persists after appendectomy
 More common in contaminated food (pork, unpasteurized milk)
 Diagnosis: Stool culture, serology
 4. Lactose Intolerance Flare-Up (Less Likely but Possible)
 Diarrhea, bloating, cramping after dairy consumption
 Nausea is possible but vomiting is rare
 No fever or severe pain usually
 Diagnosis: Lactose elimination diet, breath test
 5. Mesenteric Lymphadenitis
 Inflamed lymph nodes in the abdomen, often post-viral
 Mimics appendicitis but improves with time
 Diagnosis: Abdominal ultrasound

Diagnosis Task: I am a 45 year old woman and woke up this morning with back pain. I could only go about my daily life in pain and taking a tablet of ibuprofen. Then the pain got so bad that I was afraid my back would break apart. The pain also radiates into my right leg. It is so strong. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Spinal disc herniation
 
  Model Output:1. Lumbar Disc Herniation (Most Likely)
 Severe lower back pain with radiating leg pain (sciatica)
 Pain worsens with movement, bending, or sitting
 Possible numbness/tingling in the leg or foot
 Diagnosis: MRI of the lumbar spine
 2. Lumbar Radiculopathy (Sciatica)
 Compression or irritation of a spinal nerve root (often from a herniated disc)
 Sharp, shooting pain down the leg (follows nerve distribution)
 Possible weakness or numbness in the affected leg
 Diagnosis: Clinical exam, MRI if severe or persistent
 3. Lumbar Spinal Stenosis
 Narrowing of the spinal canal causing nerve compression
 Pain worsens with standing or walking, relieved by sitting
 Common in middle-aged individuals
 Diagnosis: MRI of the lumbar spine
 4. Vertebral Compression Fracture (Less Likely but Considered)
 Sudden, severe back pain, especially in osteoporosis or after minor trauma
 Pain worsens with movement, but no leg pain unless nerve compression
 Diagnosis: X-ray or MRI of the spine
 5. Piriformis Syndrome
 Compression of the sciatic nerve by the piriformis muscle
 Mimics sciatica with leg pain but not caused by a spinal issue
 Diagnosis: Clinical exam, no findings on MRI

Diagnosis Task: I am a 19 year old female student and I have been having an existing headache on my left temple and behind my eye since morning. The pain is throbbing and very severe. The headache has developed over a few minutes and is accompanied by marked nausea and vomiting. Movement and bright light make it worse. Over the past 6 months, there have been five similar episodes, each lasting 1-2 days. This time, however, the headache has lasted longer and I have seen flickering for 20 minutes before the headache started. I have no pre-existing conditions and I am not taking any medications. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Migraine (with/without aura)
 
  Model Output:1. Migraine with Aura (Most Likely)
 Throbbing headache, usually unilateral (one side of the head)
 Aura before the headache (flickering lights, zigzag lines, blind spots)
 Severe nausea, vomiting, sensitivity to light (photophobia) and sound (phonophobia)
 Lasts 4-72 hours, worsens with movement
 Diagnosis: Clinical history (no specific tests needed)
 2. Cluster Headache
 Severe, stabbing pain around one eye or temple
 Associated with red eye, tearing, nasal congestion, or drooping eyelid
 Episodes occur in clusters (weeks/months of frequent attacks, then remission)
 Shorter duration than migraine (15-180 min per attack)
 Diagnosis: Clinical, may require imaging to rule out secondary causes
 3. Tension-Type Headache (Less Likely, But Considered)
 Mild to moderate, pressure-like headache (not throbbing)
 Bilateral (not usually one-sided)
 No aura or vomiting, less sensitivity to light/noise
 Diagnosis: Clinical history
 4. Reversible Cerebral Vasoconstriction Syndrome (RCVS)
 Sudden, severe "thunderclap" headache
 May be triggered by stress, exertion, or vasoactive substances (e.g., caffeine, drugs)
 Diagnosis: Brain imaging (CT/MRI, MRA for vasospasms)
 5. Secondary Causes (Less Likely but Need to Rule Out)
 Meningitis (if fever, neck stiffness, altered mental status)
 Cerebral Venous Sinus Thrombosis (if on birth control, severe headaches, neurological symptoms)
 Brain aneurysm/subarachnoid hemorrhage (if worst headache of life, sudden onset, neurological deficits)

Diagnosis Task: I am a 25-year-old man and I have incredibly severe pain in my right eye. It is just 2 am and I feel like someone is stabbing me in the eye with a knife. I am unable to sit or lie still and just woke up in pain. When I look in the mirror, the right eye looks red and the eyelid hangs a bit. I had a similar event a week ago, but it was less painful and my dentist attributed it to a root infection in my upper right jaw. I have no pre-existing conditions and am not on any medications. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cluster headache
 
  Model Output:1. Cluster Headache (Most Likely) – "Suicide Headache"
 Excruciating, stabbing pain around one eye or temple
 Red, watery eye, drooping eyelid, nasal congestion or runny nose
 Restlessness (unable to sit or lie still)
 Attacks last 15-180 minutes, often occur at night, same time daily
 Can be mistaken for sinus or dental pain
 Diagnosis: Clinical (Neurologist), brain MRI to rule out other causes
 Treatment:
 Acute: 100% oxygen therapy, sumatriptan injection/nasal spray
 Preventive: Verapamil, steroids, CGRP inhibitors
 2. Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma (Ophthalmic Emergency)
 Severe eye pain, red eye, blurred vision, headache, nausea/vomiting
 Eye feels hard on touch
 Increased intraocular pressure (IOP) can cause permanent vision loss
 Diagnosis: Urgent intraocular pressure (IOP) measurement, slit-lamp exam
 Treatment: Emergency ophthalmology referral, pressure-lowering eye drops, laser therapy
 3. Trigeminal Neuralgia (Atypical Facial Pain)
 Sudden, severe, stabbing or electric-shock pain
 Triggered by touch, chewing, or dental procedures
 Can mimic dental infections, but no true tooth issue
 Diagnosis: MRI to rule out compression of trigeminal nerve
 Treatment: Carbamazepine or gabapentin
 4. Sinusitis with Orbital or Retrobulbar Involvement
 Severe pain behind or around the eye, worse when bending forward
 Facial pressure, congestion, postnasal drip
 May cause periorbital swelling
 Diagnosis: Sinus CT scan
 Treatment: Antibiotics if bacterial, nasal decongestants
 5. Orbital Cellulitis (If Fever & Eye Swelling Present)
 Eye redness, swelling, pain with eye movement
 Fever, possible vision impairment
 Can be a complication of sinus or dental infections
 Diagnosis: CT scan of orbits, blood tests
 Treatment: IV antibiotics

Diagnosis Task: I am a 40-year-old woman and I have not been able to walk properly for 2 days. My legs are numb and I have pain in my abdomen. 6 months ago I noticed a visual disturbance on the left side that lasted for days, but I did not go to the doctor. Otherwise, I have only celiac disease as a pre-existing condition. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder
 
  Model Output:1. Multiple Sclerosis (MS) – Most Likely
 Neurological symptoms affecting different areas at different times ("dissemination in time and space")
 Previous visual disturbance (likely optic neuritis) → suggests MS
 Now presenting with leg numbness, difficulty walking → suggests spinal cord involvement
 Common in young to middle-aged women (onset 20-40 years old)
 Diagnosis: Brain and spinal cord MRI with contrast (white matter lesions), lumbar puncture for oligoclonal bands
 2. Transverse Myelitis (Spinal Cord Inflammation)
 Sudden-onset leg weakness, numbness, and sensory loss
 May cause bowel/bladder dysfunction
 Associated with autoimmune conditions (including celiac disease)
 Diagnosis: Spinal MRI, lumbar puncture
 3. Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder (NMOSD)
 Severe spinal cord inflammation + previous optic neuritis (vision loss episode)
 Often mistaken for MS but more aggressive
 Diagnosis: Anti-AQP4 antibody test, spinal MRI
 4. Gluten Ataxia (Neurological Manifestation of Celiac Disease)
 Progressive difficulty walking, balance issues, and numbness
 Associated with celiac disease and gluten sensitivity
 Can cause neuropathy and ataxia (lack of muscle coordination)
 Diagnosis: Gluten-free diet trial, neurological exam, MRI
 5. Spinal Cord Compression (Herniated Disc, Tumor, or Other Lesion)
 Sudden leg weakness, numbness, and pain
 Possible due to degenerative spine disease, tumors, or infections
 Diagnosis: Spinal MRI

Diagnosis Task: My 53-year-old husband has been suffering from inner restlessness for 2 years. He constantly gets involuntary, enclosing movements in his arms, legs and face. He can hardly stand still and sometimes it looks like he is dancing. In the beginning he was able to cover this up well and he simply integrated the movement with others. In the meantime, however, this is no longer possible. During physical activity or due to mental tension, his complaints increase. He also becomes increasingly aggressive and forgets things more often. Together we have two children and four grandchildren. As a pre-existing condition he suffers only from diabetes, which he treats with insulin. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Huntington's disease
 
  Model Output:1. Huntington’s Disease (Most Likely)
 Progressive involuntary movements (chorea), cognitive decline, personality changes (aggression, forgetfulness)
 Symptoms worsen with emotional stress or movement
 Usually begins between ages 30-50, worsens over time
 Genetic disorder (autosomal dominant, CAG repeat expansion in the HTT gene)
 Diagnosis: Genetic testing for HTT gene mutation, brain MRI (caudate atrophy)
 2. Tardive Dyskinesia (If He Has Taken Dopamine-Blocking Medications)
 Involuntary, repetitive movements of the face, mouth, and limbs
 Associated with long-term use of neuroleptics (antipsychotics) or dopamine-blocking drugs (e.g., metoclopramide)
 Diagnosis: Clinical history (check medication use)
 3. Wilson’s Disease (Rare, but Possible Given Neurological & Behavioral Symptoms)
 Abnormal copper metabolism leading to movement disorders and psychiatric symptoms
 Chorea, dystonia, behavioral changes (aggression, forgetfulness)
 May also present with liver dysfunction
 Diagnosis: Serum ceruloplasmin, 24-hour urine copper, slit-lamp exam for Kayser-Fleischer rings
 4. Chorea Due to Uncontrolled Diabetes ("Diabetic Chorea" or "Hemichorea-Hemiballismus")
 Occurs in patients with poorly controlled diabetes (especially Type 2)
 Sudden, involuntary movements, often unilateral
 Resolves with better blood sugar control
 Diagnosis: Blood glucose levels, HbA1c, brain imaging
 5. Parkinson’s Disease or Atypical Parkinsonism (Less Likely but Considered)
 May have "dancing-like" movements if associated with dopamine dysregulation
 Early cognitive symptoms, personality changes, and restlessness
 Diagnosis: Neurological exam, dopamine transporter (DaT) scan

Diagnosis Task: I am a woman (52 years old) and I have weak legs. In the last week I have noticed the problem increasingly when climbing stairs. I live on the 4th floor and have always been able to get up there without any problems. Lately, however, I always need breaks in between. This usually manifests itself as weakness and pain in the pelvic muscles, although I have no shortness of breath or pressure on my chest. In addition, there is a lilac redness on my face. This does not bother me, neither itching nor pain. For this I now take a more covering day cream. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Dermatomyositis
 
  Model Output:1. Dermatomyositis (Most Likely)
 Progressive proximal muscle weakness (difficulty climbing stairs, standing up, lifting arms)
 Lilac-colored (heliotrope) rash around the eyes, cheeks, or upper body
 Can be associated with autoimmune diseases or underlying cancer (paraneoplastic syndrome in adults)
 Diagnosis:
 Muscle enzymes (CK, LDH, AST/ALT) → Elevated
 Autoantibodies (ANA, anti-Jo-1, anti-Mi-2)
 EMG (electromyography), muscle biopsy
 Skin biopsy if needed
 2. Polymyositis (Inflammatory Muscle Disease Without Skin Symptoms)
 Proximal muscle weakness (legs, pelvic area, arms)
 No rash, but possible joint pain
 Often linked to autoimmune conditions
 Diagnosis: Muscle biopsy, CK levels, autoantibodies
 3. Myasthenia Gravis (Neuromuscular Junction Disorder)
 Muscle weakness worsening with activity, improving with rest
 Drooping eyelids (ptosis) and difficulty speaking/swallowing in some cases
 No skin rash
 Diagnosis: Acetylcholine receptor antibody test, EMG, Tensilon test
 4. Hypothyroid Myopathy (Thyroid-Related Muscle Weakness)
 Muscle weakness, fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance
 Dry skin, puffy face (but no lilac rash)
 Diagnosis: TSH, free T4 levels
 5. Statin-Induced Myopathy (If Taking Cholesterol Medication)
 Muscle pain and weakness, especially in large muscle groups
 Possible with statin use (e.g., atorvastatin, simvastatin)
 Diagnosis: CK levels, stopping statin to see if symptoms improve

Diagnosis Task: I am a 35-year-old painter and have had weak hands and arms for several months. That's why I always have problems holding the paintbrush. I have also been asked about my thinner arms and legs, even though I used to do a lot of sports. Now that I pay attention to it, my hands and shoulders cramp just at the beginning of the workday. This stops after a few seconds of hand movement. However, they only hurt moderately. My father had similar complaints and then died of a heart attack at the age of 45. In addition, I suffer from the following pre-existing conditions: diabetes, unclear cardiac arrhythmia and cataracts. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)
 
  Model Output:1. Myotonic Dystrophy Type 1 (DM1) – Most Likely
 Progressive weakness in hands and arms, muscle atrophy
 Cramping and delayed muscle relaxation ("grip myotonia") → hands cramp but relax after a few seconds
 Cataracts (very common in DM1, often early onset like yours)
 Cardiac arrhythmias (can lead to sudden death in undiagnosed cases)
 Family history suggests autosomal dominant inheritance
 Diagnosis: Genetic testing for DMPK gene expansion, EMG (shows myotonia), cardiac evaluation
 2. Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS)
 Progressive muscle weakness, atrophy in hands, arms, and legs
 Fasciculations (muscle twitching), cramping, but normal sensation
 Typically no early cardiac involvement (but secondary effects possible)
 Diagnosis: EMG/NCS (nerve conduction studies), ruling out other conditions
 3. Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy (LGMD)
 Weakness in shoulders and arms (often affecting painters, electricians first)
 Progressive muscle wasting
 Possible cardiac involvement depending on subtype
 Diagnosis: Genetic testing, muscle biopsy, CK levels
 4. Mitochondrial Myopathy (MELAS Syndrome or Other Mitochondrial Disorder)
 Progressive muscle weakness with metabolic symptoms
 Cardiac involvement, diabetes, and cataracts are common
 Exercise intolerance, possibly mild lactic acidosis
 Diagnosis: Muscle biopsy, mitochondrial DNA analysis, lactate/pyruvate levels
 5. Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease (CMT)
 Progressive weakness, muscle wasting (hands, arms, lower legs)
 Neuropathy with decreased reflexes, but sensation is often impaired
 Can be inherited, like your father’s symptoms
 Diagnosis: Genetic testing, nerve conduction studies

Diagnosis Task: My 75-year-old wife has had slow but increasing speech disorders with word-finding problems for almost 5 years in retrospect. She is also increasingly forgetful and can hardly manage everyday tasks. She forgets the names of our own children. In addition, she continues to withdraw and has orientation problems (frequent trips, for example to the supermarket, are no longer possible). She also had problems with depression in the past, which is why she took lithium until a year ago. Currently she does not take it regularly. We have no comparable case in our family. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Alzheimer's disease
 
  Model Output:1. Primary Progressive Aphasia (PPA) – Most Likely
 Slowly worsening speech problems (word-finding difficulty, grammar issues) as the first symptom
 Memory and orientation problems may appear later
 Subtype of frontotemporal dementia (FTD), specifically the language variant
 Diagnosis: Neuropsychological testing, MRI (shows frontotemporal atrophy)
 2. Alzheimer’s Disease (AD)
 Most common cause of progressive memory loss and disorientation
 Forgetting names of family members, difficulty managing everyday tasks
 Speech impairment can occur but usually later in disease
 Diagnosis: Cognitive testing (MMSE, MoCA), MRI (hippocampal atrophy), PET scan (if needed)
 3. Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD) – Behavioral Variant
 Social withdrawal, personality changes, apathy, disinhibition
 Can overlap with Primary Progressive Aphasia (language variant of FTD)
 Usually occurs earlier (50s-70s), progresses faster than Alzheimer’s
 Diagnosis: MRI (frontal and temporal lobe atrophy), neuropsychological tests
 4. Vascular Dementia
 Stepwise decline rather than gradual progression
 History of strokes or cardiovascular risk factors (high blood pressure, diabetes)
 MRI may show multiple infarcts or white matter changes
 Diagnosis: MRI (vascular lesions), cognitive tests
 5. Lithium-Induced Cognitive Dysfunction
 History of lithium use → long-term effects on cognition
 May worsen underlying neurodegenerative conditions
 Diagnosis: Check lithium levels, kidney function, MRI for brain atrophy**

Diagnosis Task: I am a 23 year old woman and I work as a bank employee. Besides a general lack of drive, I have weak muscles and am gaining weight (11 kg in 6 months). I gave birth to twins 10 months ago and they are currently in foster care. This is due to my depression, for which I am also in psychiatric treatment. I haven't gotten my menstruation back yet after delivery. During pregnancy I had high blood pressure and glucosuria with impaired glucose tolerance. Other than that, I do not suffer from any pre-existing conditions. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cushing's syndrome
 
  Model Output:1. Hypothyroidism (Most Likely)
 Weight gain, fatigue, muscle weakness, depression
 Irregular or absent menstruation (secondary amenorrhea)
 Can develop postpartum ("postpartum thyroiditis")
 Diagnosis: TSH, free T4, thyroid antibodies
 2. Postpartum Pituitary Insufficiency (Sheehan’s Syndrome)
 Can occur after childbirth, especially if blood loss or low blood pressure was present
 Symptoms: Fatigue, weight gain, muscle weakness, absent menstruation, low blood pressure
 Diagnosis: Hormone panel (TSH, cortisol, LH, FSH, prolactin, ACTH stimulation test)
 3. Cushing’s Syndrome (Possibly from Chronic Stress or Adrenal Dysfunction)
 Rapid weight gain (especially trunk/face), muscle weakness, fatigue, mood changes
 History of high blood pressure and glucose intolerance increases suspicion
 Diagnosis: 24-hour urine cortisol, dexamethasone suppression test
 4. Postpartum Depression with Atypical Features
 Depression, weight gain, fatigue
 Lack of energy and motivation beyond typical postpartum blues
 Diagnosis: Clinical evaluation, thyroid function to rule out medical causes
 5. Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)
 Weight gain, absent periods (amenorrhea), insulin resistance
 History of glucose intolerance during pregnancy
 Diagnosis: LH/FSH ratio, testosterone, pelvic ultrasound

Diagnosis Task: I am a 67 year old farmer and I have excruciating pain in my lower legs every night. It also regularly wakes me up. The pain is worse on the right than on the left and sometimes so severe that I can no longer stay in bed. When I sit up, it gets a little better. Since about 2 years I am generally limited in everyday life. When walking, I feel a burning pain in my right calf after a distance of 75 meters, which becomes so severe after 150 meters that I have to stop. The discomfort then subsides so that I can walk again after a short time. This is the reason why I don't leave my yard much anymore. I smoke regularly, about 60 pack-years, and occasionally drink alcohol. My father passed away from a heart attack and my mother from a stroke. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Peripheral artery disease 
 
  Model Output:1. Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD) with Critical Limb Ischemia (Most Likely)
 Burning pain in the calf triggered by walking ("claudication") → relieved by rest
 Night pain that improves when sitting up → suggests severe arterial insufficiency
 Risk factors: Heavy smoking, family history of heart attack/stroke
 Diagnosis:
 Ankle-brachial index (ABI) test (low ABI <0.9 confirms PAD)
 Doppler ultrasound (to assess blood flow)
 Angiography (if intervention needed)
 2. Spinal Stenosis (Neurogenic Claudication)
 Leg pain worsens with walking but relieved by sitting or bending forward
 Often accompanied by lower back pain
 Can mimic PAD but is neurological in origin
 Diagnosis: MRI of the lumbar spine
 3. Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy (If Diabetes Is Present or Undiagnosed)
 Burning leg pain, often at night
 Can coexist with PAD, especially in smokers
 Diagnosis: Nerve conduction studies, HbA1c to check for diabetes
 4. Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT, Less Likely)
 Severe, unilateral calf pain with swelling, warmth
 Unlikely as the main issue but could occur in PAD patients
 Diagnosis: D-dimer, Doppler ultrasound
 5. Buerger’s Disease (Thromboangiitis Obliterans) – If Younger (<50)
 Severe limb pain, usually in heavy smokers
 Rest pain, gangrene, ulcers (advanced cases)
 Diagnosis: Angiography (shows "corkscrew" vessels)

Diagnosis Task: I am a 63-year-old man and have been suffering from fatigue, headaches and aching limbs, repeated fevers up to 39 degrees, night sweats, loss of appetite and an unwanted weight loss of 5 kg since a cold 2 months ago. Suspecting polymyalgia rheumatica, I have been treated with glucocorticoids, but this has only slightly improved the symptoms. I have diabetes. In addition, I had a mechanical valve replacement 9 months ago. The reason was a high-grade aortic valve stenosis. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Subacute bacterial endocarditis
 
  Model Output:1. Infective Endocarditis (Most Likely, Medical Emergency)
 Fever, fatigue, night sweats, weight loss (classic signs)
 History of mechanical valve replacement (major risk factor)
 Can cause headaches and muscle pain due to systemic infection and embolic events
 Possible complications: Valve dysfunction, embolism (stroke, limb ischemia)
 Diagnosis:
 Blood cultures (before antibiotics)
 Echocardiography (transesophageal echo preferred)
 Inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR, WBC)
 2. Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA) – If Headache Is Prominent
 Headache, muscle pain, weight loss, night sweats
 Polymyalgia-like symptoms (but steroids should have helped significantly)
 Can coexist with polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR)
 Diagnosis: Temporal artery biopsy, ESR, CRP
 3. Chronic Prosthetic Valve Thrombosis (Less Likely but Important)
 Fatigue, malaise, weight loss if heart function is declining
 Can cause valve dysfunction leading to heart failure symptoms
 Diagnosis: Echocardiography
 4. Tuberculosis (TB) or Other Chronic Infection
 Chronic fever, weight loss, night sweats
 History of cold 2 months ago → could have led to reactivation
 Diagnosis: Chest X-ray, sputum culture, TB test (IGRA, PPD)**
 5. Hidden Malignancy (e.g., Lymphoma)
 Weight loss, night sweats, fatigue, recurrent fevers
 May be mistaken for an infection
 Diagnosis: PET scan, blood tests (LDH, CBC, tumor markers)**

disease_GT: Insulin-dependent Diabetes mellitus / Diabetes mellitus type 1
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: Our 18 year old daughter is quite dazed. She had severe abdominal pain last night and vomited several times. This morning she barely responded to being spoken to. For the past 4 days, our daughter has also been taking an antibiotic for a middle ear infection. In addition, she has been complaining of weakness in general for a few weeks, is drinking noticeably, is getting thinner and thinner, and is constantly going to the toilet. We already think that she has an eating disorder. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Insulin-dependent Diabetes mellitus / Diabetes mellitus type 1
 
  Model Output:1. Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) – Most Likely (Medical Emergency)
 Excessive thirst, weight loss, frequent urination, general weakness → suggests undiagnosed type 1 diabetes
 Severe abdominal pain and vomiting → common in DKA
 Dazed state (altered mental status) → indicates worsening metabolic state
 Preceding infection (ear infection) → common DKA trigger
 Diagnosis:
 Urgent blood glucose test (will be very high)
 Urine ketones and blood pH (acidosis)
 Electrolytes (especially potassium, which is often abnormal in DKA)
 ⚠ Immediate action: Take her to the emergency room now! DKA requires urgent IV fluids, insulin, and electrolyte management.
 2. Sepsis (Systemic Infection)
 If her ear infection has progressed, it could lead to systemic infection
 Dazed state, vomiting, abdominal pain, and weakness could indicate septic shock
 Diagnosis: Blood cultures, inflammatory markers (CRP, WBC, procalcitonin)
 3. Toxic Metabolic Encephalopathy (Electrolyte Disturbance or Severe Acidosis)
 Severe metabolic imbalances (caused by DKA, sepsis, or dehydration) can cause confusion and dazed behavior
 Diagnosis: Blood gas analysis, electrolytes
 4. Anorexia Nervosa with Dehydration and Electrolyte Imbalance
 Weight loss, excessive thirst, and weakness could be due to an eating disorder
 However, abdominal pain and vomiting suggest metabolic derangement rather than just caloric restriction
 Diagnosis: Electrolytes, kidney function, nutritional assessment
 5. Antibiotic-Induced Adverse Reaction (e.g., Allergic Reaction, Hepatic or Renal Dysfunction)
 Rare, but some antibiotics can cause metabolic imbalances, dehydration, or encephalopathy
 Diagnosis: Liver/kidney function tests

Diagnosis Task: I am a 27 year old college student suffering from loss of appetite, weight loss (8 kg from last year) and have been sluggish for months. Last week I also collapsed several times. My skin is browner than it used to be even though I'm not out in the sun anymore. I was actually a gymnast since I was 12 years old with training several times a week and competitions. However, I had to give that up because of the ailments. I don't have any pre-existing conditions, I don't take any medications and I don't drink much alcohol or smoke. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Addison's disease
 
  Model Output:Addison’s Disease (Primary Adrenal Insufficiency)
 
 Symptoms: Weight loss, fatigue, hyperpigmentation (especially in areas of friction), low blood pressure leading to collapses, salt cravings, and nausea.
 Cause: Autoimmune destruction of the adrenal glands leading to cortisol and aldosterone deficiency.
 Key Clue: The skin darkening (browning) without sun exposure is characteristic of increased ACTH levels due to adrenal failure.
 Chronic Hypothyroidism (with Possible Adrenal Insufficiency)
 
 Symptoms: Fatigue, sluggishness, weight loss (sometimes weight gain), cold intolerance, depression, and possible collapse if associated with adrenal insufficiency.
 Cause: Autoimmune thyroid disease (e.g., Hashimoto’s thyroiditis) or central hypothyroidism.
 Key Clue: Prolonged fatigue and metabolic slowdown.
 Chronic Infection (e.g., Tuberculosis, HIV, Endocarditis)
 
 Symptoms: Unexplained weight loss, fatigue, night sweats, fever, and possible fainting episodes.
