 Cause: Persistent infections causing systemic effects.
 Key Clue: Progressive weakness, loss of appetite, and chronic symptoms.
 Celiac Disease (Severe Malabsorption Variant)
 
 Symptoms: Weight loss, fatigue, possible nutritional deficiencies, gastrointestinal discomfort, and in some cases, neurological symptoms leading to collapses.
 Cause: Autoimmune reaction to gluten damaging the intestines.
 Key Clue: History of unexplained weight loss with fatigue, possibly worsened by gluten intake.
 Pheochromocytoma (Adrenal Tumor Producing Catecholamines)
 
 Symptoms: Episodic collapse, palpitations, sweating, high blood pressure, and weight loss.
 Cause: Tumor of the adrenal medulla secreting excessive catecholamines.
 Key Clue: Recurrent collapses, autonomic instability, and metabolic symptoms.

Diagnosis Task: My 9 year old son fell at school a week ago and hit his right knee. The wound is not healing properly and large bruises have developed on his hands and elbows. This is not the first time Max has developed large bruises. This happens again and again with slight bruises, e.g. on the forehead or like now on the knee. But I have that all the time, too, probably it runs in the family. But it has never been as bad as it is now. A year ago there was also a funny situation: I lifted him up while playing and turned him in a circle, and he dislocated both arms. Apart from that, Max is completely healthy, has no previous illnesses and takes no medication. He is otherwise a bright and lively boy. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome
 
  Model Output:1. Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (EDS), particularly the Classical or Hypermobile Type
 Symptoms: Easy bruising, fragile skin with poor wound healing, joint hypermobility, frequent joint dislocations, and a family history.
 Cause: Genetic disorder affecting collagen production, leading to weak connective tissue.
 Key Clue: The combination of joint dislocations and skin fragility suggests a connective tissue disorder like EDS.
 2. Von Willebrand Disease (VWD)
 Symptoms: Easy bruising, prolonged bleeding from wounds, nosebleeds, and sometimes excessive bleeding after surgery or dental work.
 Cause: A deficiency or dysfunction of von Willebrand factor, which helps blood clot.
 Key Clue: Bruising and wound healing problems, especially if there is a family history.
 3. Hemophilia (A or B, mild form)
 Symptoms: Unexplained bruising, prolonged bleeding after injuries, joint bleeds (hemarthrosis), and excessive bleeding after minor trauma.
 Cause: Deficiency of Factor VIII (Hemophilia A) or Factor IX (Hemophilia B), leading to impaired blood clotting.
 Key Clue: Repeated significant bruising with minor trauma, possibly undiagnosed mild hemophilia.
 4. Marfan Syndrome
 Symptoms: Hypermobile joints, long limbs, easy bruising, and sometimes heart or vision problems.
 Cause: A genetic disorder affecting fibrillin, which is important for connective tissue integrity.
 Key Clue: Joint hypermobility and easy bruising, though Marfan typically presents with long limbs and heart issues.
 5. Platelet Function Disorder (e.g., Glanzmann Thrombasthenia, Bernard-Soulier Syndrome)
 Symptoms: Easy bruising, frequent nosebleeds, prolonged bleeding from minor cuts, and sometimes mucosal bleeding.
 Cause: Platelets do not work properly, leading to clotting problems.
 Key Clue: Frequent bruising without major trauma and family history.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 52 year old female vet and have had unsteadiness when I walk for 18 months. In the last few weeks, there has been a continued worsening of my symptoms and I have recently fallen several times. In the meantime, I feel like I can no longer adequately control my legs. I also have recurring headaches, difficulty concentrating, and a new hearing loss. My husband thinks I am also becoming more forgetful and not speaking as clearly. I have arterial hypertension and Algerian asthma. At 17, I suffered some broken bones in a serious horseback riding accident. At the age of 29, a severe episode of headache occurred for the first time, after which an intracranial aneurysm had to be surgically treated. I take ramipril and sometimes salbutamol. I have no other allergies, but I smoke and occasionally consume alcohol. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Superficial siderosis of the CNS
 
  Model Output:Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus (NPH) – Your progressive gait disturbance, cognitive decline, and difficulty with speech suggest a form of chronic hydrocephalus, which can occur years after an initial brain insult such as your past aneurysm surgery. NPH typically presents with the classic triad: gait disturbance, cognitive impairment, and urinary incontinence (which you haven’t mentioned but may develop).
 
 Chronic Subdural Hematoma – Your history of falls and prior head trauma raises concern for a chronic subdural hematoma, which can develop gradually, causing worsening gait instability, headaches, cognitive impairment, and speech difficulties.
 
 Brainstem or Cerebellar Stroke (or TIA) – Your arterial hypertension and smoking history significantly increase your risk of cerebrovascular disease. A stroke affecting the brainstem or cerebellum could explain your balance issues, new hearing loss, and speech changes.
 
 Multiple Sclerosis (MS) or Other Demyelinating Disease – Given your progressive neurological symptoms, including gait ataxia, cognitive difficulties, and possible cranial nerve involvement, a demyelinating disorder such as MS should be considered. The new hearing loss and motor control issues could indicate brainstem involvement.
 
 Neurodegenerative Disorder (e.g., Atypical Parkinsonism, Spinocerebellar Ataxia, or Progressive Supranuclear Palsy) – While less likely given your acute worsening, chronic neurodegenerative diseases can present with progressive gait and cognitive issues, especially in your age group.

Diagnosis Task: My 14-year-old daughter, Johanna, has severe abdominal pain. In the morning, she went to school feeling slightly unwell; during the day, she developed very severe abdominal pain with nausea and has vomited and had diarrhea several times. In time with the onset of the abdominal pain, she noticed a metallic taste in her mouth. Johanna's father has developed severe edema in the affected areas after minor bruising. Otherwise, I can't think of any hereditary diseases. Johanna used to have mild to moderate swelling of the extremities as a child, which we always treated with cold water. Johanna's condition is getting worse and worse. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Hereditary angioedema
 
  Model Output:1. Hereditary Angioedema (HAE) – Most Likely
 Symptoms: Sudden severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, extremity swelling (previous history), and no response to typical allergy treatments (antihistamines or steroids).
 Cause: A genetic deficiency or dysfunction of C1 esterase inhibitor (C1-INH), leading to uncontrolled bradykinin release and swelling.
 Key Clues:
 Recurrent swelling episodes since childhood.
 Family history of unexplained swelling (father’s severe edema after minor trauma).
 No itching (ruling out allergic reactions).
 Severe abdominal attacks with worsening condition.
 Urgency: HIGH—HAE can cause life-threatening airway swelling. Seek emergency care immediately.
 2. Acute Porphyria (e.g., Acute Intermittent Porphyria, AIP)
 Symptoms: Severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, metallic taste, and neurological symptoms (confusion, seizures, paralysis in later stages).
 Cause: Deficiency of enzymes in the heme biosynthesis pathway, leading to toxic metabolite accumulation.
 Key Clues:
 Metallic taste at symptom onset.
 Progressive worsening without clear infection.
 Possible genetic predisposition.
 Urgency: HIGH—AIP attacks can be life-threatening due to neurological complications.
 3. Toxic Ingestion or Heavy Metal Poisoning (e.g., Lead, Arsenic)
 Symptoms: Abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, metallic taste, neurological symptoms, or changes in consciousness.
 Cause: Exposure to toxic metals from contaminated food, water, or accidental ingestion.
 Key Clues:
 Metallic taste (strong indicator of metal poisoning).
 Sudden onset with rapid worsening.
 Potential environmental exposure (e.g., old pipes, contaminated food, or accidental ingestion).
 Urgency: HIGH—Toxic ingestion can lead to organ damage if untreated.
 4. Autoimmune Disease (e.g., Systemic Lupus Erythematosus, SLE)
 Symptoms: Chronic, unexplained swelling, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea.
 Cause: Autoimmune attack on tissues, potentially affecting the gut (vasculitis).
 Key Clues:
 Family history of unexplained swelling.
 Unusual presentation of abdominal symptoms.
 Urgency: Moderate—Needs specialist evaluation but is less immediately life-threatening than HAE or porphyria.
 5. Severe Gastrointestinal Infection (e.g., Norovirus, Bacterial Toxin)
 Symptoms: Acute abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, nausea, and metallic taste (rare).
 Cause: Viral or bacterial infection leading to severe gut irritation.
 Key Clues:
 Diarrhea and vomiting are prominent.
 Potential food exposure (recent meals, contaminated water, school outbreak?).
 Urgency: Moderate to high—If dehydration or worsening symptoms occur, hospitalization may be necessary.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 21 year old woman and a student at the moment. For the repeated time, I now have fever up to 39.1 degrees, muscle pain and a big skin rash on both forearms, as well as I also have eye and stomach pain. The fever has been going on for a week. In the morning I vomited for the first time. Nobody else in my environment is sick. I have had weeks like this before. That's why I had to miss school more often as a child. However, the complaints were never investigated further. I have never traveled outside Europe. I have no previous illnesses. My mother suffers from renal insufficiency. I do not consume alcohol, nor do I smoke. When I was 8 years old I had similar symptoms and that is when my appendix was removed. However, in retrospect, that turns out to be unremarkable. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: TNF receptor associated periodic syndrome
 
  Model Output:Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF) – Your recurrent fever, muscle pain, rash, and abdominal pain suggest an autoinflammatory syndrome. FMF often presents in childhood and is more common in people of Mediterranean or Middle Eastern descent, but it can also affect others. The history of unnecessary appendix removal (due to misinterpreted abdominal pain) is a common clue.
 
 Still’s Disease (Systemic Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis or Adult-Onset Still’s Disease) – This condition is characterized by recurrent high fevers, muscle and joint pain, rash, and systemic inflammation. The salmon-colored rash on your forearms and recurrent nature of your symptoms strongly suggest this.
 
 Periodic Fever Syndrome (Autoinflammatory Syndrome, e.g., TRAPS or Hyper-IgD Syndrome) – Recurrent fever episodes with skin rashes, muscle pain, and gastrointestinal symptoms are hallmarks of autoinflammatory diseases. Since your symptoms have persisted since childhood, conditions like TNF receptor-associated periodic syndrome (TRAPS) or Hyper-IgD Syndrome (HIDS/Mevalonate Kinase Deficiency) should be considered.
 
 Lupus (Systemic Lupus Erythematosus, SLE) – Although less common in younger patients, lupus can cause recurrent fever, skin rash, joint/muscle pain, stomach pain, and systemic inflammation. The presence of a family history of kidney disease (your mother’s renal insufficiency) might be relevant if it were caused by lupus nephritis.
 
 Monogenic Autoinflammatory Disease (e.g., CAPS, PFAPA) – Since your symptoms started in childhood and are periodic, rare hereditary autoinflammatory syndromes such as Cryopyrin-Associated Periodic Syndromes (CAPS) or PFAPA (Periodic Fever, Aphthous Stomatitis, Pharyngitis, Adenitis) should also be considered.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 47-year-old male and have been suffering from high fever and muscle pain for a few days. I first thought it was a flu-like infection. Other members of my family also have similar symptoms. However, since this morning I have had swelling on my face, especially around my eyes. A fortnight ago I was at a barbecue and had diarrhea and severe abdominal pain for several days. There were wild boar sausages that a friend of mine, an Italian, brought from his home country. Otherwise, I am actually healthy. I do not take any medication, I am only allergic to penicillin. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Trichinosis or trichinellosis
 
  Model Output:1. Trichinellosis (Trichinosis) – Most Likely
 Symptoms: Fever, muscle pain, facial swelling (especially around the eyes), gastrointestinal symptoms (diarrhea, abdominal pain), and possibly headaches.
 Cause: Trichinella spiralis, a parasite transmitted through undercooked or raw pork or wild game (e.g., wild boar).
 Key Clues:
 Recent wild boar sausage consumption.
 Initial diarrhea followed by muscle pain and fever.
 Facial swelling (periorbital edema), which is characteristic of Trichinellosis.
 Urgency: High—Can be serious if untreated, potentially leading to heart or neurological complications.
 2. Brucellosis (Undulant Fever)
 Symptoms: Recurrent fever, muscle pain, fatigue, joint pain, sweating, and gastrointestinal upset.
 Cause: Brucella bacteria, transmitted through contaminated animal products (such as unpasteurized dairy or undercooked meat from wild animals).
 Key Clues:
 Exposure to wild boar meat.
 Prolonged fever and muscle pain.
 Possible occupational or foodborne exposure (your Italian friend might have brought non-commercially processed meat).
 Urgency: Moderate to High—Chronic complications can develop if untreated.
 3. Hantavirus Infection
 Symptoms: Fever, muscle pain, headache, abdominal discomfort, possible kidney involvement, and sometimes respiratory symptoms.
 Cause: Hantavirus, often contracted from contact with rodent droppings or urine (possible exposure outdoors or through contaminated food).
 Key Clues:
 High fever and muscle pain.
 Potential wild animal exposure at the barbecue site.
 Possible renal involvement in later stages.
 Urgency: High—Some forms can cause hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome or severe pulmonary disease.
 4. Trichostrongylosis (Parasitic Worm Infection)
 Symptoms: Gastrointestinal symptoms (diarrhea, nausea), weight loss, muscle pain, and fever.
 Cause: Parasitic worm infection from contaminated meat (such as wild game or raw/undercooked pork).
 Key Clues:
 Gastrointestinal symptoms preceding systemic illness.
 Potential exposure via wild boar sausages.
 Urgency: Moderate—Needs stool or blood tests for confirmation.
 5. Influenza or Viral Myositis (Unlikely but Possible)
 Symptoms: High fever, muscle pain, fatigue, and flu-like symptoms.
 Cause: Seasonal influenza or other viral infections.
 Key Clues:
 Family members with similar symptoms.
 No direct connection to wild boar, but possible concurrent infection.
 Urgency: Low to Moderate—If there are no severe complications, it is self-limiting.

disease_GT: Hantavirus hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: I am a 66 year old man and have severe feelings of illness with weakness, abdominal pain, loss of appetite, chills and aching limbs. I went to the doctor 4 days ago because I had a high fever overnight (38.8), chills, severe headache and aching limbs. Since it is fall, this was considered the flu. I was told to stay on bed rest, take ibuprofen and drink enough. I did all of that, however, the symptoms got much worse from yesterday to today. In addition, I have severe pain in my stomach, I am no longer hungry and my eyes are sensitive to light and constantly watering. Even if I think back, nothing special has happened. I winterized the garden 2 weeks ago, with everything that goes with it: cleaned the pond from dead plant remains and fetched firewood for the fireplace from the shed. On top of everything, today I have a dizzy feeling when I try to get up. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Hantavirus hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome
 
  Model Output:1. Leptospirosis
 Your exposure to pond water and dead plants is a red flag for Leptospira infection, which can be contracted from rodent-contaminated water or soil.
 Symptoms like fever, chills, headache, muscle pain, abdominal pain, conjunctival irritation, and weakness are classic for leptospirosis.
 Severe cases (Weil’s disease) can cause liver and kidney dysfunction, which may explain your loss of appetite and worsening weakness.
 2. Tularemia ("Rabbit Fever")
 Handling firewood or contaminated water puts you at risk for Francisella tularensis, which is spread via rodents, rabbits, or ticks.
 Symptoms include high fever, severe weakness, muscle aches, abdominal pain, and swollen lymph nodes.
 The flu-like illness that worsened with abdominal pain suggests a typhoidal form of tularemia.
 3. Hantavirus Infection
 You reported working in the shed—a known risk for inhaling rodent droppings or urine particles carrying hantavirus.
 Early symptoms mimic the flu (fever, muscle pain, weakness, and chills), but abdominal pain and worsening illness suggest progression to hantavirus pulmonary or renal syndrome.
 Dizziness could indicate low blood pressure due to kidney involvement.
 4. Severe Bacterial Peritonitis (e.g., Diverticulitis Complication or Sepsis)
 If you have a history of diverticulosis, your symptoms might indicate a ruptured diverticulum leading to peritonitis.
 Fever, worsening abdominal pain, chills, and weakness with loss of appetite suggest a severe intra-abdominal infection.
 Sepsis could explain your dizziness (low blood pressure).
 5. Listeriosis (Severe Foodborne Infection)
 If you ate contaminated food (soft cheese, unwashed vegetables, or processed meats) in the last few weeks, Listeria monocytogenes could be a culprit.
 Symptoms like fever, muscle pain, headache, abdominal pain, and worsening weakness suggest a possible severe listeriosis infection, which can lead to meningitis (light-sensitive eyes, dizziness, and worsening condition).

Diagnosis Task: I am a 70-year-old man and have been suffering from nausea and vomiting for a week. My wife noticed yellowing of the skin in the previous two days. In addition, I have lost about 7 kg in the previous month. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cholangicarcinoma
 
  Model Output:1. Pancreatic Cancer (Head of the Pancreas) – Most Likely
 Symptoms: Jaundice (due to bile duct obstruction), weight loss, nausea, vomiting, loss of appetite, and sometimes upper abdominal pain.
 Cause: A tumor in the head of the pancreas can block the bile duct, leading to jaundice and digestive symptoms.
 Key Clues:
 Progressive jaundice.
 Significant weight loss.
 No fever (suggesting non-infectious cause).
 Urgency: HIGH—Needs urgent imaging (CT or MRI of the abdomen).
 2. Cholangiocarcinoma (Bile Duct Cancer)
 Symptoms: Progressive jaundice, nausea, vomiting, weight loss, and possibly itching (due to bile buildup).
 Cause: Cancer of the bile ducts leading to bile obstruction and jaundice.
 Key Clues:
 Jaundice with no pain.
 Weight loss.
 Possible history of gallstones or liver disease.
 Urgency: HIGH—Requires urgent imaging and possible biopsy.
 3. Hepatocellular Carcinoma (Liver Cancer)
 Symptoms: Jaundice, nausea, vomiting, weight loss, and possibly abdominal swelling (ascites).
 Cause: Liver cancer, often associated with chronic liver disease (cirrhosis, hepatitis B/C, alcohol use).
 Key Clues:
 History of liver disease? (Hepatitis, alcohol use, cirrhosis).
 Unexplained weight loss and jaundice.
 Possible liver enlargement or mass.
 Urgency: HIGH—Needs liver ultrasound, CT, or MRI with AFP (alpha-fetoprotein) blood test.
 4. Obstructive Jaundice (From Gallstones or Strictures)
 Symptoms: Jaundice, nausea, vomiting, possible itching, and dark urine.
 Cause: Blocked bile ducts due to gallstones, inflammation, or scarring (benign or malignant).
 Key Clues:
 Intermittent symptoms with sudden worsening.
 Possible previous gallbladder issues.
 Urgency: MODERATE to HIGH—Needs urgent ultrasound or MRI (MRCP) to check bile ducts.
 5. End-Stage Liver Disease (Cirrhosis with Hepatic Failure)
 Symptoms: Jaundice, nausea, vomiting, weight loss, confusion (if hepatic encephalopathy develops), ascites (abdominal swelling).
 Cause: Liver failure due to cirrhosis (from alcohol, hepatitis, or fatty liver disease).
 Key Clues:
 History of liver disease, alcohol use, or viral hepatitis?.
 Jaundice and weight loss.
 Possible swelling (ascites, leg edema).
 Urgency: HIGH—Needs liver function tests and imaging.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 53 year old woman and have had problems for some time that I can no longer hold my bowel movements completely. In addition, when I have a bowel movement itself, there is always blood and something bulges, which I then have to push back again. I have had 3 vaginal births with two stitched perineal tears. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Rectal procidentia or rectal prolapse
 
  Model Output:1. Rectal Prolapse
 The bulging sensation that needs to be pushed back suggests rectal prolapse, where part of the rectum protrudes through the anus.
 Fecal incontinence occurs due to weakening of the anal sphincter and pelvic floor, especially after multiple vaginal births.
 Blood in the stool can result from irritation or ulceration of the prolapsed tissue.
 2. Hemorrhoids (Severe or Prolapsed Hemorrhoids)
 Prolapsed internal hemorrhoids can cause rectal bleeding, a bulging mass, and incontinence due to chronic irritation.
 Multiple vaginal births and perineal tears increase the risk of hemorrhoidal disease.
 Unlike rectal prolapse, hemorrhoids usually cause pain and bleeding rather than full fecal incontinence.
 3. Pelvic Organ Prolapse (Rectocele or Pelvic Floor Dysfunction)
 A rectocele occurs when the rectum bulges into the vaginal wall due to weakened pelvic floor muscles (common after multiple childbirths).
 This can lead to incomplete bowel emptying, difficulty controlling stools, and a bulging sensation.
 It often coexists with other pelvic prolapse conditions (e.g., uterine prolapse).
 4. Anal Sphincter Dysfunction (Obstetric Sphincter Injury or Anal Incontinence Syndrome)
 Vaginal deliveries, perineal tears, and previous stitching can weaken the anal sphincter, leading to fecal leakage and incontinence.
 Blood in the stool can result from mucosal irritation or small anal fissures.
 5. Colorectal Cancer or Advanced Polyps (Less Likely but Important to Rule Out)
 Persistent rectal bleeding, bulging tissue, and changes in bowel habits require ruling out a malignancy, especially at your age.
 Colon cancer screening (colonoscopy) is essential to rule out cancer or large polyps as a cause of rectal bleeding.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 30-year-old auto mechanic and have been experiencing increasing pain in my left shoulder. At first, it only occurred when I was working overhead. In the meantime, however, I have constant problems. In addition, I have numbness in my left forearm and hand. The left hand is also pale and feels cold. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome (TOS)
 
  Model Output:1. Thoracic Outlet Syndrome (TOS) – Most Likely
 Symptoms: Shoulder pain, numbness/tingling in the arm and hand, weakness, coldness, and pallor of the hand. Symptoms worsen with overhead activity.
 Cause: Compression of the brachial plexus, subclavian artery, or subclavian vein due to tight muscles, an extra cervical rib, or trauma.
 Key Clues:
 Symptoms worsen with overhead work (common in mechanics).
 Cold, pale hand suggests vascular involvement.
 Numbness and tingling suggest nerve compression.
 Urgency: Moderate to High—Needs vascular and nerve testing (Doppler ultrasound, EMG, MRI).
 2. Cervical Radiculopathy (Pinched Nerve in the Neck)
 Symptoms: Shoulder pain radiating down the arm, numbness/tingling in the forearm and hand, and sometimes weakness in the affected arm.
 Cause: Compressed nerve root in the cervical spine due to a herniated disc or osteoarthritis (common in mechanics due to repeated neck strain).
 Key Clues:
 Pain radiates from the neck/shoulder down the arm.
 Numbness in a nerve distribution pattern (likely C6 or C7 nerve root).
 Neck movements may trigger symptoms.
 Urgency: Moderate—Needs MRI of the cervical spine and possibly nerve conduction studies.
 3. Subclavian Steal Syndrome (Vascular Insufficiency)
 Symptoms: Shoulder pain, cold/pale hand, dizziness, weakness, and worsening symptoms with arm movement.
 Cause: Blockage in the subclavian artery, leading to reduced blood flow to the arm.
 Key Clues:
 Cold, pale hand with exertion.
 Dizziness or arm fatigue with activity.
 Blood pressure difference between arms (affected side is lower).
 Urgency: High—Needs vascular ultrasound or angiography.
 4. Rotator Cuff Injury (Tendon Tear or Impingement)
 Symptoms: Shoulder pain (especially with overhead work), weakness, and reduced range of motion.
 Cause: Chronic overuse or acute tear of the rotator cuff tendons.
 Key Clues:
 Pain with lifting the arm or reaching overhead.
 Weakness when raising the arm or rotating the shoulder.
 No numbness (if nerve compression is absent).
 Urgency: Moderate—Needs shoulder MRI or ultrasound.
 5. Brachial Plexus Injury or Compression

Diagnosis Task: I (40-year-old female) have increasing pain and tingling in the thumb, index finger and middle finger of my right hand. These occur mainly at night. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Carpal tunnel syndrome
 
  Model Output:1. Carpal Tunnel Syndrome (CTS) – Most Likely
 Pain, tingling, and numbness in the thumb, index, and middle fingers (median nerve distribution)
 Worse at night and may improve with shaking the hand ("flick sign")
 Caused by compression of the median nerve in the carpal tunnel (due to repetitive hand use, pregnancy, hypothyroidism, or wrist inflammation)
 2. Cervical Radiculopathy (C6/C7 Nerve Root Compression)
 Nerve root irritation in the neck (C6-C7 disc herniation or degeneration)
 Pain and tingling can extend from the neck down to the hand
 Often associated with neck stiffness or pain
 3. Thoracic Outlet Syndrome
 Compression of the brachial plexus or subclavian vessels
 Symptoms similar to CTS but can affect the whole arm
 Often worsens with arm elevation or prolonged posture (e.g., sleeping position, desk work)
 4. Diabetic Neuropathy (if applicable)
 More common if you have diabetes or prediabetes
 Symptoms are often bilateral but can start in one hand
 Gradual onset with tingling, burning, or numbness
 5. Ulnar Neuropathy at the Wrist or Elbow (Less Likely but Considered if Symptoms Extend to Ring Finger)

Diagnosis Task: I am a 51-year-old man and have been suffering from pain and itching at the anus for some time. Maybe they are hemorrhoids, because sometimes there is also blood on the toilet paper. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.

 Bright red blood on toilet paper is a typical sign
 Worsens with constipation, straining, prolonged sitting, or heavy lifting
 2. Anal Fissure
 Sharp pain during or after bowel movements, often with bright red blood
 Can cause itching as it heals
 Associated with constipation or trauma from hard stools
 3. Perianal Dermatitis (Skin Irritation)

 Can result from sweating, poor hygiene, excessive wiping, infections, or allergic reactions
 4. Anal Abscess or Fistula

 Often due to infected anal glands
 Fever may be present in severe cases

 Requires colonoscopy for confirmation

Because of the fever, she has also been taking daily 3-4 tablets of an antibiotic (cotrimoxazole) from my father's stock for the last few days. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Drug-induced agranulocytosis
 
  Model Output:Pneumonia (bacterial or viral, including COVID-19 or influenza-related pneumonia)
 Sepsis (possibly from a respiratory or urinary source)
 Acute Methotrexate Toxicity (due to infection or drug interaction with cotrimoxazole)
 Clostridioides difficile infection (antibiotic-associated colitis due to cotrimoxazole use)
 Acute kidney injury (due to dehydration, infection, or drug interactions)

disease_GT: Nodular Goiter with normal hormone production
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: I am a woman of 33 years. My thyroid gland has slowly increased in size over the past few years. I am more and more prone to nervousness and have gained 4 kg of weight in the past 2 years. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
