 Hypothyroidism (primary or secondary)
 Goiter due to iodine deficiency
 Subclinical or mild hyperthyroidism (e.g., early Graves' disease or toxic nodular goiter)
 Thyroid nodule(s) with possible hormonal imbalance

Diagnosis Task: I am a 23-year-old woman suffering from acute shortness of breath. Since the beginning of April, I have had attacks of shortness of breath, usually accompanied by cough and glassy, viscous sputum. At the same time, I have no fever, night sweats, or weight loss. Apart from hay fever, I have no previous illnesses. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Bronchial asthma
 
  Model Output:Bronchial asthma
 Allergic bronchitis
 Vocal cord dysfunction (VCD)
 Hyperventilation syndrome
 Eosinophilic bronchitis

Diagnosis Task: I am a 34 year old woman and have been fatigued and tired for months. I have also collapsed several times in the past few weeks. Otherwise I have never been ill and I do not take medication regularly. However, since a short time I have brownish hyperpigmentation, which rather does not come from the sun. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Chronic primary adrenal insufficiency
 
  Model Output:Addison’s Disease (Primary Adrenal Insufficiency) – Chronic fatigue, unexplained fainting (due to low blood pressure), and hyperpigmentation (due to high ACTH levels stimulating melanin production).
 Iron Deficiency Anemia – Fatigue, dizziness, and possible fainting due to low oxygen transport, though hyperpigmentation is uncommon.
 Chronic Liver Disease (e.g., Hemochromatosis) – Fatigue, hyperpigmentation (bronze skin), and fainting due to liver dysfunction and iron overload.
 Hypothyroidism – Persistent fatigue, weakness, and possible fainting due to slow metabolism and low blood pressure.
 Orthostatic Hypotension (e.g., due to Dysautonomia or Adrenal Insufficiency) – Frequent collapses upon standing, fatigue, and possible skin changes.

Diagnosis Task: My father is 78 years old and has been experiencing pain in his left lower abdomen for several days. He also complains of alternating constipation and diarrhea. He also has a fever of 38.7 degrees Celsius. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute colonic diverticulitis
 
  Model Output:Diverticulitis (with Possible Complications such as Abscess or Perforation)
 Colorectal Cancer (Especially with Bowel Habit Changes and Fever, Suggesting Obstruction or Infection)
 Infectious Colitis (e.g., Clostridioides difficile or Bacterial Gastroenteritis)
 Ischemic Colitis (Common in Older Adults with Vascular Disease)
 Inflammatory Bowel Disease (Less Likely at This Age but Possible, e.g., Ulcerative Colitis or Crohn’s Disease)

Diagnosis Task: I am a 36-year-old male and I am having increasing difficulty swallowing. I've had this for a while, both when swallowing solid foods and when drinking. Recently, I have sometimes had to regurgitate food. Nevertheless, I have not lost any weight and I have no other pain. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Achalasia
 
  Model Output:Achalasia – A rare disorder where the lower esophageal sphincter fails to relax properly, causing difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids. It often leads to food regurgitation without significant pain or weight loss.
 
 Esophageal stricture – A narrowing of the esophagus, often due to chronic acid reflux (GERD), prior injury, or scarring. This can make it progressively harder to swallow, especially solid foods.
 
 Esophageal motility disorder (e.g., diffuse esophageal spasm) – Abnormal muscle contractions in the esophagus that disrupt normal swallowing, affecting both solids and liquids.
 
 Eosinophilic esophagitis – A chronic immune-mediated condition where inflammation leads to difficulty swallowing and food getting stuck, often linked to allergies.
 
 Zenker’s diverticulum – A pouch that forms in the upper esophagus, leading to difficulty swallowing and food regurgitation. It typically affects older adults but can occur earlier.

Diagnosis Task: I (17 years old girl) have been suffering from high fever, sweating and joint pain for several days. It all started 10 days ago with a sore throat. In addition, my knee joints, ankles and wrists are swollen and red. I have a fever of 39.5 degrees Celsius and strange spots and nodules on my forearm. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute rheumatic fever
 
  Model Output:Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF) – Post-streptococcal immune reaction after a sore throat, causing fever, joint inflammation (migratory arthritis), and skin manifestations (erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules).
 
 Post-Streptococcal Reactive Arthritis (PSRA) – A non-migratory arthritis following a streptococcal infection, often affecting multiple joints without the cardiac involvement seen in ARF.
 
 Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) – Can present with fever, arthritis, and skin lesions (malar rash or other cutaneous manifestations), though the association with a preceding sore throat is less typical.
 
 Viral Arthritis (e.g., Parvovirus B19 or Epstein-Barr Virus) – Post-viral inflammatory response with fever, polyarthritis, and skin manifestations like petechiae or erythematous rashes.
 
 Septic Arthritis or Reactive Arthritis – Bacterial or post-infectious arthritis that can occur after a systemic infection, potentially leading to fever, joint swelling, and inflammatory skin lesions.

Diagnosis Task: I am a woman of 24 years. I have been having cramping pain in my lower right abdomen for weeks and recurrent non-bloody diarrhea, as well as a decrease in my body weight by 6 kg. I have already had my appendix and tonsils removed. I have no fever, but I have a fistula at my anus and swelling on my right knee joint. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Crohn disease
 
  Model Output:Crohn’s Disease – A chronic inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) that commonly affects the terminal ileum (lower right abdomen) and can lead to fistulas, perianal disease, weight loss, and extraintestinal manifestations such as arthritis.
 
 Reactive Arthritis (Reiter's Syndrome) – Can occur after gastrointestinal infections and present with a triad of arthritis (knee swelling), urethritis (may be mild or asymptomatic), and conjunctivitis. You don’t mention an eye or urinary infection, but your joint involvement suggests it as a possible secondary condition.
 
 Intestinal Tuberculosis – Although less common in developed countries, it can mimic Crohn’s disease with chronic diarrhea, weight loss, right lower abdominal pain, and perianal fistulas.
 
 Behçet’s Disease – A systemic vasculitis that can cause intestinal ulcers, recurrent diarrhea, perianal disease, and arthritis. Oral/genital ulcers would make this diagnosis more likely.
 
 Whipple’s Disease – A rare infection caused by Tropheryma whipplei, leading to chronic diarrhea, weight loss, joint involvement, and abdominal pain. Less likely but should be considered.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 40-year-old man and have been physically fatigued for about 6 months. In addition, I have a dry cough and night sweats. In addition, I have been suffering from a chronic cold with frequent nosebleeds and a very crusty nose for 9 months. For about 3 weeks now, I have also been suffering from swelling of the right knee joint, the left wrist and both ankles. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis
 
  Model Output:Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (Wegener’s Granulomatosis) – Chronic nasal crusting, nosebleeds, persistent cough, fatigue, night sweats, and multiple joint swelling due to systemic vasculitis affecting the respiratory tract and kidneys.
 Tuberculosis (TB) – Chronic fatigue, night sweats, dry cough, and joint involvement (Poncet’s disease – reactive arthritis in TB).
 Sarcoidosis – Fatigue, dry cough, night sweats, nasal involvement, and arthritis (Löfgren’s syndrome).
 Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA) – Chronic fatigue, joint swelling, and possibly extra-articular manifestations (lungs, nasal mucosa involvement due to vasculitis-like symptoms).
 Secondary Syphilis – Fatigue, night sweats, joint swelling, and chronic rhinitis with crusting (luetic rhinitis).

Diagnosis Task: I am a 21 year old woman with headache. If I were to describe it: existing for 5 hours, left-sided, of pulsating character with a moderate nausea. I have headaches more often, however, I have had similar symptoms 2 times a year since I was 17. Before the onset of the headache today I saw 20 minutes of wandering flashes of light. After about 30 minutes that was gone and the pain started. 1 tablet of aspirin only gave mild relief. The onset of visual and speech disturbance is a new dimension for me. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Migraine with aura
 
  Model Output:Migraine with aura – The combination of a unilateral pulsating headache, nausea, and visual disturbances (flashes of light) strongly suggests migraine with aura. The aura (temporary visual disturbances) typically precedes the headache and resolves before or as the pain starts.
 
 Migraine with brainstem aura (formerly basilar migraine) – If the speech disturbances were significant, this could be a subtype of migraine affecting the brainstem, sometimes causing more neurological symptoms like dizziness or coordination issues.
 
 Retinal migraine – A rare form of migraine that involves transient visual disturbances in one eye, which may precede or accompany headache episodes. It is important to differentiate this from more serious vascular conditions.
 
 Transient ischemic attack (TIA) – Though less likely in a young woman, a new-onset speech disturbance raises concern for a transient vascular event. However, the presence of a typical migraine history makes TIA less probable.
 
 Ophthalmic (or ocular) migraine – A condition where visual symptoms occur with or without headache. If your symptoms ever occur without headache, this might be a possibility.

Diagnosis Task: My 69-year-old husband has had memory problems for about 1 year. He has increasing difficulty in retaining new information for more than a few minutes, while he remembers things from earlier times well. Thus, he often correctly recounts his school days. On the other hand, 6 months earlier he did not find his car again after a shopping trip in the city center and had to ask the police to search for it. Otherwise, my husband behaves as he always has, is friendly and courteous in his dealings with me, family and friends. A distant relative who visited us recently did not notice anything about him at first, but my husband did not recognize her at first. There are actually no pre-existing conditions and medication or alcohol abuse does not apply. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Alzheimer disease
 
  Model Output:Alzheimer’s Disease – Progressive memory loss (especially short-term), difficulty retaining new information, spatial disorientation (losing car), while long-term memory remains intact in early stages.
 Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) – Memory problems beyond normal aging but without significant impairment in daily life; can progress to dementia.
 Vascular Dementia – Gradual memory loss with possible sudden worsening due to small strokes (if risk factors like hypertension or diabetes exist).
 Lewy Body Dementia (LBD) – Memory loss with possible visual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, and Parkinsonian features (though not mentioned yet).
 Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD, less likely given symptoms) – Personality changes and executive dysfunction, though memory loss is less prominent in early stages.

Diagnosis Task: My husband is 65 years old and has been suffering increasingly from memory loss for half a year. In addition, he is often sleepy during the day, can rarely concentrate and he has little desire for his hobbies. 3 years ago he had a slight stroke, but the symptoms are gone except for slight walking problems. It could now be another stroke. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Vascular dementia, vascular cognitive impairment
 
  Model Output:Vascular Dementia – Cognitive decline caused by past strokes or chronic vascular damage, often associated with memory problems, poor concentration, and daytime sleepiness.
 
 Alzheimer’s Disease – Progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by memory loss, apathy, and reduced interest in hobbies, though it may coexist with vascular dementia.
 
 Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) due to cerebrovascular disease – A transitional stage between normal cognition and dementia, often seen in individuals with a history of strokes.
 
 Depression (Late-Onset or Post-Stroke Depression) – Can mimic dementia with symptoms like low motivation, impaired concentration, and excessive daytime sleepiness.
 
 Another Stroke (Recurrent Stroke or Silent Stroke) – A new stroke could worsen cognitive function, increase daytime drowsiness, and contribute to mobility problems.

Diagnosis Task: My brother is a 44-year-old engineer and has been suffering from increasing movement abnormalities for 3 years. For example, there are always spontaneous movements in all 4 extremities, especially hands and feet. Sometimes there are also twitches in his face. My brother is also becoming increasingly depressed, but this could also be due to his unemployment. His marriage also broke up a year ago. In addition, his alcohol consumption is significantly increased. He does not admit to drinking much alcohol or to the strange gestures and movements that occur. Overall, he is more often irritable. When he argues, these striking movements occur more often. Also his speech sometimes seems a bit blurred. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Huntington disease
 
  Model Output:Huntington’s disease – A hereditary neurodegenerative disorder that causes progressive involuntary movements (chorea), psychiatric symptoms (irritability, depression), and cognitive decline. Symptoms often worsen with stress, and patients may be unaware of their abnormal movements (anosognosia).
 
 Alcohol-related neurological disorder (e.g., alcoholic cerebellar degeneration or hepatic encephalopathy) – Chronic alcohol abuse can lead to movement disorders, speech disturbances, and cognitive impairment due to brain damage or liver dysfunction.
 
 Wilson’s disease – A rare genetic disorder causing copper accumulation in the body, leading to movement abnormalities, psychiatric symptoms (such as irritability and depression), and sometimes speech disturbances. This condition often presents in younger adults.
 
 Dystonia or another movement disorder (e.g., tardive dyskinesia or Tourette’s syndrome) – Dystonic movements and spontaneous twitches in the face and limbs could indicate a primary movement disorder or a medication-induced condition (e.g., from antipsychotics).
 
 Functional (psychogenic) movement disorder – Stress-related or unconscious neurological symptoms, where movement abnormalities worsen with emotional distress, without an identifiable organic cause.

disease_GT: Myotonic dystrophy Type 2 (DM2), formerly proximal myotonic myopathy (PROMM)
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: I am a 37 year old woman and have had slightly increasing weakness in my shoulder and gluteal muscles for 10 years. I have also been suffering from muscle soreness for 5 years. In addition, my muscles are stiff especially in the winter months. However, except for these muscle problems, I am healthy. My close relatives do not have these problems. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Myotonic dystrophy Type 2 (DM2), formerly proximal myotonic myopathy (PROMM)
 
  Model Output:Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy (LGMD)
 Myotonic Dystrophy (Mild or Late-Onset Form)
 Metabolic Myopathy (e.g., McArdle Disease or Mitochondrial Myopathy)
 Hypothyroid Myopathy (Especially if Symptoms Worsen in Winter)
 Inflammatory Myopathy (e.g., Polymyositis or Dermatomyositis, Though Unlikely Without Skin Symptoms)

Diagnosis Task: I am a 34 year old male and have been having unilateral pain behind my right eye every night for weeks. This one wakes me up almost always at the same time, around 1:00 am. It feels like a knife behind the eye, so severe that the eye is watery and all red. When I get up and walking around in my appartment, it is better after about 1 hour. Eye and ENT doctors have not found anything. My dentist has also not been able to get any improvement with a root canal. I have also tried to take aspirin or diclofenac against the pain, but unfortunately neither brings anything. Apart from that, I am a smoker and have higher blood pressure more often. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cluster headache
 
  Model Output:1. Cluster Headache (Most Likely – "Suicide Headache")
 Excruciating, stabbing pain behind one eye ("knife-like")
 Attacks occur at the same time every night ("clock-like precision")
 Eye redness, tearing, nasal congestion on the affected side
 Pain lasts 15–180 minutes and is not relieved by standard painkillers
 Restlessness (patients often pace or move around)
 Diagnosis: Clinical (neurology exam, rule out secondary causes with MRI if needed)
 Treatment:
 Acute: 100% oxygen therapy, sumatriptan injection or nasal spray
 Preventive: Verapamil, corticosteroids, lithium, CGRP inhibitors
 2. Paroxysmal Hemicrania (Rare, but Similar to Cluster Headache)
 Severe unilateral eye pain, but shorter attacks (2–30 minutes)
 Occurs multiple times a day (more frequent than cluster headaches)
 Absolutely responsive to indomethacin (key diagnostic test)
 Diagnosis: Indomethacin trial
 3. Trigeminal Neuralgia (Atypical Variant)
 Short, electric shock-like pains on one side of the face
 Triggered by touch, eating, or talking
 Less likely due to your persistent, longer-lasting pain
 Diagnosis: MRI (to rule out vascular compression of the trigeminal nerve)
 4. SUNCT (Short-lasting Unilateral Neuralgiform Headache with Conjunctival Injection and Tearing)
 Similar to cluster headaches but with shorter, more frequent attacks
 Pain lasts 5–240 seconds and recurs up to hundreds of times a day
 Unresponsive to usual painkillers, responds to lamotrigine or other anti-seizure drugs
 Diagnosis: Clinical, MRI to rule out secondary causes
 5. Carotid Artery Dissection (If New, Severe, or Sudden)
 Severe, unilateral headache with possible Horner’s syndrome (drooping eyelid, small pupil)
 History of high blood pressure may increase risk
 Diagnosis: Urgent vascular imaging (MRI/MRA of the carotid arteries)

Diagnosis Task: I am a 55-year-old saleswoman and have been suffering from muscle pain for 8 weeks. In addition, I have had increasing weakness in my extremities for 2 weeks. I also find it increasingly difficult to swallow. Lately I have also had joint pains, but they are better now. I have always been healthy and do not take any medication. I also drink alcohol very responsibly, but smoke about 30 cigarettes a day. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Polymyositis
 
  Model Output:Polymyositis – An inflammatory muscle disease that causes progressive muscle weakness, particularly in the proximal muscles, as well as swallowing difficulties (dysphagia). The chronic nature and worsening weakness fit well with this diagnosis.
 
 Myasthenia gravis – An autoimmune neuromuscular disorder causing muscle weakness that worsens with use, often affecting swallowing and limb strength. It typically does not cause pain, but some cases can present with fatigue and discomfort.
 
 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) – A neurodegenerative disease affecting motor neurons, leading to progressive weakness, difficulty swallowing, and muscle wasting. However, muscle pain is less prominent early on.
 
 Dermatomyositis – Similar to polymyositis but often associated with skin changes. Smoking is a risk factor, and it may have a paraneoplastic association (linked to cancer).
 
 Paraneoplastic syndrome (e.g., Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome) – Muscle weakness, difficulty swallowing, and joint pain could be a sign of an underlying malignancy, particularly in a long-time smoker. Small-cell lung cancer is commonly associated with such syndromes.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 45-year-old woman and have been seeing double vision for a few days. Although only slight and are not always present, but still disturbing. Especially when driving a car. Then I usually just close my eyes. I also keep noticing a feeling of pressure behind my left eye. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Third cranial nerve (oculomotor nerve) palsy
 
  Model Output:Myasthenia Gravis – Intermittent double vision (diplopia) that worsens with fatigue, possible eye muscle weakness, and ptosis (drooping eyelid).
 Cranial Nerve Palsy (III, IV, or VI Nerve Dysfunction) – Pressure behind the eye and double vision due to nerve compression (caused by microvascular disease, aneurysm, or tumor).
 Orbital Mass or Tumor (e.g., Meningioma, Optic Nerve Tumor) – Persistent pressure behind the eye and progressive diplopia due to compression of ocular structures.
 Multiple Sclerosis (MS) – Intermittent diplopia, visual disturbances, and other neurological symptoms due to demyelination.
 Thyroid Eye Disease (Graves' Orbitopathy) – Pressure behind the eye, eye bulging (proptosis), and occasional double vision, linked to thyroid dysfunction.

Diagnosis Task: My 56-year-old wife had an epileptic seizure for the first time. Her hand twitched involuntarily, then the whole right arm shook. This happened while she was fully conscious. Somehow she has also changed a bit in the last weeks. She has been complaining of constant headaches for about 1 month, which she fights with many painkillers. Maybe the pills are to blame that she behaves so strangely. Somehow she also moves slower. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Glioblastoma
 
  Model Output:Brain Tumor (e.g., Glioma, Meningioma, or Metastasis) – Progressive headaches, personality changes, motor slowing, and focal seizures suggest possible mass effect or cortical irritation.
 
 Focal Epilepsy (due to Structural Brain Lesion, e.g., Stroke, Trauma, or Tumor) – A first-time focal seizure with motor symptoms (Jacksonian march) could be caused by an underlying brain lesion.
 
 Stroke or Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA) with Post-Stroke Epilepsy – A prior or ongoing cerebrovascular event could explain new-onset focal seizures, behavioral changes, and motor slowing.
 
 Toxic or Metabolic Encephalopathy (e.g., Medication Overuse, Liver/Kidney Dysfunction, Electrolyte Imbalance) – Excessive painkiller use (especially opioids or NSAIDs) could cause neurological symptoms, confusion, and behavioral changes.
 
 Neurodegenerative Disease (e.g., Early Parkinson’s or Frontotemporal Dementia with Epilepsy) – Subtle behavioral changes, slowed movements, and new-onset seizures could indicate an early neurodegenerative process.

disease_GT: Giant cell arteritis/temporal arteritis, Horton disease
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: I am 73 years old and female. I have had headaches for about 4 weeks. Since yesterday, however, I have had visual disturbances for the first time. Maybe the visual disturbances are side effects of the tablets. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Giant cell arteritis/temporal arteritis, Horton disease
 
  Model Output:Giant Cell Arteritis (Temporal Arteritis)
 
 Symptoms: Persistent headache (often in the temples), scalp tenderness, jaw pain while chewing, sudden vision loss or blurriness in one eye.
 Urgency: Medical emergency! Can cause permanent blindness if untreated.
 Diagnosis: Elevated ESR/CRP, temporal artery ultrasound/biopsy.
 Treatment: Immediate high-dose corticosteroids (prednisone).
 Stroke (Ischemic or Hemorrhagic)
 
 Symptoms: Sudden headache, visual changes (double vision, field loss, blurred vision), weakness, speech difficulty.
 Urgency: Medical emergency! Seek urgent care.
 Diagnosis: CT/MRI brain scan.
 Treatment: Depending on type—clot-busting drugs (if ischemic), blood pressure management (if hemorrhagic).
 Brain Tumor (Primary or Metastatic)
 
 Symptoms: Progressive headaches, vision problems, nausea, cognitive changes, balance issues.
 Diagnosis: MRI brain with contrast.
 Treatment: Surgery, radiation, chemotherapy (depending on type and location).
 Medication Side Effects (or Toxicity)
 
 Possible drugs: Blood pressure meds, anticoagulants, painkillers, steroids, or excessive NSAIDs (ibuprofen, aspirin, etc.).
 Symptoms: Blurred vision, headaches, dizziness, nausea.
 Diagnosis: Review medication history, possible blood tests.
 Treatment: Adjust or change medication.
 Intracranial Hypertension (Idiopathic or Secondary)
 
 Symptoms: Chronic headaches, worsening vision (papilledema), nausea, tinnitus.
 Causes: Can be related to menopause, medication use (e.g., steroids, vitamin A), or brain conditions.
 Diagnosis: Fundoscopy (optic nerve swelling), lumbar puncture (high CSF pressure).
 Treatment: Diuretics (acetazolamide), weight loss, shunt surgery (if severe).

Diagnosis Task: I am a 37 year old woman and have had a mild gait disorder for a few days. However, I also have problems with urination. And when I have to, I have to significantly more urgently. I have also missed a few drops in the past. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Neuromyelitis optica (spectrum disorders)
 
  Model Output:Multiple Sclerosis (MS)
 Cauda Equina Syndrome (Emergency!)
 Spinal Cord Compression (e.g., Herniated Disc, Tumor, or Myelitis)
 Neurogenic Bladder Due to Central Nervous System Disease (e.g., Early-Onset Parkinson’s, MSA)
 Transverse Myelitis (Autoimmune or Post-Viral Inflammation of the Spinal Cord)

Diagnosis Task: My 6-year-old son suddenly developed a fever of 39 to 40 degrees yesterday, which cannot be lowered by calf compresses and paracetamol. In addition, he has severe headaches and neck pain and has already vomited several times. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Bacterial meningitis
 
  Model Output:1. Bacterial Meningitis (Medical Emergency!)
 Most serious and life-threatening possibility!
 Symptoms: High fever, severe headache, stiff neck, vomiting, confusion, irritability, possible seizures.
 Urgency: Requires immediate medical attention!
 Diagnosis: Lumbar puncture (CSF analysis), blood tests, CT/MRI if needed.
 Treatment: Immediate IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin), steroids, hospitalization.
 2. Viral Meningitis
 Milder form of meningitis, but still serious.
 Symptoms: High fever, headache, stiff neck, nausea/vomiting, but less severe than bacterial meningitis.
 Diagnosis: Lumbar puncture (shows clear CSF with normal glucose, increased lymphocytes).
 Treatment: Supportive care (fluids, pain relief); no antibiotics needed.
 3. Influenza (Severe Flu with Neurological Symptoms)
 Influenza can cause severe fever, headaches, and vomiting.
 Symptoms: High fever, headache, body aches, sore throat, vomiting, extreme fatigue.
 Complications: Can lead to viral meningitis or encephalitis.
 Diagnosis: Clinical exam, flu test if needed.
 Treatment: Supportive care, antivirals (Tamiflu) if diagnosed early.
 4. Encephalitis (Brain Inflammation, Often Viral)
 Can be caused by herpes virus, enteroviruses, or post-viral immune reactions.
 Symptoms: Fever, severe headache, vomiting, altered consciousness, confusion, seizures.
 Diagnosis: MRI, lumbar puncture, viral PCR tests.
 Treatment: Acyclovir (if herpes suspected), supportive care, hospitalization.
 5. Severe Strep Throat (Streptococcal Pharyngitis with Complications)
 Strep throat can cause fever, headache, vomiting, and neck pain due to swollen lymph nodes.
 Symptoms: Sore throat, difficulty swallowing, fever, vomiting in children, sometimes rash (scarlet fever).
 Diagnosis: Rapid strep test, throat culture.
 Treatment: Antibiotics (Penicillin or Amoxicillin).

Diagnosis Task: My 3 year old daughter is convulsing like she is having an epileptic seizure. It lasted about 3 minutes. My daughter seems tired, but otherwise responds adequately. She appears to have a fever of 39.8 degrees Celsius. This is the first time we have seen anything like this in her. The fever keeps going up despite calf wraps and acetaminophen suppositories. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Febrile seizure
 
  Model Output:Febrile seizure – The most common cause of seizures in young children, typically occurring with high fever (above 38°C). They are usually brief, generalized, and resolve without long-term effects.
 
 Meningitis (bacterial or viral) – Fever and seizures can be signs of meningitis, especially if the child develops persistent lethargy, neck stiffness, or irritability. Urgent medical evaluation is needed to rule this out.
 
 Encephalitis – A viral or autoimmune inflammation of the brain that can cause seizures, fever, and altered consciousness. Unlike febrile seizures, encephalitis often leads to prolonged confusion or neurological deficits.
 
 Severe bacterial infection (e.g., pneumonia, urinary tract infection, sepsis) – High fever and seizures can be triggered by serious infections, particularly in young children. Signs of respiratory distress or decreased responsiveness would suggest this possibility.
 
 Electrolyte imbalance (e.g., hyponatremia, hypocalcemia, or hypoglycemia) – Fever-related dehydration or an underlying metabolic disorder can lead to convulsions, especially in young children.

Diagnosis Task: My 10-year-old boy has pain and swelling on his right knee. He does not have a fever. Last week he complained of joint and muscle pain, but always in different locations. We thought it might be growing pains. I also noticed an annular rash that spread and then subsided. There is an additional small redness and swelling on the earlobe. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Lyme disease
 
  Model Output:Lyme Disease (Lyme Borreliosis – Lyme Arthritis) – Knee swelling, migratory joint and muscle pain, annular rash (Erythema migrans), and redness on the earlobe (possible tick bite site).
 Post-Streptococcal Reactive Arthritis (PSRA) – Joint swelling and pain after a recent infection, but without the heart involvement of rheumatic fever.
 Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA, Oligoarticular Type) – Chronic knee swelling and joint pain, often without fever, but no clear infectious trigger.
 Erythema Multiforme (with Associated Arthritis) – Annular rash and transient joint pain, possibly triggered by an infection or medication.
 Borrelial Lymphocytoma – Painless reddish swelling on the earlobe (classic Lyme-related manifestation in children), often associated with Lyme arthritis.

Diagnosis Task: My 15-year-old daughter has been increasingly suffering from sore throat, difficulty swallowing, fatigue and exhaustion as well as fever between 39 and 40 degrees Celsius for several days. She has a feeling of pressure especially in the right upper abdomen. Yesterday she also had a fine- to medium-spotted skin rash, but it has now faded again. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Infectious mononucleosis
 
  Model Output:Infectious Mononucleosis (Epstein-Barr Virus, EBV) – Classic symptoms include sore throat, fatigue, high fever, and possible liver/spleen enlargement (causing right upper abdominal pressure), often with a transient rash.
 
 Streptococcal Pharyngitis (Scarlet Fever if Rash Present) – Streptococcus infection can cause fever, throat pain, difficulty swallowing, and a fine red rash that fades.
 
 Cytomegalovirus (CMV) Infection – Similar to mononucleosis, with prolonged fever, sore throat, fatigue, and potential liver involvement.
 
 Viral Exanthema (e.g., Measles or Rubella, though less likely with vaccination) – Viral infections can present with fever, fatigue, sore throat, and transient rash.
 
 Hepatitis (e.g., Viral Hepatitis A, B, or C) – Fatigue, fever, right upper quadrant pressure, and systemic symptoms could indicate liver involvement, especially with Epstein-Barr or CMV co-infection.

Diagnosis Task: My 10 year old daughter has had a cold and cough for a week. She was actually feeling better until she suddenly developed a high fever. In addition, she now complains of severe frontal headaches, which are particularly bad whenever she tilts her head forward. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute sinusitis and rhinosinusitis
 
  Model Output:Bacterial sinusitis (frontal sinusitis) – After a viral infection, bacterial superinfection of the sinuses can occur. Severe frontal headaches, especially worsening with head movement, suggest involvement of the frontal sinuses.
 
 Meningitis (bacterial or viral) – A serious condition where infection spreads to the meninges (brain coverings). Fever, severe headache, and worsening pain when tilting the head forward are concerning signs that require urgent evaluation.
 
 Complicated viral infection (e.g., influenza or adenovirus with secondary bacterial infection) – Some viruses can cause prolonged illness, and secondary bacterial infections (such as pneumonia) may lead to worsening fever.
 
 Brain abscess (rare but serious complication of sinusitis or ear infections) – If a sinus or ear infection spreads to the brain, it can cause persistent headaches, fever, and neurological symptoms. While rare, it should be considered in cases of severe headache with fever.
 
 Post-viral fatigue with secondary infection (e.g., pneumonia or streptococcal pharyngitis) – A child improving from a viral illness who suddenly worsens with high fever m

Diagnosis Task: My 14-year-old daughter has lost 16kg of weight within the last year. Her body weight is only 40kg at a height of 160cm. She started her diet because she was called a "fat boy" at school. Her friends all have model figures. Even now she still feels too fat. She keeps withdrawing and the girl no longer participates in meals together, preferring to prepare her own healthy low-calorie food (e.g., half an apple or 1 carrot for breakfast, 1 lean yogurt for lunch, salad for dinner). I suspect my daughter is secretly sticking her finger down her throat. She also exercises several times a day on the family exercise bike. It is no longer possible to have a conversation, everything the daughter understands as an attack on her healthy lifestyle. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Anorexia nervosa
 
  Model Output:Anorexia Nervosa (Restrictive Type or Binge-Purge Type)
 Bulimia Nervosa (if self-induced vomiting is confirmed)
 Atypical Eating Disorder (e.g., OSFED – Other Specified Feeding or Eating Disorder)
 Body Dysmorphic Disorder (BDD) with Eating-Related Focus
 Depression with Disordered Eating

Diagnosis Task: My 10-year-old son has upper abdominal pain, nausea and loss of appetite. He feels listless and tired, has a slight fever (38.4 degrees Celsius). In addition, he has yellow eyes. 3 weeks ago we were still in Turkey visiting relatives. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Hepatitis a
 
  Model Output:Hepatitis A – A common viral liver infection in regions with lower sanitation standards. Symptoms typically appear 2–6 weeks after exposure and include jaundice, fever, nausea, and fatigue.
 
 Hepatitis B – Another viral hepatitis that can be acquired through contact with infected bodily fluids or perinatal transmission. It can cause similar symptoms but may have a more prolonged course.
 
 Hepatitis E – Endemic in some regions, this virus spreads through contaminated water and food. Symptoms are similar to hepatitis A but can be more severe in certain cases.
 
 Leptospirosis – A bacterial infection that can be acquired from contaminated water or animals, sometimes leading to liver involvement, jaundice, fever, and malaise.
 
 Gallbladder disease (e.g., cholecystitis or gallstones) – While less common in children, gallbladder conditions can cause upper abdominal pain, nausea, jaundice, and fever.

Diagnosis Task: My 4-year-old daughter suffers increasingly from facial swelling in the last few days. In particular, the eyelids are really swollen shut in the morning after waking up. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Nephrotic syndrome
 
  Model Output:Nephrotic syndrome – A kidney disorder that causes excessive protein loss in the urine, leading to fluid retention and swelling, particularly around the eyes (periorbital edema), which is most noticeable in the morning.
 
 Allergic reaction (e.g., allergic conjunctivitis or angioedema) – Allergies can cause swelling of the face and eyelids, often triggered by environmental allergens, food, or insect bites. It may worsen in the morning if allergens are present in the bedding.
 
 Sinusitis or periorbital cellulitis – An infection in the sinuses or around the eyes can lead to swelling, particularly if one side is more affected. Periorbital cellulitis is more concerning if redness, warmth, or fever is present.
 
 Hypothyroidism (Congenital or Acquired) – A rare but possible cause, as hypothyroidism can lead to facial puffiness due to fluid retention, though other systemic symptoms (fatigue, slow growth) would typically be present.
 
 Viral infection (e.g., Epstein-Barr virus/mononucleosis or other viral illness) – Some viral infections can cause facial swelling due to inflammation, lymph node involvement, or mild kidney effects.

Diagnosis Task: Our 4-year-old son has been suffering from abdominal pain, fever (39 degrees Celsius) and vomiting since the night before (more than 12 hours). The fever does not go down despite calf compress and paracetamol. The stomach pain is getting worse. Our son is otherwise not sensitive at all and is hardly ever sick. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute appendicitis
 
  Model Output:Acute Appendicitis – Progressively worsening abdominal pain (often starting near the navel and moving to the lower right abdomen), high fever, vomiting, and worsening symptoms.
 Gastroenteritis (Severe Bacterial or Viral Infection) – Fever, vomiting, and abdominal pain, though typically diarrhea is also present.
 Intussusception – Severe intermittent abdominal pain, vomiting, possible “currant jelly” stools (blood and mucus), and worsening lethargy.
 Mesenteric Lymphadenitis – Fever and abdominal pain that mimics appendicitis but caused by viral or bacterial infection affecting lymph nodes in the abdomen.
 Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) or Pyelonephritis – Fever, vomiting, and abdominal pain, especially if associated with painful urination or foul-smelling urine.

Diagnosis Task: My 10-year-old son has had sudden visual disturbances for about 1 hour with eye flickering and numbness first in his right arm, then in his right leg. In addition, he speaks somewhat slurred. This has subsided, but instead he now complains of a severe left-sided headache with nausea. He finds bright light unpleasant. We have never noticed anything like this in him before. He used to have febrile convulsions as a toddler, but otherwise he grew up normally. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Migraine with aura
 
  Model Output:Hemiplegic Migraine – A rare form of migraine with aura, featuring transient neurological deficits (visual disturbances, numbness, speech impairment) followed by a severe headache, often one-sided, with nausea and photophobia.
 
 Migraine with Aura (Typical or Basilar-Type) – Visual symptoms, sensory disturbances, speech difficulties, and a subsequent severe headache suggest a classic migraine aura, though the motor symptoms are less common.
 
 Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA, though rare in children) – Sudden focal neurological symptoms that resolve could indicate a temporary vascular event, but this is much less common in children without risk factors.
 
 Focal Seizure with Postictal Headache – A non-convulsive seizure could cause transient sensory and speech deficits, followed by a headache resembling a postictal state. Febrile convulsions in childhood could indicate increased seizure susceptibility.
 
 Complex or Atypical Migraine (e.g., Alice in Wonderland Syndrome) – Some children experience atypical migraines with transient neurological symptoms, including distorted perception, focal deficits, and severe headaches.

disease_GT: Diabetes mellitus type one, insulindependent diabetes mellitus
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: My son is 8 years old, he has been having stomach pain and nausea for 2 days. Last night he vomited several times. He does not have diarrhea or fever. However, he is sleepy. The sleepiness along with grogginess has been going on for almost 2 weeks. He has also lost 3kg in that time, although he has been eating and drinking with appetite. Our son has to go to the toilet all the time and for 2 days he even wet the bed again. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Diabetes mellitus type one, insulindependent diabetes mellitus
 
  Model Output:1. Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (Diabetic Ketoacidosis - DKA)
 Most likely and urgent condition!
 Key symptoms: Weight loss, frequent urination, bedwetting, fatigue, nausea, vomiting, and drowsiness.
 DKA is a life-threatening emergency due to high blood sugar and dehydration. It requires urgent medical attention to check blood glucose, ketones, and acid-base balance.
 2. Addison’s Disease (Adrenal Insufficiency)
 Possible cause of fatigue, nausea, weight loss, and electrolyte imbalance.
 Symptoms: Stomach pain, salt cravings, low blood pressure, darkened skin, excessive tiredness.
 Requires blood tests for cortisol, ACTH, and electrolytes.
 3. Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) with Possible Kidney Involvement
 Symptoms: Bedwetting, frequent urination, nausea, and tiredness.
 Infection affecting the kidneys (pyelonephritis) could cause nausea, vomiting, and systemic illness.
 Needs urinalysis and urine culture.
 4. Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid)
 Can cause weight loss despite normal eating, frequent urination, fatigue, and sleep disturbances.
 Needs thyroid hormone tests (TSH, free T4, free T3).
 5. Diabetes Insipidus (Rare)
 Caused by impaired kidney function or hormone deficiency (not related to diabetes mellitus).
 Symptoms: Excessive thirst, frequent urination, dehydration, and fatigue.
 Needs blood sodium levels and urine concentration tests.

Diagnosis Task: My 3-year-old daughter has woken up from her sleep with a barking cough. She has difficulty breathing. Even if she doesn't exert herself, you can hear a whistling sound when she breathes in. I am afraid that my child might suffocate. She has no fever. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Croup syndrome
 
  Model Output:Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis) – The most common cause in young children. It is usually viral, causes a barking cough, hoarseness, and inspiratory stridor, and often worsens at night. It can cause respiratory distress but is typically manageable with cool air or steam and, in severe cases, steroids and nebulized epinephrine.
 
 Spasmodic Croup – A variant of croup without an infectious cause, often occurring suddenly at night. It has similar symptoms but no fever or cold-like symptoms.
 
 Foreign Body Aspiration – If the onset was sudden, consider this, especially if she was playing with small objects. It can cause stridor, wheezing, or choking.
 
 Epiglottitis (rare since HiB vaccination) – A life-threatening bacterial infection causing rapid onset of stridor, drooling, difficulty swallowing, and a muffled voice. Children typically sit forward in distress.
 
 Allergic Reaction (Anaphylaxis or Angioedema) – If associated with swelling, rash, or recent allergen exposure, it could be an allergic reaction causing airway swelling and stridor.

Diagnosis Task: My 12-month-old son has been crying continuously for 2 hours and simply cannot be calmed down. When I changed his diaper, I noticed that his scrotum is red and his right testicle is very swollen. He has no fever. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Testicular torsion
 
  Model Output:1. Testicular Torsion (Most Urgent)
 Cause: Twisting of the spermatic cord, cutting off blood supply to the testicle.
 Symptoms: Sudden, severe scrotal pain, swelling, redness, nausea/vomiting (though not always), and irritability in infants.
 Urgency: Medical emergency—requires immediate surgical intervention to save the testicle.
 2. Torsion of the Testicular or Epididymal Appendix
 Cause: Twisting of a small vestigial structure, not the entire testicle.
 Symptoms: Localized swelling and pain (less severe than full testicular torsion), often a "blue dot sign" near the upper testicle.
 Urgency: Requires urgent evaluation but is usually self-limiting and treated with pain relief.
 3. Incarcerated Inguinal Hernia
 Cause: A loop of intestine becomes trapped in the inguinal canal, causing testicular swelling.
 Symptoms: Firm, non-reducible mass in the groin or scrotum, crying due to pain, possible vomiting.
 Urgency: Urgent condition—risk of strangulation of bowel; requires immediate surgical evaluation.
 4. Acute Epididymitis or Orchitis
 Cause: Inflammation of the epididymis (often due to infections, though rare in infants).
 Symptoms: Scrotal pain, swelling, redness, possible fever.
 Urgency: Requires medical evaluation but is not typically an emergency unless severe.
 5. Idiopathic Scrotal Edema
 Cause: Benign, self-limiting condition of unknown cause.
 Symptoms: Painless or mildly painful scrotal swelling and redness without systemic symptoms.
 Urgency: Typically not an emergency, but must be differentiated from serious conditions.
 Next Steps
 Since testicular torsion is a medical emergency, you should seek immediate medical attention (Emergency Room/Urgent Pediatric Urology Evaluation). If torsion is confirmed, surgery must be performed within 4–6 hours to save the testicle.

Diagnosis Task: My 5-month-old son and I have only been living in Germany for a few months. We are originally from Bulgaria. My son has had an increasingly severe cough for a few weeks now. He is almost constantly tired and listless, drinks badly and vomits when he coughs. At night he is often soaked with sweat. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Tuberculosis
 
  Model Output:Based on your son's symptoms—persistent worsening cough, fatigue, poor drinking, vomiting with coughing, night sweats, and listlessness—several possible diagnoses should be considered:
 
 Pertussis (Whooping Cough) – A prolonged cough illness in infants that can cause severe coughing fits, vomiting after coughing, and exhaustion. It is particularly dangerous in young babies.
 
 Bronchiolitis (RSV or other viral causes) – Common in infants, causing coughing, difficulty feeding, wheezing, and fatigue.
 
 Tuberculosis (TB) – Given the prolonged cough, night sweats, and listlessness, TB should be considered, especially if there has been any contact with TB cases.
 
 Pneumonia (Bacterial or Viral) – Can cause a worsening cough, difficulty feeding, and listlessness, potentially leading to serious complications if untreated.
 
 Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) with Aspiration – Can lead to chronic coughing, vomiting after feeds, and poor weight gain.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 55-year-old woman. Unfortunately, I have not been able to sleep through the night for about 2 years. I am very tired during the day and have hardly been able to concentrate on my work at the office in recent months. I have been to several general practioneers who have prescribed me various sleeping pills and tranquillizers. However, after taking them for a longer time, they have made things worse rather than better. The whole situation is such a burden that I recently went to a psychiatrist who diagnosed me with “depressive syndrome” and started me on pills again. But even that has made things worse rather than better. In the meantime, I no longer sleep in the same bed as my husband. He feels disturbed by the strange, sweeping leg movements I make. I wake up more and more often at night. Then I feel a tingling sensation in my legs. If I massage them, it gets better, but as the night progresses, I become increasingly restless and have to walk around the room. I have noticed that this urge to move always occurs when I sit for long periods, especially towards the evening. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Restless legs syndrome
 
  Model Output:Restless Legs Syndrome (RLS) – A neurological disorder characterized by an irresistible urge to move the legs, often accompanied by tingling or discomfort, especially during rest or in the evening. Movement provides temporary relief. RLS can worsen with certain medications, including some antidepressants and sleeping pills.
 
 Periodic Limb Movement Disorder (PLMD) – Involuntary, repetitive limb movements (often in the legs) that occur during sleep, which can disturb sleep quality and lead to daytime fatigue. It often coexists with RLS.
 
 Chronic Insomnia Disorder – Persistent difficulty falling asleep or staying asleep despite adequate sleep opportunities, leading to daytime impairment. This can be linked to stress, psychiatric conditions like depression, or medical disorders like RLS.
 
 Depression with Sleep Disturbance – Depression often leads to difficulty sleeping, early morning awakenings, and non-restorative sleep. However, given your leg symptoms, it may not be the primary cause of your sleep issues.
 
 Iron Deficiency (with or without Anemia) – Low iron levels can contribute to RLS and sleep disturbances. Checking your ferritin level is important, as iron supplementation can sometimes improve RLS symptoms.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 22-year-old woman. For at least 2 years, I have noticed an increasing clumsiness when walking. As I have had pronounced hollow feet since I was a child - I inherited them from my mother - which is why I have been wearing special shoes for a long time. But even with these, I feel increasingly unsteady. Unfortunately, I can't bend or stretch my toes. But I think I can feel physical touch on my body normally. I work as a waitress. This means I often have pain in my shoulder, but no other complaints. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Hereditary motor and sensory neuropathies
 
  Model Output:Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease (CMT) – A hereditary peripheral neuropathy that often causes pes cavus, progressive weakness in the lower limbs, difficulty with balance, and reduced muscle function in the feet and legs. It typically affects both motor and sensory nerves.
 
 Hereditary Spastic Paraplegia (HSP) – A genetic disorder that leads to progressive stiffness and weakness in the legs, which can cause walking difficulties. Some forms of HSP can also lead to foot deformities like pes cavus.
 
 Friedreich’s Ataxia – A hereditary neurodegenerative disease that can cause progressive gait instability, loss of coordination, muscle weakness, and sometimes pes cavus. It often starts in young adulthood and can also affect reflexes.
 
 Spinal Cord Disease (e.g., Syringomyelia or a Spinal Cord Tumor) – A spinal cord lesion could lead to motor dysfunction, difficulty walking, and muscle weakness while potentially sparing sensation early on.
 
 Adult Onset Motor Neuron Disease (Mild ALS Variant or Kennedy's Disease) – Less likely, but motor neuron diseases can cause progressive weakness in the lower limbs, which might initially be misinterpreted as clumsiness.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 37-year-old man. I have noticed a deterioration in my walking for a few months now. I can't say exactly when it started. I also have increasing pain in my neck and both shoulders. Recently, I have been injuring my arms more frequently, but didn't even realize it at first. For example, I've burned myself several times on the stove when touching hot pots. But I did not realize it right away. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Syringomyelia
 
  Model Output:Cervical Myelopathy (e.g., from Cervical Spondylotic Myelopathy or Herniated Disc) – Compression of the spinal cord in the neck can cause gait disturbances, neck/shoulder pain, weakness, and sensory deficits (including decreased pain perception in the arms). It is often progressive.
 
 Syringomyelia – A fluid-filled cyst (syrinx) in the spinal cord, often at the cervical level, can cause progressive weakness, gait instability, and loss of pain/temperature sensation in a "cape-like" distribution over the shoulders and arms (leading to unnoticed burns or injuries).
 
 Multiple Sclerosis (MS) – MS can cause progressive neurological deficits, including gait disturbance, sensory loss, and chronic pain, though it often involves relapses and remissions.
 
 Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) – ALS can present with progressive muscle weakness, gait instability, neck/shoulder pain, and spasticity. Sensory loss is not typical, but patients may feel weak and clumsy in their arms.
 
 Peripheral Neuropathy (e.g., Diabetic or Alcoholic Neuropathy) – Damage to peripheral nerves can lead to progressive gait impairment, pain, numbness, and reduced pain sensation in the hands/arms, increasing the risk of unnoticed injuries.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 30-year-old woman. I am currently in hospital because I had my appendix removed two days ago. I now have pain in my right hand, where the cannula is inserted, and in my right forearm. My right hand is swollen, reddened and sensitive around the cannula. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Thrombophlebitis
 
 Thrombophlebitis
 
  Model Output:1. Phlebitis (Superficial Thrombophlebitis)
 Cause: Inflammation of the vein due to irritation from the IV cannula or infused fluids.
 Symptoms: Localized redness, pain, swelling, warmth along the vein.
 Urgency: Common and usually not serious, but IV should be removed, and the area should be monitored.
 2. Cannula-Associated Infection (Cellulitis or Abscess)
 Cause: Bacterial infection at the IV site.
 Symptoms: Increasing pain, swelling, redness, warmth, possible pus or fever.
 Urgency: Requires prompt medical attention; may need antibiotics or drainage if abscess forms.
 3. Extravasation Injury
 Cause: Leakage of IV fluids or medications into surrounding tissues.
 Symptoms: Swelling, pain, redness, and possible skin damage depending on the medication.
 Urgency: Requires assessment, especially if vesicant drugs were infused.
 4. Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) of the Upper Limb
 Cause: Blood clot in a deep vein due to IV catheter use.
 Symptoms: Swelling, pain, redness, and sometimes a feeling of heaviness in the arm.
 Urgency: Requires immediate evaluation, ultrasound, and possibly anticoagulation.
 5. Allergic Reaction to IV Materials or Fluids
 Cause: Hypersensitivity reaction to IV dressings, tape, or medications.
 Symptoms: Localized redness, itching, swelling, and possible rash.
 Urgency: Usually mild, but if severe, IV should be stopped.
 Next Steps
 Inform hospital staff immediately. The IV should likely be removed and replaced at another site.
 A doctor should assess for infection or thrombosis. Ultrasound may be needed to check for DVT.
 If infection is suspected, antibiotics may be required.
 If extravasation of a damaging drug occurred, treatment depends on the substance.

disease_GT: Sudeck's atrophy (Complex regional pain syndrome type 1)
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: I am a 60-year-old woman. I have been suffering from severe pain in my right forearm and right hand for about a week. I have a bluish swelling on my forearm. It hurts when I touch it and my whole arm feels warm. I broke my wrist about 2 months ago. The cast was removed 1 week ago. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Sudeck's atrophy (Complex regional pain syndrome type 1)
 
  Model Output:Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) of the Upper Limb – A blood clot in a deep vein, which can develop after immobilization (such as wearing a cast). Symptoms include swelling, pain, warmth, and a bluish discoloration.
 
 Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS, formerly Reflex Sympathetic Dystrophy) – A chronic pain condition that can develop after trauma like a fracture. Symptoms include severe pain, swelling, color changes, and temperature differences in the affected limb.
 
 Post-Traumatic Hematoma – A collection of blood under the skin or in the muscle due to trauma, which can cause swelling, pain, and discoloration.
 
 Cellulitis or Soft Tissue Infection – An infection of the skin or deeper tissues, which can present with warmth, swelling, pain, and redness. If the swelling worsens or you develop fever, it may indicate a serious infection.
 
 Recurrent Fracture or Delayed Bone Healing – If the wrist was not fully healed, a new injury or strain could cause further damage, leading to swelling and pain.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 22-year-old man. I injured my left forearm while gardening. The wound was very small, so I just put a plaster on it. I have had headaches and back pain for a few days now and feel increasingly restless. Noises and strong sunlight cause the muscles in my face to tense up, which I can't control. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Tetanus
 
  Model Output:Tetanus (Clostridium tetani infection) – Your symptoms (muscle stiffness, headaches, restlessness, and sensitivity to stimuli like noise and light) strongly suggest early signs of tetanus. The bacteria enter through wounds (even small ones) and produce toxins that cause muscle spasms and rigidity. This is a medical emergency requiring immediate treatment.
 
 Localized or Systemic Infection (Cellulitis or Sepsis) – If the wound became infected, systemic symptoms like headache, muscle aches, and restlessness could indicate an advancing infection. If the infection spreads, it can lead to fever and sepsis.
 
 Dystonia or Other Neurological Disorder – While less likely given the acute onset, muscle contractions in the face could indicate an underlying neurological condition. However, this would not typically follow a wound.
 
 Meningitis (Bacterial or Viral) – Stiffness, headaches, and light sensitivity could suggest meningitis. However, fever and neck stiffness would usually be present.
 
 Acute Stress Reaction or Anxiety Disorder – If an infection or tetanus is ruled out, severe stress or anxiety could manifest with muscle tension and sensitivity to stimuli, but it would not explain the progression of symptoms after a wound.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 47-year-old man and work as a long-distance driver. However, I am constantly tired and sometimes fall asleep during the day while working. On two occasions I narrowly escaped a traffic accident. If I sleep at night, I don't feel refreshed afterwards. My wife often complains that I snore very loudly. I suffer from high cholesterol and high blood pressure. I am 176 cm tall and weigh 102 kg. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Obstructive sleep apnea
 
  Model Output:Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA) – The most likely cause. OSA leads to repeated airway obstruction during sleep, resulting in poor sleep quality, excessive daytime sleepiness, loud snoring, and increased risk of cardiovascular disease. Your symptoms strongly align with OSA, which is common in overweight individuals with high blood pressure.
 
 Chronic Sleep Deprivation – Long-distance drivers often have irregular sleep schedules, leading to cumulative sleep debt. However, given your non-refreshing sleep and snoring, OSA is still the primary concern.
 
 Idiopathic Hypersomnia – A condition causing excessive daytime sleepiness despite seemingly adequate sleep, though it usually does not involve snoring.
 
 Narcolepsy – Less likely but possible, especially if you experience sudden sleep attacks or muscle weakness triggered by emotions (cataplexy). However, snoring is not a typical symptom.
 
 Hypothyroidism – An underactive thyroid can cause fatigue, weight gain, and daytime sleepiness. A thyroid function test (TSH, free T4) could help rule this out.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 48-year-old man. I have had persistent pulling pain in my upper abdomen for a few days. The pain radiates into my back like a belt. I have also been feeling a little nauseous for several weeks whenever I have eaten fatty foods. Then I also have diarrhea. My stool looks gray and shiny. In the past 6 months I have unintentionally lost 4 kg. I have been drinking 1 bottle of vodka and 8 bottles of beer a day for many years. I have no previous illnesses, but I never go to the doctor. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Chornic pancreatitis
 
  Model Output:Chronic Pancreatitis – Long-term alcohol abuse is the most common cause. Symptoms include persistent epigastric pain radiating to the back (belt-like), steatorrhea (fatty, gray stools), nausea with fatty foods, and weight loss due to malabsorption.
 
 Pancreatic Cancer – Alcoholism increases the risk. Symptoms include progressive upper abdominal pain radiating to the back, weight loss, steatorrhea, and digestive problems. Cancer in the head of the pancreas can also cause jaundice (yellowing of skin/eyes) if it blocks the bile duct.
 
 Alcoholic Hepatitis or Liver Cirrhosis – Long-term heavy alcohol use leads to liver damage, malabsorption, nausea, and weight loss. Advanced liver disease can cause portal hypertension, ascites, and liver failure. Fatty stools (steatorrhea) can also occur due to reduced bile production.
 
 Peptic Ulcer Disease (Possibly Perforated) – Chronic alcohol use increases the risk of stomach or duodenal ulcers, which can cause epigastric pain, nausea, and weight loss. If an ulcer perforates, pain can be severe and radiate to the back.
 
 Gallbladder Disease (Chronic Cholecystitis or Biliary Obstruction) – Alcohol does not directly cause gallbladder disease, but bile duct obstruction from gallstones or tumors can lead to fatty food intolerance, steatorrhea, and weight loss. Chronic gallbladder inflammation can mimic pancreatitis.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 19-year-old woman. My lips, eyelids, tongue and throat are suddenly swollen. I find it harder and harder to breathe in. When I breathe in, it makes a whistling sound. I have had hives for years. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Angioedema Histamine-mediated
 
  Model Output:1. Anaphylaxis (Most Urgent)
 Cause: Severe allergic reaction to food, insect stings, medication, or other allergens.
 Symptoms: Rapid swelling of the throat, difficulty breathing, wheezing or stridor, low blood pressure, dizziness, and potentially loss of consciousness.
 Urgency: Medical emergency—requires immediate epinephrine injection (EpiPen) and emergency medical care. Call an ambulance immediately.
 2. Hereditary or Acquired Angioedema (HAE or AAE)
 Cause: Deficiency or dysfunction of C1-esterase inhibitor, leading to recurrent episodes of deep swelling, often without an obvious trigger.
 Symptoms: Swelling of lips, eyelids, tongue, and airway, often without itching or hives. Can cause life-threatening airway obstruction.
 Urgency: Emergency situation—requires specialized treatment (e.g., C1-inhibitor concentrate, icatibant, or fresh frozen plasma in emergencies).
 3. Idiopathic Angioedema
 Cause: Unknown cause of recurrent angioedema, possibly linked to mast cell activation.
 Symptoms: Similar to allergic angioedema but may not be linked to specific allergens. Can be life-threatening if airway is involved.
 Urgency: Emergency situation if airway swelling is present—treatment with epinephrine, antihistamines, and corticosteroids.
 4. Mast Cell Activation Syndrome (MCAS)
 Cause: Excessive mast cell activation leading to anaphylaxis-like symptoms.
 Symptoms: Swelling, flushing, wheezing, hives, and low blood pressure, often triggered by stress, temperature changes, or food.
 Urgency: Can be life-threatening—managed similarly to anaphylaxis.
 5. Allergic Angioedema
 Cause: Release of histamine due to an allergic reaction, similar to anaphylaxis but sometimes milder.
 Symptoms: Rapid swelling of soft tissues, often with hives and itching.
 Urgency: If airway is affected, this is an emergency.

Diagnosis Task: I gave birth to my child 3 days ago. I was discharged the day after the birth. I now have pain in my lower abdomen and a persistent fever >38°C, which continues to rise in the evening. I also have heavy, foul-smelling discharge. I feel very bad and have a headache. The birth took a very long time. My waters had already broken 2 days earlier. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Endomyometritis
 
  Model Output:Endometritis (Postpartum Uterine Infection) – A bacterial infection of the uterine lining, especially common after prolonged labor or early rupture of membranes. Symptoms include fever, abdominal pain, foul-smelling discharge, and general malaise.
 
 Puerperal Sepsis (Severe Postpartum Infection) – A systemic infection that can develop from untreated endometritis or other postpartum infections. Symptoms include high fever, chills, headache, rapid heartbeat, and worsening general condition.
 
 Retained Products of Conception (RPOC) – If placental or fetal tissue remains in the uterus, it can cause persistent bleeding, infection, and fever.
 
 Pelvic Abscess or Peritonitis – If an infection spreads beyond the uterus, it can lead to an abscess in the pelvis or even peritonitis, causing severe pain and worsening fever.
 
 Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) or Pyelonephritis – Postpartum UTIs are common, especially after catheter use during labor. Symptoms include fever, abdominal or back pain, painful urination, and malaise.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 30-year-old woman. I have a large swelling on my right labia. It causes me unbearable pain and a feeling of tightness when I sit or walk. I can move the swelling, but when I press on it, it hurts a lot. The skin on the area is very red and feels very warm. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Bartholinitis
 
  Model Output:Bartholin’s Gland Abscess – This is the most probable diagnosis. A blocked Bartholin’s gland duct can lead to fluid buildup, infection, and abscess formation. It causes intense pain, redness, warmth, and swelling near the vaginal opening. Abscesses often require drainage and antibiotics.
 
 Bartholin’s Cyst (Infected or Inflamed) – If the Bartholin’s gland is blocked but not fully infected, it may form a cyst, which can later become an abscess. A cyst alone is usually less painful than an abscess but can still cause discomfort and swelling.
 
 Infected Sebaceous Cyst – A sebaceous gland blockage can lead to a cyst that becomes infected, swollen, and tender. It can be mistaken for a Bartholin’s gland abscess but occurs in different locations on the vulva.

 3. **Seizure:** Seizures can cause a variety of symptoms, including visual disturbances, numbness, weakness, and speech problems. They can be triggered by various factors, including fever, stress, and lack of sleep. Your son's history of febrile seizures makes this a possibility.
 
 4. **Hemiplegic Migraine:** This is a rare type of migraine that can cause weakness on one side of the body, along with other migraine symptoms. It can sometimes mimic a stroke.
 
 5. **Cerebral Venous Sinus Thrombosis (CVST):** This is a rare condition that occurs when a blood clot forms in one of the veins that drain blood from the brain. It can cause a variety of symptoms, including headache, visual disturbances, seizures, and weakness.
 
 **Additional Considerations:**
 
 * **Age:** Your son's age is a factor in the likelihood of certain diagnoses. For example, TIAs are less common in children than in adults.
 * **Medical History:** Your son's history of febrile seizures is relevant, as it may suggest a predisposition to neurological events.
 * **Symptom Progression:** The specific sequence of symptoms (visual disturbances, followed by numbness, then headache) can provide clues to the diagnosis.
 
 **Recommendations:**
 
 * **Seek immediate medical attention:** Given the potential seriousness of the symptoms, it is crucial to take your son to the nearest emergency room or call for an ambulance immediately.
 * **Document the event:** Write down all the details of the event, including the time of onset, the specific symptoms, their duration, and any other relevant information. This will be helpful for the medical professionals evaluating your son.
 * **Follow-up with a neurologist:** After the immediate medical evaluation, it is important to schedule a follow-up appointment with a neurologist for further evaluation and management.
 
 Please remember that this is just a list of possible diagnoses, and a medical professional should be consulted for a proper diagnosis and treatment plan.

disease_GT: Diabetes mellitus type one, insulindependent diabetes mellitus
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: My son is 8 years old, he has been having stomach pain and nausea for 2 days. Last night he vomited several times. He does not have diarrhea or fever. However, he is sleepy. The sleepiness along with grogginess has been going on for almost 2 weeks. He has also lost 3kg in that time, although he has been eating and drinking with appetite. Our son has to go to the toilet all the time and for 2 days he even wet the bed again. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Diabetes mellitus type one, insulindependent diabetes mellitus
 
  Model Output:This is for informational purposes only. For medical advice or diagnosis, consult a professional.
 
 I understand that you're concerned about your son's symptoms. It's important to seek professional medical advice for a proper diagnosis and treatment plan. However, based on the information you provided, here are some possible diagnoses that a doctor might consider:
 
 1. **Urinary Tract Infection (UTI):** UTIs can cause stomach pain, nausea, vomiting, frequent urination, and bedwetting. They are more common in girls but can also occur in boys.
 
 2. **Diabetes:** Diabetes can cause increased thirst and urination, weight loss despite normal eating, fatigue, and stomach pain.
 
 3. **Gastrointestinal Issues:** Several gastrointestinal issues can cause stomach pain, nausea, vomiting, and weight loss. These include:
  * **Gastroenteritis (stomach flu):** This viral infection can cause vomiting, nausea, stomach pain, and fatigue.
  * **Food intolerance or allergy:** This can cause stomach pain, nausea, vomiting, and fatigue.
  * **Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD):** This chronic condition can cause stomach pain, nausea, vomiting, weight loss, and fatigue.
 
 4. **Anxiety or Stress:** Anxiety or stress can sometimes manifest as physical symptoms like stomach pain, nausea, and fatigue.
 
 5. **Other Medical Conditions:** There are other medical conditions that can cause similar symptoms, such as kidney problems or neurological issues.
 
 **It is crucial to take your son to a doctor as soon as possible for a proper diagnosis and treatment plan.** The doctor will likely ask about your son's medical history, perform a physical exam, and may order some tests, such as a urine test, blood test, or stool test.
 
 Here are some additional things to keep in mind:
 
 * **Keep a record of your son's symptoms:** This will help the doctor make a diagnosis.
 * **Make sure your son stays hydrated:** Dehydration can be a serious complication of vomiting and nausea.
 * **Offer your son bland foods:** Bland foods like crackers, toast, and rice may be easier on his stomach.
 
 I hope this information is helpful. Please remember that I am not a medical professional, and this is not a substitute for professional medical advice.

Diagnosis Task: My 3-year-old daughter has woken up from her sleep with a barking cough. She has difficulty breathing. Even if she doesn't exert herself, you can hear a whistling sound when she breathes in. I am afraid that my child might suffocate. She has no fever. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Croup syndrome
 
  Model Output:Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis): This is the most common cause of barking cough and stridor in young children, especially in the fall and winter months. It's usually caused by a viral infection that inflames the upper airways (larynx and trachea). The barking cough and stridor are classic signs. While fever can be present, it's often low-grade or absent, especially at the onset.
 
 Spasmodic Croup: This is a variant of croup that comes on suddenly, often at night, and can be triggered by allergies or irritants. The symptoms are similar to croup (barking cough, stridor) but may resolve more quickly. Fever is usually absent in spasmodic croup.
 
 Foreign Body Aspiration: Although less likely if there was no known choking incident, it's always a consideration in young children. If your daughter inhaled a small object, it could be lodged in her airway and causing obstruction, leading to stridor and breathing difficulty. The cough might not always be barking in this case, but it's important to rule out.
 
 Bacterial Tracheitis: This is a bacterial infection of the trachea (windpipe). It can sometimes mimic croup initially but is often more severe and can progress rapidly. While fever is often present in bacterial tracheitis, it's not always high initially. It can cause significant airway obstruction and stridor.
 
 Anaphylaxis or Severe Allergic Reaction: While less typical with a barking cough, a severe allergic reaction to something inhaled or ingested could cause swelling in the upper airway, leading to stridor and breathing difficulty. This is less likely if there are no other signs of allergy like hives, rash, or swelling of the face/lips, but it's still a possibility, especially with sudden onset respiratory distress.

Diagnosis Task: My 12-month-old son has been crying continuously for 2 hours and simply cannot be calmed down. When I changed his diaper, I noticed that his scrotum is red and his right testicle is very swollen. He has no fever. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Testicular torsion
 
  Model Output:Testicular Torsion: This is a surgical emergency that occurs when the testicle twists on the spermatic cord, cutting off blood supply. It causes sudden, severe pain, swelling, and redness of the scrotum and testicle. In infants, it can be harder to diagnose because they cannot verbalize the pain, and inconsolable crying is a key sign. This is a time-sensitive condition requiring urgent surgery to save the testicle.
 
 Incarcerated or Strangulated Inguinal Hernia: An inguinal hernia is when a part of the intestine or other abdominal tissue protrudes through the abdominal wall into the groin. If the hernia becomes trapped (incarcerated) or its blood supply is cut off (strangulated), it can cause severe pain, swelling, redness, and inconsolable crying in infants. This is also a surgical emergency.
 
 Hydrocele: This is a collection of fluid around the testicle. While hydroceles are common in newborns and infants and often resolve on their own, a sudden increase in size or a tense hydrocele could cause discomfort and swelling. However, a hydrocele alone is less likely to cause inconsolable crying for two hours unless it has become very tense and uncomfortable or something else is going on.
 
 Epididymitis or Orchitis: These are infections of the epididymis (a tube at the back of the testicle that stores and carries sperm) or the testicle itself. While less common in infants than in older children and adults, they can occur. They can cause pain, swelling, and redness. While fever is often present in infections, it's not always the case, especially in the early stages.
 
 Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (HSP): This is a type of vasculitis (inflammation of blood vessels) that can sometimes affect the scrotum and testicles, causing swelling and pain. HSP usually presents with other symptoms like a characteristic rash (purpura), abdominal pain, and joint pain, but in some cases, scrotal involvement can be the initial or prominent symptom.

Diagnosis Task: My 5-month-old son and I have only been living in Germany for a few months. We are originally from Bulgaria. My son has had an increasingly severe cough for a few weeks now. He is almost constantly tired and listless, drinks badly and vomits when he coughs. At night he is often soaked with sweat. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Tuberculosis
 
  Model Output:Pertussis (Whooping Cough): This is a highly contagious bacterial infection of the respiratory tract and can be very serious, especially in infants. The cough in pertussis often starts like a common cold but then progresses to severe coughing fits. These fits can be so intense that they lead to vomiting, and they can be exhausting, causing tiredness and poor feeding. Night sweats can also occur. While the characteristic "whooping" sound might not always be present in very young infants, the prolonged and worsening cough, vomiting with coughing, and tiredness are strong indicators. Given the severity and duration of the cough (weeks), pertussis is a very important consideration, especially in an infant.
 
 Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) Bronchiolitis: RSV is a common viral infection that can cause bronchiolitis (inflammation of the small airways in the lungs), particularly in infants. Symptoms can include cough, wheezing, difficulty breathing, poor feeding, tiredness, and vomiting after coughing fits. Night sweats are less directly associated with RSV but can be present in viral illnesses. Bronchiolitis is very common in infants and often peaks in severity around this age.
 
 Pneumonia (Viral or Bacterial): Pneumonia is an infection of the lungs. It can be caused by viruses or bacteria. Symptoms in infants can include cough, fever (though not always high or present), rapid breathing, difficulty breathing, poor feeding, vomiting, tiredness, and sometimes night sweats. The prolonged and worsening cough, along with other systemic symptoms like tiredness and poor feeding, makes pneumonia a serious possibility.
 
 Tuberculosis (TB): While less common in infants in Germany compared to some other parts of the world, TB is still a possibility, especially if there are risk factors or if you have recently moved from a region with higher TB prevalence. Symptoms of TB in infants can be non-specific and include persistent cough, failure to thrive (poor weight gain and feeding), tiredness, night sweats, and sometimes fever. Mentioning your recent move from Bulgaria to your doctor is important, as they will consider this factor in their assessment.
 
 Cystic Fibrosis (CF): Cystic fibrosis is a genetic condition that affects the lungs and digestive system. While CF often presents with digestive issues and poor weight gain, respiratory symptoms like chronic cough, recurrent lung infections, and sometimes night sweats can also be prominent, even in infancy. While less likely to present suddenly at 5 months with these symptoms alone, it is something doctors might consider in the differential diagnosis of persistent respiratory issues in infants, especially if the symptoms are chronic and recurrent.
