 Systemic Inflammatory Disease (e.g., Sarcoidosis or Vasculitis): Conditions like sarcoidosis or other forms of vasculitis can cause systemic symptoms, including fever, fatigue, and weight loss. These conditions often require a thorough workup to diagnose.

disease_GT: Insulin-dependent Diabetes mellitus / Diabetes mellitus type 1
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: Our 18 year old daughter is quite dazed. She had severe abdominal pain last night and vomited several times. This morning she barely responded to being spoken to. For the past 4 days, our daughter has also been taking an antibiotic for a middle ear infection. In addition, she has been complaining of weakness in general for a few weeks, is drinking noticeably, is getting thinner and thinner, and is constantly going to the toilet. We already think that she has an eating disorder. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Insulin-dependent Diabetes mellitus / Diabetes mellitus type 1
 
  Model Output:Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA): This is a life-threatening complication of diabetes, often triggered by infection or illness. Symptoms include severe abdominal pain, vomiting, confusion, excessive thirst, frequent urination, weight loss, and weakness. The antibiotic use for her ear infection could have masked or exacerbated underlying diabetes.
 
 Eating Disorder with Electrolyte Imbalance: While eating disorders can cause weakness, weight loss, and behavioral changes, the acute symptoms (e.g., abdominal pain, vomiting, and altered mental status) suggest a more urgent medical issue, such as severe electrolyte imbalance or dehydration.
 
 Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) or Pyelonephritis: A severe UTI or kidney infection can cause abdominal pain, vomiting, confusion, and frequent urination. If left untreated, it can lead to sepsis, which may explain her altered mental state.
 
 Acute Pancreatitis: This condition can cause severe abdominal pain, vomiting, and confusion. It can be triggered by infections, medications, or underlying metabolic disorders. However, it is less likely given her other symptoms.
 
 Adrenal Crisis (e.g., Addisonian Crisis): If your daughter has an underlying adrenal insufficiency (e.g., Addison’s disease), an infection or stress could trigger an adrenal crisis, leading to vomiting, abdominal pain, confusion, and weakness. This is rare but serious.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 27 year old college student suffering from loss of appetite, weight loss (8 kg from last year) and have been sluggish for months. Last week I also collapsed several times. My skin is browner than it used to be even though I'm not out in the sun anymore. I was actually a gymnast since I was 12 years old with training several times a week and competitions. However, I had to give that up because of the ailments. I don't have any pre-existing conditions, I don't take any medications and I don't drink much alcohol or smoke. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Addison's disease
 
  Model Output:Addison’s Disease (Primary Adrenal Insufficiency): This condition occurs when the adrenal glands fail to produce sufficient hormones, particularly cortisol and aldosterone. Symptoms include fatigue, weight loss, loss of appetite, hyperpigmentation (darkening of the skin), and episodes of low blood pressure (which could explain the collapses). Addison’s disease is a serious but treatable condition.
 
 Hyperthyroidism (e.g., Graves’ Disease): While hyperthyroidism typically causes weight loss, increased appetite, and hyperactivity, some individuals may experience fatigue and weakness. Skin changes and episodes of collapse could be related to associated cardiovascular symptoms.
 
 Chronic Infection (e.g., Tuberculosis or HIV): Chronic infections can cause weight loss, fatigue, and loss of appetite. Tuberculosis, in particular, can also lead to adrenal insufficiency (secondary Addison’s disease), which could explain the skin darkening and collapses.
 
 Malignancy (e.g., Lymphoma or Other Cancers): Certain cancers can cause systemic symptoms such as weight loss, fatigue, and loss of appetite. Skin changes and collapses could be related to anemia or other complications.
 
 Anorexia Nervosa or Other Eating Disorders: While eating disorders often involve intentional weight loss and food restriction, they can also lead to fatigue, hormonal imbalances, and skin changes. However, the skin darkening and collapses are less typical of eating disorders alone.

Diagnosis Task: My 9 year old son fell at school a week ago and hit his right knee. The wound is not healing properly and large bruises have developed on his hands and elbows. This is not the first time Max has developed large bruises. This happens again and again with slight bruises, e.g. on the forehead or like now on the knee. But I have that all the time, too, probably it runs in the family. But it has never been as bad as it is now. A year ago there was also a funny situation: I lifted him up while playing and turned him in a circle, and he dislocated both arms. Apart from that, Max is completely healthy, has no previous illnesses and takes no medication. He is otherwise a bright and lively boy. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome
 
  Model Output:Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (EDS), particularly Hypermobile Type
 
 EDS is a group of connective tissue disorders that can cause joint hypermobility, easy bruising, and fragile skin that may heal poorly. The hypermobile type is the most common and often runs in families.
 
 Von Willebrand Disease (VWD)
 
 This is a common inherited bleeding disorder that can cause easy bruising, prolonged bleeding, and poor wound healing. It is caused by a deficiency or dysfunction of von Willebrand factor, a protein important for blood clotting.
 
 Hemophilia (Mild Form)
 
 Hemophilia is a genetic disorder that impairs the body’s ability to clot blood, leading to easy bruising and prolonged bleeding. A mild form might not be diagnosed until later in life, especially if symptoms are subtle.
 
 Platelet Function Disorders
 
 Conditions like Glanzmann’s thrombasthenia or other platelet function disorders can cause easy bruising and bleeding due to problems with platelet function, even if platelet counts are normal.
 
 Vitamin K Deficiency or Other Nutritional Deficiencies
 
 Vitamin K is essential for blood clotting, and a deficiency can lead to easy bruising and poor wound healing. Other deficiencies (e.g., vitamin C or iron) could also contribute to these symptoms.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 52 year old female vet and have had unsteadiness when I walk for 18 months. In the last few weeks, there has been a continued worsening of my symptoms and I have recently fallen several times. In the meantime, I feel like I can no longer adequately control my legs. I also have recurring headaches, difficulty concentrating, and a new hearing loss. My husband thinks I am also becoming more forgetful and not speaking as clearly. I have arterial hypertension and Algerian asthma. At 17, I suffered some broken bones in a serious horseback riding accident. At the age of 29, a severe episode of headache occurred for the first time, after which an intracranial aneurysm had to be surgically treated. I take ramipril and sometimes salbutamol. I have no other allergies, but I smoke and occasionally consume alcohol. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Superficial siderosis of the CNS
 
  Model Output:Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus (NPH)
 
 NPH is characterized by a triad of symptoms: unsteady gait (difficulty walking), cognitive decline (forgetfulness, difficulty concentrating), and urinary incontinence (though you haven’t mentioned this). It is caused by an abnormal buildup of cerebrospinal fluid in the brain and can worsen over time.
 
 Cerebellar Degeneration or Ataxia
 
 Damage or degeneration of the cerebellum, which controls coordination and balance, could explain your unsteadiness, falls, and difficulty controlling your legs. This could be due to conditions like multiple system atrophy (MSA), alcohol-related cerebellar degeneration, or other causes.
 
 Vascular Cognitive Impairment or Multi-Infarct Dementia
 
 Given your history of arterial hypertension and a surgically treated intracranial aneurysm, small vessel disease or recurrent mini-strokes (transient ischemic attacks) could lead to cognitive decline, gait disturbances, and other neurological symptoms.
 
 Chronic Subdural Hematoma
 
 A history of head trauma (e.g., from your horseback riding accident or recent falls) could lead to a slow bleed in the brain, causing symptoms like headaches, unsteadiness, cognitive changes, and hearing loss. This is especially concerning given your history of intracranial aneurysm.
 
 Progressive Supranuclear Palsy (PSP)
 
 PSP is a rare neurodegenerative disorder that can cause balance problems, frequent falls, cognitive decline, and speech difficulties. It often progresses over time and can mimic other conditions like Parkinson’s disease.

Diagnosis Task: My 14-year-old daughter, Johanna, has severe abdominal pain. In the morning, she went to school feeling slightly unwell; during the day, she developed very severe abdominal pain with nausea and has vomited and had diarrhea several times. In time with the onset of the abdominal pain, she noticed a metallic taste in her mouth. Johanna's father has developed severe edema in the affected areas after minor bruising. Otherwise, I can't think of any hereditary diseases. Johanna used to have mild to moderate swelling of the extremities as a child, which we always treated with cold water. Johanna's condition is getting worse and worse. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Hereditary angioedema
 
  Model Output:Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP): This is a rare genetic disorder that affects the production of heme, a component of hemoglobin. Symptoms include severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and neurological symptoms like a metallic taste. AIP can also cause swelling and sensitivity to trauma, which aligns with her father’s history of edema after bruising.
 
 Heavy Metal Poisoning (e.g., Lead or Mercury): Exposure to heavy metals can cause abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and a metallic taste. However, this would require a history of exposure, which you haven’t mentioned.
 
 Acute Pancreatitis: This condition can cause severe abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. However, it is less likely to explain the metallic taste or the family history of swelling.
 
 Hereditary Angioedema (HAE): This is a genetic disorder that causes recurrent episodes of severe swelling (edema) in various parts of the body, including the extremities and gastrointestinal tract. Abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting can occur during attacks. The family history of swelling after bruising could be relevant.
 
 Gastroenteritis (e.g., Viral or Bacterial Infection): While common, gastroenteritis typically does not cause a metallic taste or explain the family history of swelling. However, it could cause the acute symptoms of abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 21 year old woman and a student at the moment. For the repeated time, I now have fever up to 39.1 degrees, muscle pain and a big skin rash on both forearms, as well as I also have eye and stomach pain. The fever has been going on for a week. In the morning I vomited for the first time. Nobody else in my environment is sick. I have had weeks like this before. That's why I had to miss school more often as a child. However, the complaints were never investigated further. I have never traveled outside Europe. I have no previous illnesses. My mother suffers from renal insufficiency. I do not consume alcohol, nor do I smoke. When I was 8 years old I had similar symptoms and that is when my appendix was removed. However, in retrospect, that turns out to be unremarkable. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: TNF receptor associated periodic syndrome
 
  Model Output:Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)
 
 Lupus is an autoimmune disease that can cause recurrent fevers, skin rashes (often on sun-exposed areas like the forearms), muscle pain, eye inflammation, and gastrointestinal symptoms. It can also have periods of flare-ups and remissions, which might explain your recurrent episodes.
 
 Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF)
 
 FMF is a genetic autoinflammatory disorder that causes recurrent episodes of fever, abdominal pain, muscle pain, and rash. Although it is more common in people of Mediterranean descent, it can occur in other populations. It often starts in childhood and can be mistaken for appendicitis.
 
 Periodic Fever Syndromes (e.g., TRAPS or PFAPA)
 
 These are a group of autoinflammatory disorders characterized by recurrent fevers, abdominal pain, muscle pain, and rash. TRAPS (Tumor Necrosis Factor Receptor-Associated Periodic Syndrome) and PFAPA (Periodic Fever, Aphthous Stomatitis, Pharyngitis, Adenitis) are examples that can present with similar symptoms.
 
 Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD), such as Crohn's Disease or Ulcerative Colitis
 
 IBD can cause recurrent abdominal pain, fever, and systemic symptoms like muscle pain and rash. Eye pain (uveitis) is also a known extraintestinal manifestation of IBD.
 
 Vasculitis (e.g., Henoch-Schönlein Purpura or Polyarteritis Nodosa)
 
 Vasculitis involves inflammation of blood vessels and can cause fever, skin rash (often purpuric or raised), abdominal pain, and muscle pain. Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (HSP) is more common in children but can recur in adulthood.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 47-year-old male and have been suffering from high fever and muscle pain for a few days. I first thought it was a flu-like infection. Other members of my family also have similar symptoms. However, since this morning I have had swelling on my face, especially around my eyes. A fortnight ago I was at a barbecue and had diarrhea and severe abdominal pain for several days. There were wild boar sausages that a friend of mine, an Italian, brought from his home country. Otherwise, I am actually healthy. I do not take any medication, I am only allergic to penicillin. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Trichinosis or trichinellosis
 
  Model Output:Trichinellosis (Trichinosis): This is a parasitic infection caused by eating undercooked or raw meat (especially pork or wild game) contaminated with Trichinella larvae. Symptoms include fever, muscle pain, facial swelling, and gastrointestinal symptoms (e.g., diarrhea, abdominal pain). The timeline and exposure to wild boar sausages make this a strong possibility.
 
 Allergic Reaction or Angioedema: Facial swelling, especially around the eyes, can be a sign of an allergic reaction or angioedema. However, this would not typically explain the fever, muscle pain, or gastrointestinal symptoms.
 
 Viral Infection (e.g., Influenza or COVID-19): While viral infections can cause fever, muscle pain, and gastrointestinal symptoms, they are less likely to cause isolated facial swelling.
 
 Bacterial Infection (e.g., Streptococcal Infection or Leptospirosis): Certain bacterial infections can cause fever, muscle pain, and facial swelling. Leptospirosis, for example, is transmitted through contact with contaminated water or animals, but it is less likely given your history of consuming wild boar.
 
 Toxoplasmosis: This is a parasitic infection that can cause flu-like symptoms, muscle pain, and swollen lymph nodes. However, it is less likely to cause significant facial swelling or be linked to the consumption of wild boar.

disease_GT: Hantavirus hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: I am a 66 year old man and have severe feelings of illness with weakness, abdominal pain, loss of appetite, chills and aching limbs. I went to the doctor 4 days ago because I had a high fever overnight (38.8), chills, severe headache and aching limbs. Since it is fall, this was considered the flu. I was told to stay on bed rest, take ibuprofen and drink enough. I did all of that, however, the symptoms got much worse from yesterday to today. In addition, I have severe pain in my stomach, I am no longer hungry and my eyes are sensitive to light and constantly watering. Even if I think back, nothing special has happened. I winterized the garden 2 weeks ago, with everything that goes with it: cleaned the pond from dead plant remains and fetched firewood for the fireplace from the shed. On top of everything, today I have a dizzy feeling when I try to get up. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Hantavirus hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome
 
  Model Output:Leptospirosis: This is a bacterial infection caused by Leptospira bacteria, often transmitted through contact with water, soil, or vegetation contaminated by animal urine. Symptoms include high fever, chills, headache, muscle pain, abdominal pain, and light sensitivity. Your activities (cleaning the pond, handling firewood) could have exposed you to contaminated environments.
 
 Influenza with Complications: While initially considered flu-like, your worsening symptoms (e.g., severe abdominal pain, light sensitivity, and dizziness) suggest a possible complication, such as secondary bacterial infection (e.g., pneumonia or sepsis).
 
 Tick-Borne Illness (e.g., Lyme Disease or Anaplasmosis): If you were exposed to ticks while working outdoors, a tick-borne illness could cause fever, chills, headache, muscle pain, and light sensitivity. However, these conditions typically involve a rash (in Lyme disease) or specific blood abnormalities.
 
 Viral Hepatitis (e.g., Hepatitis A or E): Viral hepatitis can cause fever, abdominal pain, loss of appetite, and general malaise. However, jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes) is usually present, which you haven’t mentioned.
 
 Meningitis or Encephalitis: Inflammation of the brain or its surrounding membranes can cause fever, headache, light sensitivity, and worsening neurological symptoms (e.g., dizziness). Bacterial or viral meningitis is a serious condition requiring urgent treatment.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 70-year-old man and have been suffering from nausea and vomiting for a week. My wife noticed yellowing of the skin in the previous two days. In addition, I have lost about 7 kg in the previous month. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cholangicarcinoma
 
  Model Output:Pancreatic Cancer: This is a serious condition that can cause jaundice (due to bile duct obstruction), weight loss, nausea, and vomiting. It often presents with vague symptoms initially, making early diagnosis challenging.
 
 Hepatitis (Viral or Alcoholic): Inflammation of the liver, whether due to viral infection (e.g., hepatitis B or C) or excessive alcohol consumption, can cause jaundice, nausea, vomiting, and weight loss.
 
 Gallbladder or Biliary Tract Disease (e.g., Cholecystitis or Cholangitis): Conditions affecting the gallbladder or bile ducts, such as gallstones or infections, can lead to jaundice, nausea, and vomiting. Weight loss may occur if the condition is chronic.
 
 Liver Cirrhosis: Chronic liver damage from conditions like hepatitis, alcohol use, or fatty liver disease can cause jaundice, nausea, vomiting, and weight loss. Cirrhosis often progresses silently until symptoms become severe.
 
 Gastric or Duodenal Ulcer with Complications: While ulcers typically cause abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting, complications like obstruction or perforation can lead to weight loss and systemic symptoms. However, jaundice is less common unless there is associated liver or biliary involvement.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 53 year old woman and have had problems for some time that I can no longer hold my bowel movements completely. In addition, when I have a bowel movement itself, there is always blood and something bulges, which I then have to push back again. I have had 3 vaginal births with two stitched perineal tears. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Rectal procidentia or rectal prolapse
 
  Model Output:Hemorrhoids (Prolapsed)
 
 Hemorrhoids are swollen blood vessels in the rectum or anus that can cause bleeding during bowel movements and a bulging mass that may need to be pushed back in. They are common after childbirth and can contribute to fecal incontinence.
 
 Rectal Prolapse
 
 This occurs when the rectum protrudes through the anus, often causing a visible bulge that may need to be manually reduced. It can also lead to fecal incontinence and bleeding.
 
 Anal Fissure
 
 An anal fissure is a small tear in the lining of the anus, which can cause pain and bleeding during bowel movements. While it doesn’t typically cause a bulging mass, it can contribute to discomfort and incontinence.
 
 Pelvic Floor Dysfunction
 
 Damage to the pelvic floor muscles during childbirth (especially with perineal tears) can lead to fecal incontinence and difficulty controlling bowel movements. This can also contribute to rectal prolapse or hemorrhoids.
 
 Fecal Incontinence Due to Sphincter Damage
 
 Vaginal deliveries, particularly those involving perineal tears, can damage the anal sphincter muscles, leading to fecal incontinence. This may be accompanied by other symptoms like bleeding or bulging if hemorrhoids or prolapse are also present.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 30-year-old auto mechanic and have been experiencing increasing pain in my left shoulder. At first, it only occurred when I was working overhead. In the meantime, however, I have constant problems. In addition, I have numbness in my left forearm and hand. The left hand is also pale and feels cold. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome (TOS)
 
  Model Output:Thoracic Outlet Syndrome (TOS): This condition occurs when nerves or blood vessels in the thoracic outlet (the space between your collarbone and first rib) are compressed. Symptoms include shoulder pain, numbness or tingling in the arm and hand, and coldness or pallor due to reduced blood flow.
 
 Cervical Radiculopathy: Compression or irritation of a nerve root in the cervical spine (neck) can cause pain radiating to the shoulder, arm, and hand, along with numbness and weakness. This is often due to a herniated disc or degenerative changes in the spine.
 
 Rotator Cuff Injury: A tear or inflammation in the rotator cuff tendons can cause shoulder pain, especially with overhead movements. However, this typically does not explain the numbness, coldness, or pallor in the hand.
 
 Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD): While less common in younger individuals, PAD can cause reduced blood flow to the extremities, leading to coldness, pallor, and pain. However, this would not typically explain the shoulder pain or numbness.
 
 Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS): This is a chronic pain condition that can develop after an injury or surgery, causing pain, swelling, and changes in skin color and temperature. However, CRPS usually involves more diffuse symptoms and is less likely to explain the specific shoulder and arm symptoms.

Diagnosis Task: I (40-year-old female) have increasing pain and tingling in the thumb, index finger and middle finger of my right hand. These occur mainly at night. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Carpal tunnel syndrome
 
  Model Output:Carpal Tunnel Syndrome (CTS)
 
 CTS is the most common cause of these symptoms. It occurs when the median nerve, which runs through the carpal tunnel in your wrist, becomes compressed. Symptoms often worsen at night and can include pain, tingling, and numbness in the thumb, index, and middle fingers.
 
 Cervical Radiculopathy
 
 Compression or irritation of a nerve root in the cervical spine (neck) can cause similar symptoms, including pain and tingling in the fingers. This is more likely if you have neck pain or a history of neck injury.
 
 Thoracic Outlet Syndrome (TOS)
 
 TOS occurs when nerves or blood vessels in the space between your collarbone and first rib (thoracic outlet) are compressed. This can cause pain, tingling, and numbness in the fingers, often exacerbated by certain arm positions.
 
 Peripheral Neuropathy
 
 Damage to peripheral nerves, often due to conditions like diabetes, vitamin deficiencies, or repetitive strain, can cause tingling and pain in the fingers. However, this typically affects both hands and other parts of the body as well.
 
 De Quervain's Tenosynovitis
 
 This condition involves inflammation of the tendons on the thumb side of the wrist. While it primarily causes pain and swelling near the thumb, it can sometimes lead to referred pain or tingling in the fingers.

Diagnosis Task: My 14-year-old daughter has been complaining of headaches, nausea and vomiting for several days. In addition, she has increased salivation and can not really drink anything despite thirst. She has no previous illnesses but was bitten by a cat last week. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Rabies
 
  Model Output:Rabies
 
 Rabies is a viral infection transmitted through the saliva of infected animals, such as cats. Symptoms include headache, nausea, vomiting, excessive salivation, difficulty swallowing (hydrophobia), and neurological changes. This is a medical emergency and requires immediate attention.
 
 Cat Scratch Disease (Bartonella henselae Infection)
 
 This bacterial infection can occur after a cat bite or scratch. Symptoms include fever, headache, nausea, and fatigue. While it typically causes swollen lymph nodes, systemic symptoms like those your daughter is experiencing can occur in severe cases.
 
 Tetanus
 
 Although rare, tetanus can develop from a cat bite if the wound is contaminated with Clostridium tetani bacteria. Symptoms include muscle stiffness, difficulty swallowing, and increased salivation, along with headache and nausea.
 
 Sepsis or Systemic Infection
 
 A cat bite can introduce bacteria into the bloodstream, leading to a systemic infection. Symptoms include fever, headache, nausea, vomiting, and difficulty drinking due to general malaise or throat inflammation.
 
 Meningitis or Encephalitis
 
 Inflammation of the brain or its lining (meninges) can cause headaches, nausea, vomiting, and difficulty swallowing. This could be due to an infection (viral, bacterial, or fungal) or other causes.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 51-year-old man and have been suffering from pain and itching at the anus for some time. Maybe they are hemorrhoids, because sometimes there is also blood on the toilet paper. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Anal cancer
 
  Model Output:Hemorrhoids: Swollen blood vessels in the rectum or anus can cause pain, itching, and bleeding, especially during bowel movements. Hemorrhoids are a common cause of these symptoms.
 
 Anal Fissure: A small tear in the lining of the anus can cause pain, itching, and bleeding. The pain is often sharp and may occur during or after bowel movements.
 
 Pruritus Ani: This condition involves chronic itching of the anal area, which can be caused by various factors, including hygiene issues, skin conditions, or dietary irritants. It may or may not be associated with bleeding.
 
 Anal Abscess or Fistula: An abscess is a collection of pus caused by an infection, while a fistula is an abnormal connection between the anal canal and the skin. Both can cause pain, swelling, and sometimes bleeding or discharge.
 
 Colorectal Conditions (e.g., Polyps or Colitis): While less common, conditions like colorectal polyps or inflammatory bowel disease (e.g., ulcerative colitis) can cause rectal bleeding, pain, and discomfort. These conditions often have additional symptoms, such as changes in bowel habits.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 41-year-old man and have stomach pain. My family doctor has already put me on proton pump inhibitor therapy for 3 months due to two diagnosed duodenal ulcers. This seemed to work, but shortly after stopping the medication, the stomach pain recurred. I now also suffer from diarrhea. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrinoma)
 
  Model Output:Recurrent Duodenal Ulcers: The recurrence of stomach pain after stopping proton pump inhibitor (PPI) therapy suggests that the duodenal ulcers may not have fully healed or that there is an underlying cause (e.g., Helicobacter pylori infection or excessive use of NSAIDs) contributing to their recurrence.
 Helicobacter pylori Infection: This bacterial infection is a common cause of duodenal ulcers and can lead to recurrent symptoms if not adequately treated. It may also cause diarrhea in some cases.
 Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD): While GERD typically causes heartburn and acid reflux, it can sometimes present with stomach pain and may worsen after stopping PPI therapy.
 Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS): The onset of diarrhea alongside stomach pain could indicate IBS, especially if there is no evidence of ongoing ulceration or infection. IBS can coexist with other gastrointestinal conditions.
 Bile Acid Malabsorption: This condition can cause diarrhea and abdominal pain, particularly after meals, and may develop secondary to other gastrointestinal issues or surgeries.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 17 year old girl and I am having increasingly severe pain in my lower right abdomen as well as nausea and vomiting. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute appendicitis
 
  Model Output:Appendicitis: Inflammation of the appendix, often causing sharp pain in the lower right abdomen, nausea, vomiting, and fever. This is a medical emergency.
 
 Ovarian Cyst Rupture or Torsion: A ruptured ovarian cyst or ovarian torsion (twisting of the ovary) can cause sudden, severe pain in the lower abdomen, often on one side, along with nausea and vomiting.
 
 Kidney Stones: Pain from kidney stones can radiate to the lower abdomen and may be accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and blood in the urine.
 
 Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID): An infection of the reproductive organs, often caused by sexually transmitted infections, which can lead to lower abdominal pain, nausea, and fever.
 
 Gastroenteritis: Inflammation of the stomach and intestines, often due to a viral or bacterial infection, which can cause abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 32 year old male and I have severe pain during stool. This pain is accompanied by itching and secretion of mucus. In addition, blood is found more often on the toilet paper. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute anal fissure 
 
  Model Output:Hemorrhoids: Swollen blood vessels in the rectum or anus, which can cause pain, itching, bleeding, and mucus discharge. They are often aggravated by straining during bowel movements.
 
 Anal Fissure: A small tear in the lining of the anus, which can cause sharp pain during bowel movements, bleeding, and itching.
 
 Anal Abscess or Fistula: An abscess is a collection of pus caused by infection, while a fistula is an abnormal tunnel between the anal canal and the skin. Both can cause pain, swelling, discharge, and sometimes bleeding.
 
 Proctitis: Inflammation of the lining of the rectum, which can cause pain, mucus discharge, bleeding, and a feeling of incomplete bowel movements. It can be caused by infections, inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), or other conditions.
 
 Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD): Conditions like Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis can cause inflammation in the digestive tract, leading to symptoms such as rectal pain, mucus discharge, bleeding, and changes in bowel habits.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 52 year old woman and have dysphagia that occurs mainly with liquid foods. In addition, I have pain behind the breastbone after eating. I have had this pain for several years, especially at night it has become unbearable as I often wake up and have to cough. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Achalasia
 
  Model Output:Achalasia: This is a motility disorder of the esophagus where the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) fails to relax properly, leading to difficulty swallowing (dysphagia), particularly with liquids. It can also cause regurgitation and chest pain.
 Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD): Chronic acid reflux can lead to dysphagia, retrosternal pain, and nighttime symptoms like coughing due to aspiration of stomach contents. GERD can also cause complications like esophagitis or strictures.
 Esophageal Stricture: Long-standing GERD or other causes of inflammation can lead to narrowing of the esophagus, resulting in dysphagia, particularly with solids initially but sometimes also with liquids.
 Hiatal Hernia: A hiatal hernia can exacerbate GERD symptoms, leading to retrosternal pain, dysphagia, and nighttime coughing due to acid reflux.
 Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE): This is an allergic/immune condition that causes inflammation of the esophagus, leading to dysphagia (often with solids but sometimes liquids) and chest pain. It can be triggered by food allergens.

disease_GT: Proximal biceps tendon rupture, more specifically of the long head biceps tendon (LHBT rupture)
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: I am a 45-year-old male. I was lifting a sink onto a truck at work yesterday and suddenly felt a sharp pain in my upper left arm. After that, I could not continue working. Now the pain is still there for more than 12 hours. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Proximal biceps tendon rupture, more specifically of the long head biceps tendon (LHBT rupture)
 
  Model Output:Muscle Strain or Tear: Lifting a heavy object can cause overstretching or tearing of the muscles in the upper arm (e.g., biceps or triceps), leading to sharp pain and limited mobility.
 Rotator Cuff Injury: Although typically associated with shoulder pain, a rotator cuff injury can refer pain to the upper arm, especially after heavy lifting or repetitive overhead activities.
 Tendon Rupture (e.g., Biceps Tendon): A sudden, sharp pain during heavy lifting could indicate a partial or complete rupture of a tendon, such as the biceps tendon, which can cause significant pain and weakness in the arm.
 Brachial Plexus Injury: The brachial plexus is a network of nerves that supply the arm. Heavy lifting or sudden strain can cause nerve irritation or injury, leading to sharp pain and possible weakness or numbness.
 Shoulder Impingement or Labral Tear: Heavy lifting can cause impingement of structures in the shoulder or a tear in the labrum (the cartilage that stabilizes the shoulder joint), which can refer pain to the upper arm.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 60-year-old man and have increasing swelling in the right groin, and since a few days also of the right testicle. At first, the swelling was completely gone when I was lying down and only worsened when I was standing and coughing. In the meantime, however, the swelling is always present. Otherwise, however, I'm fine. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Inguinal hernia
 
  Model Output:Inguinal Hernia: A common cause of groin swelling, where part of the intestine or abdominal tissue protrudes through a weak spot in the abdominal wall. Initially, the swelling may reduce when lying down, but it can become irreducible (incarcerated) or even strangulated, which is a medical emergency.
 
 Hydrocele: A fluid-filled sac around the testicle, which can cause swelling in the scrotum. It is usually painless but can become large and uncomfortable.
 
 Varicocele: Enlarged veins within the scrotum, often described as a "bag of worms." It can cause swelling and discomfort, particularly when standing.
 
 Epididymitis or Orchitis: Inflammation of the epididymis (epididymitis) or testicle (orchitis), often caused by infection. This can lead to swelling, pain, and tenderness in the scrotum and groin.
 
 Testicular Tumor: A less common but serious cause of testicular swelling. Tumors may present as a painless or painful mass in the testicle, sometimes accompanied by a feeling of heaviness.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 32 year old woman and today I had my attitude examination. It revealed high blood pressure with values of 180/100 and blood sugar 220. In addition, I have been suffering from fatigue and muscle weakness for a year. In addition, I have gained 10 kg since a few months. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cushing's Syndrome
 
  Model Output:Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus: Elevated blood sugar (hyperglycemia) and symptoms like fatigue and weight gain are classic signs of type 2 diabetes. High blood pressure often coexists with diabetes as part of metabolic syndrome.
 Metabolic Syndrome: This condition is characterized by a cluster of symptoms, including high blood pressure, elevated blood sugar, weight gain (particularly central obesity), and insulin resistance. It increases the risk of cardiovascular disease and diabetes.
 Cushing’s Syndrome: This condition results from prolonged exposure to high levels of cortisol. Symptoms include weight gain (especially in the face and abdomen), fatigue, muscle weakness, high blood pressure, and elevated blood sugar.
 Hypothyroidism: An underactive thyroid can cause fatigue, muscle weakness, weight gain, and elevated blood pressure. It can also contribute to metabolic disturbances, including elevated blood sugar.
 Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS): PCOS is a hormonal disorder that can cause weight gain, insulin resistance, elevated blood sugar, and high blood pressure. Fatigue and muscle weakness may also occur due to metabolic and hormonal imbalances.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 42 year old male and have been having headaches for about 6 months. In addition, I suffer from sweating and heart palpitations again and again. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Pheochromocytoma or catecholamine-secreting paraganglioma
 
  Model Output:Hypertension (High Blood Pressure): Elevated blood pressure can cause headaches, sweating, and palpitations. It is often asymptomatic but can lead to these symptoms if severe or uncontrolled.
 
 Hyperthyroidism: Overactivity of the thyroid gland can lead to symptoms such as headaches, excessive sweating, heart palpitations, weight loss, and anxiety.
 
 Migraine or Tension-Type Headaches: Chronic headaches can be due to migraines or tension-type headaches, which may sometimes be accompanied by autonomic symptoms like sweating or palpitations, especially during severe episodes.
 
 Anxiety or Panic Disorders: Psychological conditions like anxiety or panic attacks can cause headaches, sweating, and heart palpitations. These symptoms often occur during periods of stress or anxiety.
 
 Pheochromocytoma: A rare tumor of the adrenal gland that causes excessive release of adrenaline and noradrenaline, leading to episodic headaches, sweating, and palpitations. This condition is often accompanied by high blood pressure.

Diagnosis Task: I (67-year-old man) have increasing pain in my left hip joint when walking and the resulting limited mobility. Especially in the morning, I have a lot of difficulty walking, sometimes I limp because of it. I have not had an accident. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Hip osteoarthritis
 
  Model Output:Osteoarthritis (OA): This is the most common cause of hip pain in older adults. It involves the breakdown of cartilage in the hip joint, leading to pain, stiffness (especially in the morning), and difficulty walking. Symptoms typically worsen with activity.
 Trochanteric Bursitis: Inflammation of the bursa (a fluid-filled sac) near the hip joint can cause pain on the outer side of the hip, which may radiate down the thigh. Pain is often worse with walking or lying on the affected side.
 Hip Labral Tear: The labrum is a ring of cartilage that surrounds the hip socket. A tear in the labrum can cause pain, stiffness, and a sensation of the hip "catching" or "locking," particularly during movement.
 Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA): RA is an autoimmune condition that can affect the hip joint, causing pain, morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes, and swelling. Unlike osteoarthritis, RA often affects multiple joints symmetrically.
 Piriformis Syndrome: The piriformis muscle, located near the hip joint, can irritate the sciatic nerve if it becomes tight or inflamed. This can cause pain in the hip area, which may radiate down the leg and worsen with walking or sitting.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 22 year old woman and have been having severe labor-like pain in my lower right abdomen for the past 3 hours. My last period was only a week ago, but I have a slightly elevated temperature of 37.7 degrees Celcius. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute pelvic inflammatory disease (PID)
 
  Model Output:Appendicitis: This is a common cause of severe right lower abdominal pain. It often starts near the belly button and migrates to the lower right side. Fever, nausea, and vomiting may also be present. Appendicitis requires urgent medical attention.
 Ovarian Torsion: This occurs when an ovary twists around its supporting structures, cutting off blood supply. It causes sudden, severe pain on one side of the lower abdomen and may be associated with nausea and fever. It is a medical emergency.
 Ruptured Ovarian Cyst: A cyst on the ovary can rupture, causing sudden, sharp pain on one side of the lower abdomen. Fever and mild elevation in temperature may occur if there is associated inflammation or infection.
 Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID): This is an infection of the female reproductive organs, often caused by sexually transmitted infections. It can cause lower abdominal pain, fever, and abnormal vaginal discharge. Pain may be one-sided or generalized.
 Ectopic Pregnancy: If there is any chance of pregnancy, an ectopic pregnancy (where the fertilized egg implants outside the uterus, often in the fallopian tube) must be considered. It causes severe, one-sided abdominal pain and may be associated with vaginal bleeding. This is a life-threatening emergency.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 28 year old woman and have been suffering from extremely unpleasant smelling vaginal discharge for about a week. In addition, there is also a burning and itching of the vagina. My boyfriend also has discomfort when urinating. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Bacterial vaginosis, (vaginal) trichomoniasis
 
  Model Output:Bacterial Vaginosis (BV): This is a common cause of foul-smelling (often described as "fishy") vaginal discharge, along with itching and burning. It results from an imbalance in the vaginal microbiome and is not typically sexually transmitted, though sexual activity can increase the risk.
 Trichomoniasis: This is a sexually transmitted infection (STI) caused by the parasite Trichomonas vaginalis. It can cause foul-smelling, greenish or yellowish discharge, itching, burning, and discomfort during urination. Partners often experience similar symptoms.
 Candidiasis (Yeast Infection): While yeast infections typically cause thick, white, odorless discharge and intense itching, they can sometimes be associated with a mild odor. However, the discharge is usually not as foul-smelling as in BV or trichomoniasis.
 Chlamydia or Gonorrhea: These are sexually transmitted bacterial infections that can cause abnormal vaginal discharge, burning, and itching. They may also cause discomfort during urination in both partners. If left untreated, they can lead to more serious complications.
 Mixed Infection: It’s possible to have more than one type of infection simultaneously, such as BV along with an STI like trichomoniasis or chlamydia, which could explain the combination of symptoms.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 22 year old woman and I am overweight. My last period was about 4 months ago. However, my cycle has always been quite irregular. I also have increasing hair growth on my upper lip and cheeks. I had a laparoscopic appendectomy 2 years earlier, during which a biopsy was taken from the ovary. The findings at that time said: thickened tunica albuginea, hyperplasia of theca interna, thickened cortical stroma, multiple subcapsular follicles of different stages of maturation. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)
 
  Model Output:Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS): This is the most likely diagnosis given your irregular periods, hirsutism, and ovarian findings (thickened tunica albuginea, hyperplasia of theca interna, and multiple subcapsular follicles). PCOS is also associated with insulin resistance and weight gain.
 Idiopathic Hirsutism: This condition involves excessive hair growth in women without a clear underlying cause. However, it does not typically explain the irregular periods or ovarian changes seen in your biopsy.
 Non-Classic Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (NCAH): This is a milder form of congenital adrenal hyperplasia that can cause hirsutism, irregular periods, and ovarian changes. It is caused by a deficiency in enzymes involved in cortisol production.
 Hyperthecosis: This is a condition characterized by excessive androgen production due to the presence of luteinized theca cells in the ovaries. It can cause hirsutism, irregular periods, and ovarian changes similar to PCOS.
 Cushing’s Syndrome: While less likely, this condition involves excessive cortisol production and can cause weight gain, hirsutism, and menstrual irregularities. However, it typically presents with additional symptoms like a rounded face, purple stretch marks, and high blood pressure.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 49 year old woman and have hot flashes several times a day. When this happens, you can see a reddening of my skin, which usually starts in the head and neck area and then spreads across my chest and back to my arms. My heart beats faster and I have beads of sweat on my forehead. I can no longer concentrate at work. I have no previous illnesses and my periods are regular, although they are now 35 days long. It used to come every 4 weeks to the day. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Perimenopause
 
  Model Output:Perimenopause: The transitional phase before menopause, during which hormonal fluctuations (especially estrogen) can cause hot flashes, night sweats, irregular periods, and difficulty concentrating. Your slightly lengthened menstrual cycle (35 days) is consistent with this stage.
 
 Hyperthyroidism: An overactive thyroid gland can cause symptoms like hot flashes, sweating, rapid heartbeat, and difficulty concentrating. Thyroid function tests can help rule this out.
 
 Anxiety or Stress: Psychological factors can sometimes mimic physical symptoms, including hot flashes, sweating, and palpitations. However, your symptoms seem more consistent with a hormonal cause.
 
 Carcinoid Syndrome: A rare condition caused by a neuroendocrine tumor that releases serotonin and other chemicals, leading to flushing, rapid heartbeat, and sweating. This is less likely but worth considering if symptoms persist or worsen.
 
 Medication Side Effects: Certain medications, such as antidepressants or hormonal treatments, can cause hot flashes and sweating. If you’re taking any medications, review their side effects with your doctor.

Diagnosis Task: My wife has acute stabbing pain in her right lower abdomen. She has pain almost all the time, e.g. during sexual intercourse, during bowel movements, even two days before she gets her period. When we met, it was not like that. And we don't get pregnant either, even though she stopped taking the pill 2 years ago. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Endometriosis
 
  Model Output:Endometriosis: This is a condition where tissue similar to the lining of the uterus grows outside the uterus, often on the ovaries, fallopian tubes, or pelvic lining. It can cause chronic pelvic pain, pain during intercourse, bowel movements, and menstruation, as well as infertility.
 Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID): This is an infection of the female reproductive organs, often caused by sexually transmitted infections. It can lead to chronic pelvic pain, pain during intercourse, and infertility due to scarring of the fallopian tubes.
 Ovarian Cysts: Cysts on the ovary, such as endometriomas or functional cysts, can cause chronic pelvic pain, especially if they rupture or twist (ovarian torsion). Pain may worsen during certain activities or phases of the menstrual cycle.
 Adhesions: Scar tissue from previous surgeries (e.g., appendectomy, laparoscopy) or infections can cause chronic pelvic pain, pain during intercourse, and bowel movements. Adhesions may also contribute to infertility.
 Adenomyosis: This condition involves the growth of endometrial tissue into the muscular wall of the uterus, causing heavy, painful periods and chronic pelvic pain. It can also lead to pain during intercourse and bowel movements.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 25-year-old woman and I have unbearable itching and burning in the vagina and vaginal entrance. In addition, there is increased discharge. This is pretty much the first time. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Vulvovaginal candidiasis
 
  Model Output:Yeast Infection (Candidiasis): A fungal infection caused by Candida overgrowth, leading to intense itching, burning, and a thick, white, cottage cheese-like discharge. It is very common and often occurs after antibiotic use, hormonal changes, or weakened immunity.
 
 Bacterial Vaginosis (BV): An imbalance of the normal vaginal flora, leading to an overgrowth of harmful bacteria. Symptoms include itching, burning, and a thin, grayish-white discharge with a fishy odor.
 
 Trichomoniasis: A sexually transmitted infection (STI) caused by the parasite Trichomonas vaginalis. It can cause itching, burning, and a frothy, yellow-green discharge with a strong odor.
 
 Contact Dermatitis or Irritation: An allergic reaction or irritation from products like soaps, detergents, condoms, or feminine hygiene products. This can cause itching, burning, and redness without significant discharge.
 
 Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs): Other STIs, such as chlamydia or gonorrhea, can cause vaginal itching, burning, and increased discharge. These infections often have additional symptoms like pelvic pain or pain during urination.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 55-year-old woman and I feel that my clothes no longer fit me at the stomach. It certainly can't be from eating, since I have hardly any appetite lately. I also have constipation all the time, sometimes the stool is dark-blackish. In addition, there is a non-specific feeling of pressure in the lower abdomen, but no pain. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: (Advanced stage) Ovarian cancer
 
  Model Output:Colorectal Cancer: This can cause abdominal distension, changes in bowel habits (such as constipation), and dark or black stools due to bleeding in the gastrointestinal tract. It is more common in older adults and requires urgent evaluation.
 Ovarian Cancer: Ovarian cancer often presents with nonspecific symptoms such as abdominal distension, feeling of pressure or fullness, loss of appetite, and changes in bowel habits. It is sometimes called the "silent killer" because symptoms are often vague and overlooked.
 Diverticulitis or Diverticular Disease: Diverticula (small pouches in the colon) can become inflamed or infected, leading to abdominal distension, constipation, and sometimes bleeding, which may cause dark stools.
 Gastrointestinal Bleeding (Upper or Lower): Dark or black stools (melena) suggest bleeding in the upper gastrointestinal tract (e.g., stomach or duodenum), while bright red blood may indicate lower GI bleeding. Causes include peptic ulcers, gastritis, or tumors.
 Chronic Constipation with Fecal Impaction: Long-standing constipation can lead to fecal impaction, causing abdominal distension and discomfort. Dark stools may occur if there is minor bleeding from straining or irritation of the intestinal lining.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 20 year old female and have been experiencing acute and crampy lower abdominal pain for about 4 hours. I have slightly elevated temperature with 37.8 degrees Celsius. My last period was 5 days ago. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID)
 
  Model Output:Ovarian Cyst Rupture or Torsion: A ruptured ovarian cyst or ovarian torsion (twisting of the ovary) can cause sudden, severe lower abdominal pain. This is often accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and sometimes a low-grade fever.
 
 Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID): An infection of the reproductive organs, often caused by sexually transmitted infections (STIs) like chlamydia or gonorrhea. Symptoms include lower abdominal pain, fever, and sometimes abnormal vaginal discharge.
 
 Appendicitis: Inflammation of the appendix can cause acute lower abdominal pain, often starting near the belly button and moving to the lower right side. Fever, nausea, and vomiting may also occur.
 
 Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) or Pyelonephritis: A UTI can cause lower abdominal pain and fever. If the infection spreads to the kidneys (pyelonephritis), symptoms may include flank pain, high fever, and chills.
 
 Mittelschmerz (Ovulation Pain): Some women experience pain during ovulation, which occurs about midway through the menstrual cycle. This pain is usually mild and short-lived but can sometimes be more severe.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 46-year-old woman and I have heavy vaginal bleeding. Actually, I have always had my period regularly every 28 days. However, in the last 7 weeks I have not had any bleeding. As a precaution, I took a pregnancy test, but it was negative. This morning I started bleeding, which is much heavier than the normal period. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Endometrial hyperplasia, follicular persistence
 
  Model Output:Dysfunctional Uterine Bleeding (DUB): This is abnormal uterine bleeding without an obvious structural or systemic cause. It is often related to hormonal imbalances, particularly during perimenopause, which can cause irregular or heavy bleeding.
 Uterine Fibroids: These are noncancerous growths in the uterus that can cause heavy menstrual bleeding, irregular periods, and sometimes a sensation of pelvic pressure or pain.
 Endometrial Hyperplasia: This is a thickening of the uterine lining, often due to excess estrogen without enough progesterone. It can cause irregular or heavy bleeding and is more common in women approaching menopause.
 Miscarriage or Incomplete Miscarriage: Even though your pregnancy test was negative, a very early miscarriage or incomplete miscarriage (where some tissue remains in the uterus) can cause heavy bleeding after a missed period. A follow-up pregnancy test or ultrasound may be needed to rule this out.
 Endometrial Cancer: While less common, endometrial cancer can cause abnormal uterine bleeding, particularly in women over 40. Heavy bleeding after a missed period, especially in the context of risk factors like obesity or a history of irregular cycles, should be evaluated.

Diagnosis Task: I am 35 years old and gave birth to my first child 9 months ago. I have been losing increasingly large amounts of urine for a few weeks. In the beginning, I only lost a drop when I coughed or laughed. Now I lose urine every day, even when climbing stairs or going for a walk. This occurs quite suddenly and is not accompanied by a feeling of urge to urinate. Somehow I also have the feeling that something is bulging out of my vagina, something like a hemisphere. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Female stress urinary incontinence
 
  Model Output:Stress Urinary Incontinence (SUI): This is the most common type of incontinence after childbirth. It occurs when the pelvic floor muscles are weakened, leading to urine leakage during activities that increase abdominal pressure (e.g., coughing, laughing, or exercising).
 
 Pelvic Organ Prolapse (e.g., Cystocele or Uterine Prolapse): The sensation of something bulging in the vagina may indicate a prolapse of the bladder (cystocele), uterus, or other pelvic organs. This occurs when the pelvic floor muscles and ligaments are weakened, allowing organs to descend into the vaginal canal.
 
 Overactive Bladder (OAB): While OAB typically involves a sudden urge to urinate, it can sometimes present with leakage without urgency, especially if there is coexisting pelvic floor weakness.
 
 Urinary Tract Infection (UTI): Although less likely given your description, a UTI can sometimes cause incontinence or leakage. However, it is usually accompanied by other symptoms like burning during urination or frequent urges to urinate.
 
 Vaginal or Pelvic Floor Trauma from Childbirth: Tears or damage to the pelvic floor muscles, nerves, or connective tissues during childbirth can lead to both incontinence and a sensation of bulging in the vagina.

disease_GT: Coronary artery disease, stable angina pectoris
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: I am a 49-year-old gardener and I have a feeling of pressure behind my sternum that has been occurring at work for a few days. It then lasts for several minutes and after a short break from work (due to pain) it also disappears again. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Coronary artery disease, stable angina pectoris
 
  Model Output:Angina Pectoris: This is chest pain or pressure caused by reduced blood flow to the heart muscle, often due to coronary artery disease. It typically occurs during physical exertion or stress and resolves with rest. Angina is a warning sign of potential heart problems and requires immediate medical evaluation.
 
 Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD): Acid reflux can cause a burning or pressure sensation behind the sternum, often triggered by physical activity, bending over, or lying down. Symptoms may improve with rest or antacids.
 
 Esophageal Spasm: Abnormal contractions of the esophagus can cause chest pressure or pain that may mimic angina. It can be triggered by physical activity or stress.
 
 Costochondritis: Inflammation of the cartilage connecting the ribs to the sternum can cause localized chest pain or pressure, which may worsen with movement or physical activity. However, it is usually tender to touch, which doesn’t seem to fit your description.
 
 Hiatal Hernia: A condition where part of the stomach pushes up into the chest through the diaphragm. It can cause pressure or pain behind the sternum, especially during physical activity or after eating.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 34 year old woman and for the last 4 days I have had redness of the skin on my lower left leg - just above the ankle. The redness is ring-shaped and has been expanding for the last few days. I just came from a mountain vacation, but otherwise have no problems. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Lyme disease
 
  Model Output:Lyme Disease: This is caused by the bacterium Borrelia burgdorferi, transmitted through tick bites. A classic early sign is a bull's-eye rash (erythema migrans), which is ring-shaped and expands over time. It is commonly associated with outdoor activities in wooded or grassy areas.
 Ringworm (Tinea Corporis): Despite its name, ringworm is a fungal infection that causes a red, ring-shaped rash with a clearer center. It can spread and is often itchy. It is unrelated to worms but can be contracted from soil, animals, or contaminated surfaces.
 Cellulitis: This is a bacterial skin infection that can cause redness, warmth, and swelling. While it typically does not have a ring-shaped appearance, it can spread rapidly and may be associated with breaks in the skin (e.g., cuts or insect bites).
 Erythema Multiforme: This is a hypersensitivity reaction that can cause ring-shaped or target-like rashes. It may be triggered by infections, medications, or other factors. It is usually not itchy or painful.
 Contact Dermatitis: Exposure to plants (e.g., poison ivy) or other irritants during your mountain vacation could cause a red, itchy, and sometimes ring-shaped rash. However, this is less likely if the rash is expanding without itching or blistering.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 32 year old woman and have had worsening muscle weakness for 3 months. In particular, getting up from sitting and lying down, climbing stairs and lifting my arms is becoming increasingly difficult. There is no significant muscle pain, but there are recurring pains and swellings of the finger and wrist joints. In addition, I notice increased dandruff on the hands and slightly red swollen eyelids. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Dermatomyositis
 
  Model Output:Dermatomyositis: This is an inflammatory muscle disease that causes progressive muscle weakness, particularly in the proximal muscles (e.g., difficulty standing up, climbing stairs, or lifting arms). It is often associated with skin changes, such as a rash on the eyelids (heliotrope rash), swollen red eyelids, and scaly patches on the hands (Gottron's papules).
 Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE): Lupus is an autoimmune disease that can cause muscle weakness, joint pain and swelling, and skin rashes. It may also involve other organs, such as the kidneys or lungs. The swollen red eyelids and skin changes could be consistent with lupus-related skin involvement.
 Polymyositis: This is another inflammatory muscle disease that causes muscle weakness, particularly in the proximal muscles. Unlike dermatomyositis, it does not typically involve skin changes, but it can coexist with other autoimmune conditions that cause joint pain and swelling.
 Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA): RA is an autoimmune condition that primarily affects the joints, causing pain, swelling, and stiffness, particularly in the small joints of the hands and wrists. While muscle weakness is not a primary feature, chronic inflammation and joint damage can lead to secondary muscle weakness.
 Mixed Connective Tissue Disease (MCTD): This is an overlap syndrome that combines features of lupus, scleroderma, and polymyositis. It can cause muscle weakness, joint pain and swelling, and skin changes, including swollen eyelids and rashes.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 67-year-old man and have pain in my left calf. They occur from a distance of 150m and disappear when I stop. This has been happening for 6 months. At rest I do not notice anything. I have known arterial hypertension and diabetes mellitus type II. I smoke about a pack a day for almost 50 years. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Peripheral artery disease
 
  Model Output:Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD): This is the most likely diagnosis. PAD is caused by atherosclerosis (narrowing or blockage of arteries) in the legs, leading to reduced blood flow. Symptoms include cramping, pain, or fatigue in the legs during activity (claudication), which improves with rest. Your risk factors (smoking, diabetes, hypertension) strongly predispose you to PAD.
 
 Diabetic Neuropathy: While diabetic neuropathy typically causes burning, tingling, or numbness in the feet and legs, it can sometimes present with pain. However, it is less likely to cause activity-induced pain that resolves with rest.
 
 Chronic Venous Insufficiency (CVI): This condition occurs when veins in the legs fail to return blood efficiently to the heart, causing pain, swelling, and sometimes cramping. However, the pain from CVI is usually worse after prolonged standing and improves with elevation, which doesn’t fully match your symptoms.
 
 Spinal Stenosis: Narrowing of the spinal canal can cause nerve compression, leading to pain in the legs that worsens with walking (pseudoclaudication). However, this pain is often relieved by sitting or leaning forward, not just by stopping.
 
 Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT): A blood clot in the deep veins of the leg can cause calf pain, swelling, and redness. However, DVT typically causes constant pain, not just pain with walking, and is often accompanied by swelling and warmth in the affected leg.

disease_GT: Diabetes insipidus (both types possible: central ~ = Arginine vasopressin deficiency, nephrogenic ~ = Arginine vasopressin resistence)
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: I am a 34-year-old woman and have been experiencing increasing thirst for months. I have to drink large amounts of fluid all the time. That's why I often have to urinate a lot during the day and at night. In addition, I sleep worse and become increasingly nervous. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Diabetes insipidus (both types possible: central ~ = Arginine vasopressin deficiency, nephrogenic ~ = Arginine vasopressin resistence)
 
  Model Output:Diabetes Mellitus (Type 1 or Type 2): Increased thirst (polydipsia) and frequent urination (polyuria) are hallmark symptoms of diabetes, caused by high blood sugar levels. Other symptoms may include fatigue, weight loss, and blurred vision. Your symptoms strongly suggest the need to check your blood glucose levels.
 
 Diabetes Insipidus: A condition where the kidneys cannot properly regulate water balance, leading to excessive urination and thirst. Unlike diabetes mellitus, this is not related to blood sugar levels but rather to a problem with antidiuretic hormone (ADH).
 
 Hypercalcemia: Elevated calcium levels in the blood, often due to conditions like hyperparathyroidism or certain cancers, can cause increased thirst, frequent urination, fatigue, and nervousness.
 
 Psychogenic Polydipsia: A psychological condition where excessive fluid intake leads to frequent urination. This is often seen in individuals with psychiatric disorders but can also occur due to stress or anxiety.
 
 Hyperthyroidism: An overactive thyroid gland can cause symptoms such as nervousness, sleep disturbances, increased thirst, and frequent urination due to an accelerated metabolism.

Diagnosis Task: I am 24 years old, male and have had diarrhea for 4 days: up to 10 bowel movements per day. The stool is watery, discolored and unformed. I do not take any medication nor do I have any other pre-existing conditions. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute diarrhea
 
  Model Output:Acute Gastroenteritis: This is the most common cause of acute diarrhea and is often caused by viral (e.g., norovirus, rotavirus), bacterial (e.g., E. coli, Salmonella, Campylobacter), or parasitic (e.g., Giardia) infections. It is often accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and abdominal cramps.
 
 Food Poisoning: Consuming contaminated food or water can lead to acute diarrhea, often within hours to days after ingestion. Symptoms may include nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain.
 
 Traveler’s Diarrhea: If you have recently traveled, especially to regions with poor sanitation, you may have contracted a bacterial, viral, or parasitic infection causing diarrhea.
 
 Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD): Conditions like Crohn’s disease or ulcerative colitis can cause acute diarrhea, though they are typically associated with chronic symptoms. However, an initial flare-up could present similarly.
 
 Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea (if recently exposed to antibiotics): If you have taken antibiotics recently, even if not currently, it could lead to an overgrowth of Clostridioides difficile (C. diff), causing severe diarrhea. However, you mentioned not taking any medications.

Diagnosis Task: My mother is 79 years old, who has had chills, diarrhea, cough and shortness of breath for several hours. She has had a fever for several days, currently around 39.4 degrees Celsius. She regularly takes the tablets Diclofenac, Ramipril and Methotrexate. Because of the fever, she has also been taking daily 3-4 tablets of an antibiotic (cotrimoxazole) from my father's stock for the last few days. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Drug-induced agranulocytosis
 
  Model Output:Pneumonia: Fever, chills, cough, and shortness of breath are classic symptoms of pneumonia, which can be bacterial, viral, or fungal. Given her age and symptoms, this is a leading possibility.
 
 COVID-19 or Influenza: Viral infections like COVID-19 or influenza can cause fever, chills, cough, diarrhea, and shortness of breath. These infections are particularly dangerous in older adults.
 
 Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) or Pyelonephritis: UTIs can cause fever, chills, and sometimes diarrhea, especially in older adults. If the infection spreads to the kidneys (pyelonephritis), symptoms may worsen.
 
 Adverse Drug Reaction or Overuse of Antibiotics: Taking antibiotics like cotrimoxazole without a prescription can lead to side effects (e.g., diarrhea, fever) or antibiotic resistance. Additionally, methotrexate (an immunosuppressant) increases the risk of infections.
 
 Sepsis: A life-threatening condition caused by the body’s response to an infection. Symptoms include fever, chills, rapid breathing, and confusion. Given her age and medications, she is at higher risk.

disease_GT: Nodular Goiter with normal hormone production
Disease_description:
Diagnosis Task: I am a woman of 33 years. My thyroid gland has slowly increased in size over the past few years. I am more and more prone to nervousness and have gained 4 kg of weight in the past 2 years. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Nodular Goiter with normal hormone production
 
  Model Output:Hypothyroidism: An underactive thyroid gland can cause weight gain, fatigue, and sometimes a goiter. Nervousness is less common but can occur due to associated stress or anxiety. Hypothyroidism is often caused by Hashimoto's thyroiditis, an autoimmune condition.
 
 Hashimoto's Thyroiditis: An autoimmune disorder where the immune system attacks the thyroid gland, leading to gradual thyroid enlargement (goiter) and eventual hypothyroidism. Symptoms may include weight gain, fatigue, and nervousness.
 
 Simple or Nontoxic Goiter: Enlargement of the thyroid gland without over- or underactivity. It can be caused by iodine deficiency (rare in areas with iodized salt) or other factors. Symptoms are often mild, but nervousness and weight changes can occur.
 
 Hyperthyroidism: While hyperthyroidism typically causes weight loss, some individuals may experience weight gain due to increased appetite. Nervousness, anxiety, and a goiter are common symptoms. Conditions like Graves' disease or toxic nodular goiter can cause hyperthyroidism.
 
 Thyroid Nodules or Multinodular Goiter: Benign or cancerous growths in the thyroid gland can cause enlargement and sometimes hormonal imbalances. Nervousness and weight changes may occur depending on thyroid function.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 23-year-old woman suffering from acute shortness of breath. Since the beginning of April, I have had attacks of shortness of breath, usually accompanied by cough and glassy, viscous sputum. At the same time, I have no fever, night sweats, or weight loss. Apart from hay fever, I have no previous illnesses. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Bronchial asthma
 
  Model Output:Asthma: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory condition of the airways that can cause episodes of shortness of breath, coughing, and thick, glassy sputum. It is often triggered by allergens (e.g., pollen, dust), exercise, or cold air. Your history of hay fever increases the likelihood of allergic asthma.
 
 Allergic Bronchitis: Inflammation of the bronchi due to an allergic reaction, often triggered by environmental allergens. Symptoms include coughing, shortness of breath, and production of thick mucus.
 
 Bronchitis (Acute or Chronic): Inflammation of the bronchial tubes, which can cause coughing, shortness of breath, and sputum production. Acute bronchitis is often viral, while chronic bronchitis is associated with long-term irritation (e.g., smoking or allergies).
 
 Postnasal Drip Syndrome: Mucus from the nasal passages dripping down the throat can cause coughing, shortness of breath, and the sensation of thick, glassy sputum. This is often associated with hay fever or other allergic rhinitis.
 
 Exercise-Induced Bronchoconstriction (EIB): Narrowing of the airways during or after physical activity, leading to shortness of breath and coughing. This can occur in individuals with or without asthma.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 34 year old woman and have been fatigued and tired for months. I have also collapsed several times in the past few weeks. Otherwise I have never been ill and I do not take medication regularly. However, since a short time I have brownish hyperpigmentation, which rather does not come from the sun. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Chronic primary adrenal insufficiency
 
  Model Output:Addison’s Disease (Primary Adrenal Insufficiency): This condition occurs when the adrenal glands do not produce enough cortisol and aldosterone. Symptoms include fatigue, weakness, episodes of collapse (due to low blood pressure), and hyperpigmentation (caused by elevated ACTH levels). The brownish discoloration is often most noticeable in sun-exposed areas, skin folds, and mucous membranes.
 
 Hypothyroidism: An underactive thyroid gland can cause fatigue, weakness, and sometimes skin changes. However, hyperpigmentation is less common in hypothyroidism unless it is associated with other conditions (e.g., adrenal insufficiency).
 
 Iron Deficiency Anemia: Chronic fatigue and weakness can result from low iron levels, which may also cause pallor rather than hyperpigmentation. However, anemia alone does not explain the hyperpigmentation or episodes of collapse.
 
 Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (CFS): A condition characterized by persistent, unexplained fatigue that is not relieved by rest. However, CFS does not typically cause hyperpigmentation or episodes of collapse.
 
 Hemochromatosis: A genetic disorder causing excessive iron accumulation in the body, leading to fatigue, joint pain, and hyperpigmentation (bronze skin). However, this condition is less common and usually presents with additional symptoms like liver dysfunction or diabetes.

Diagnosis Task: My father is 78 years old and has been experiencing pain in his left lower abdomen for several days. He also complains of alternating constipation and diarrhea. He also has a fever of 38.7 degrees Celsius. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute colonic diverticulitis
 
  Model Output:Diverticulitis: This is a common cause of left lower abdominal pain in older adults. It occurs when small pouches (diverticula) in the colon become inflamed or infected. Symptoms often include fever, localized pain, and changes in bowel habits (constipation or diarrhea).
 Colorectal Cancer: While less common, colorectal cancer can cause abdominal pain, changes in bowel habits (alternating constipation and diarrhea), and systemic symptoms like fever, especially if there is obstruction or infection.
 Infectious Colitis: An infection of the colon (bacterial, viral, or parasitic) can cause abdominal pain, fever, and diarrhea. In some cases, constipation may alternate with diarrhea due to inflammation.
 Ischemic Colitis: Reduced blood flow to the colon, often due to atherosclerosis, can cause abdominal pain, fever, and changes in bowel habits. This is more common in older adults with vascular risk factors.
 Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD): Conditions like Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis can cause abdominal pain, fever, and alternating constipation and diarrhea. While IBD typically presents at a younger age, it can occasionally manifest or be diagnosed in older adults.

Diagnosis Task: I am a 36-year-old male and I am having increasing difficulty swallowing. I've had this for a while, both when swallowing solid foods and when drinking. Recently, I have sometimes had to regurgitate food. Nevertheless, I have not lost any weight and I have no other pain. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Achalasia
 
  Model Output:Achalasia: A motility disorder of the esophagus where the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) fails to relax, and the esophagus loses its ability to move food downward. Symptoms include dysphagia for both solids and liquids, regurgitation of undigested food, and sometimes chest pain.
 
 Esophageal Stricture: Narrowing of the esophagus, often due to chronic acid reflux (GERD), scarring, or inflammation. This can cause difficulty swallowing, particularly with solids, and may lead to regurgitation.
 
 Esophageal Spasm: Abnormal contractions of the esophagus can cause dysphagia and regurgitation. This condition is often intermittent and may be triggered by stress or certain foods.
 
 Hiatal Hernia: A condition where part of the stomach pushes up into the chest through the diaphragm. While often asymptomatic, it can cause dysphagia and regurgitation, especially if it leads to GERD or esophageal obstruction.
 
 Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE): An allergic/immune condition causing inflammation of the esophagus, leading to dysphagia and food impaction. It is often associated with a history of allergies or asthma.

Diagnosis Task: I (17 years old girl) have been suffering from high fever, sweating and joint pain for several days. It all started 10 days ago with a sore throat. In addition, my knee joints, ankles and wrists are swollen and red. I have a fever of 39.5 degrees Celsius and strange spots and nodules on my forearm. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute rheumatic fever
 
  Model Output:Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF): A complication of untreated streptococcal pharyngitis (strep throat). Symptoms include fever, migratory joint pain and swelling (especially in large joints like knees and ankles), skin nodules, and sometimes a rash (erythema marginatum). The sore throat 10 days ago is a key clue.
 
 Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA): A type of autoimmune arthritis that can cause fever, joint swelling, and pain. Systemic JIA (Still’s disease) often presents with high fever, rash, and joint symptoms.
 
 Lyme Disease: Caused by the bacterium Borrelia burgdorferi and transmitted by tick bites. Symptoms include fever, joint pain, and swelling, often in large joints, and sometimes a characteristic rash (erythema migrans). However, the sore throat is less typical.
 
 Viral Arthritis: Certain viral infections (e.g., parvovirus B19, rubella, or hepatitis) can cause fever, joint pain, and swelling. These symptoms are usually self-limiting and resolve as the infection clears.
 
 Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE): An autoimmune disease that can cause fever, joint pain, swelling, and skin rashes. However, SLE is less common in your age group and typically presents with additional symptoms like fatigue and organ involvement.

Diagnosis Task: I am a woman of 24 years. I have been having cramping pain in my lower right abdomen for weeks and recurrent non-bloody diarrhea, as well as a decrease in my body weight by 6 kg. I have already had my appendix and tonsils removed. I have no fever, but I have a fistula at my anus and swelling on my right knee joint. What are most likely diagnoses? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Crohn disease
